Gennifer Weathermay-Foxgrove.text:
Gennifer learned a variety of medicinal and spiritual practices, both traditional and esoteric, from Rudolph van Richten. She has conducted in-depth studies of lycanthropy to help her and her sister pursue Natalia Vhorishkova, who remains at large. Secretly, Gennifer fears that she might have been infected with lycanthropy after their encounter with Natalia. She hasn’t shown signs of the curse yet, but she takes medication of her own concoction to ward off unwanted transformation. Gennifer quietly researches lycanthropy, searching for medicinal cures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwGenniferWeath statblock caption=false classes="hide-in-embed"]

Laurie Weathermay-Foxgrove.name: Laurie Weathermay-Foxgrove
Laurie Weathermay-Foxgrove.text:
Laurie learned the ways of martial combat from her uncle George, training in techniques to combat the restless dead. She rarely travels without her canine companions, Joan and Tirran, the grown pups of her uncle’s foxhounds. She also wields her uncle’s magic sword, Gossamer, which, according to family legend, contains the soul of a heroic Weathermay ancestor. Laurie suspects that Gennifer is keeping something from her, but she won’t pry into her sister’s secrets.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwLaurieWeather statblock caption=false classes="hide-in-embed"]

Wereraven.name: Wereraven
Wereraven.text:
Ominous Shape-Shifter

Habitat: Forest, Urban; Treasure: Individual

Wereravens are secretive, cautious lycanthropes that seek to blend into society and subtly combat evil. To avoid the claws of the wicked forces that oppose them, and to protect those they hold dear, wereravens hide their identities and rarely reveal their hybrid forms. Nevertheless, folktales persist of eerie, raven-winged figures who appear prior to disasters, share mysterious prophecies, and aid hapless victims. Such stories are true in a way, as evil hunts wereravens wherever they go.

Because evil frequently travels in packs, wereravens flock together to battle such threats. They congregate in hard-to-reach aeries within or just outside urban settlements, such as atop bell towers, lighthouses, or ancient trees. Wereraven groups tend to be loosely organized, since members understand the importance of anonymity.

The @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwEnemiesandAll.JournalEntryPage.Organizations5af#keepers-of-the-feather]{Keepers of the Feather} (described in chapter 3) is one example of a secret society of evil-opposing wereravens.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwWereraven0000 statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.Z5pg2KGYEdFXHDjK inline]

Yithian.name: Yithian
Yithian.text:
Fungous Alien Body Swapper

Habitat: Desert, Underdark, Urban; Treasure: Arcana

Yithians are cosmos-traveling scholars with distinctive, conical bodies and four limbs that end in pincers, sensory structures, and flute-like communication organs. They search for secrets to add to their vast libraries, conducting research by swapping minds with the inhabitants of other worlds—sometimes indefinitely. When a yithian swaps minds with a creature, it traps its victim’s mind in the yithian’s body, which it teleports to its unfathomable, alien home world.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwYithian000000 statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.bBxLmJHGg1oyJOw6 inline]

Zombies.name: Zombi
Zombies.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.wu3Hb4qrG0x78NI7 inline]

Relentless Reanimated Corpses

Habitat: Planar (Shadowfell), Underdark, Urban; Treasure: None

The zombies of Ravenloft come in many monstrous forms. These Undead horrors befoul and beleaguer any living thing in their way.

Swarm of Zombie Limbs.name: Swarm of Zombie Limbs
Swarm of Zombie Limbs.text:
Dismemberment sometimes isn’t enough to disable a zombie’s undying rage. Swarms of zombie limbs reanimate from the slough of zombie flesh molters and surge on hapless monster hunters who mistakenly believed their work is done.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwZombieLimbsSw statblock caption=false classes="hide-in-embed"]

Zombie Plague Spreader.name: Zombie Plague Spreader
Zombie Plague Spreader.text:
Necromancers who wish to sow as much toxic mayhem as possible often create zombie plague spreaders to do their dirty work for them.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwPlagueSpreade statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Zombie Clot.name: Zombie Clot
Zombie Clot.text:
When a throng of zombies becomes inextricably tangled or multiple swarms of zombie limbs coalesce, the result is a zombie clot—a putrid mass of rotten flesh with a singular will.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwZombieClot000 statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Bestiary.name: Bestiary
Bestiary.text:

Ivana Boritsi.name: Ivana Boritsi
Ivana Boritsi.text:
Darklord of Poisonous Ambition

Ivana Boritsi tires of playing games no one realizes she’s already won. The head of Borca’s most influential family, Ivana effortlessly manipulates all aspects of society. Her servants, employees, and fractious cousins prove endlessly unreliable, forcing her to attend personally to tedious duties and social functions to maintain her power.

Ivana looks eternally youthful, causing some to doubt her competence and attribute her success to her elder cousin, Ivan Dilisnya. Perpetually bored with and disappointed by predictable people, she takes refuge in her alchemical studies and her gardens of poisonous plants. Her vast knowledge of toxins makes her a peerless poisoner. She uses her creations to make people more pliable and to assassinate those she deems too boorish to live. Secretly, Ivana seeks someone who can match her intellect and relieve her boredom, but she quickly grows weary of even the most charming and clever souls.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwIvanaBoritsi0 statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Ivana’s Tale

The firstborn of a fantastically wealthy family, Ivana Boritsi was clever, poised, and—in the eyes of her father, Klaus—utterly unfit to lead the family. Driven to prove her father wrong and encouraged by her mother, Camille, Ivana learned every aspect of her family’s vast perfume and medicine business.

Her focus on her goals faltered when she met Pieter, a skilled chemist in her family’s service. Camille recognized her daughter’s budding romance and saw the lowborn Pieter as a threat to the girl’s prospects. Camille seduced Pieter and arranged for her daughter to discover them, intent on convincing her to forsake this and all future romances.

Ivana took a different lesson from the experience. Realizing the depths of her family’s depravity, she poisoned her mother and her callous younger siblings with toxins of her own design. None suspected Ivana, but soon after, Klaus summoned her. Having lost his sons, Klaus named her cousin, Ivan Dilisnya, his heir. Ivana was the last to be informed, her father having already announced his decision to the estate and sent a messenger to inform the Dilisnyas.

Having expected this, Ivana had poisoned the perfumery on the estate’s grounds, turning its far-ranging smells deadly. Ivana laughed as toxic mist poured across the estate. As she sipped the antidote, she watched Klaus and the manor’s staff die choking amid a violet fog. When the poison cleared, the Mists had surrounded the land of Borca (for details on Ivana’s domain and lair, see “Ivan Dilisnya”).

Ivan Dilisnya—a spoiled, selfish attention seeker—has the tastes of a child but a withered wreck of a body. Borcan society widely regards this recluse as an innovator and a genius. In truth, he is neither. Ivan is a wicked and off-putting manipulator who, despite his appearance and intellect, affects childlike behaviors and speech patterns for attention. He is alone—despite having been born with everything—and the solitude terrifies him.

By staying out of sight or using deceptive correspondence, Ivan makes his targets doubt reality and overestimate his control. He manipulates people into performing pointless errands for him or spying on his cousin, Ivana Boritsi, whom he believes mocks him and has caused his aged, squalid state.

Ivan surrounds himself with dangerous clockwork toys and fawning, mechanical servants. While he goes through the motions of crafting such devices, the Dark Powers provide him with any creation he desires, no matter how bizarre.

Ivana’s Lair

The vast Boritsi estate, Misericordia, hosts fields, greenhouses, and alchemy labs used to create Boritsi-branded perfumes and tinctures. Within the manor, the Darklord cultivates rare and unique plants in greenhouse-like conservatory wings only she can enter.

Misericordia Conservatories

The conservatories of Misericordia, the Boritsi estate, hold strange plants, many of which Ivana cultivated. One such site is depicted on @UUID[Scene.6ka3ghFV5CtMIwD7]{map 2.4} and has the following features:

Gardens. Plants of exceptional beauty and toxicity flourish under Ivana’s care in Misericordia’s gardens and greenhouses. Many require unusual fertilizers to flourish—particularly the carnivorous plants.

Laboratory. In her lab, the Darklord brews deadly alchemical concoctions and wondrous perfumes. A sizable perfume organ here is filled with rare scents and lethal poisons.

Nursery. Hidden behind a rotating wall is Ivana’s private nursery and study. Here she cultivates new plant species, many of which have supernatural properties or monstrous life.

Adventures in Borca.name: Adventures in Borca
Adventures in Borca.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.BULrVgZNTwbPIUlN inline]

Adventures set in Borca involve intrigue between petty nobles, family secrets, manipulation, and deception. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

Horror in Borca

Adventures in Borca often include the following elements.

Nobles of Borca

Twelve depraved families rule over every aspect of life in Borca. Each is a core family of fifteen or fewer irredeemable individuals united around the head of the house, who tyrannically controls the clan’s myriad ventures. Servants and business representatives across the domain support these core families, as do members of branch families—lackeys with different surnames who are distantly related to the core family or indentured to them.

The Noble Families of Borca table details the domain’s most powerful families.

Noble Families of Borca

Name

Estate Name

Concern

Nobriskov

Ocrotire

Olzanik

Kinisaradia

Piechota

Alieselti

Ranching

Pretorius

Mundorhova

Gambling

Ritter

Vetistiqua

Fashion

Tatenna

Fulchighora

Banking

Supernatural Intrigue

Magic and the supernatural heighten the gilded depravities of Borca. When planning adventures in Borca, consider including the following elements.

Curses and Poison. Chapter 3 of the Dungeon Master’s Guide describes curses, magical contagions, and poison. Villains in Borca might use these or adjusted versions of your own design—perhaps with signature cosmetic effects.

Monsters. Monsters can serve as threats but also agents of noble houses. Shape-shifting creatures such as @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmImp00000000000]{Imps} and @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWererat0000000]{Wererat}} excel at infiltration, while @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmDoppelganger00]{Doppelganger}} and @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmSuccubus000000]{Succubi} might pose as nobles, innocents, or trusted allies. Creatures like @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGhost000000000]{Ghost}} and @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmIntellectDevou]{Intellect Devourer}} might control individuals, making them behave in unusual ways. Choose a single monster, and consider how you might make it central to a devious noble’s plot.

Spells and Magic Items. Don’t try to account for or incorporate all magic into adventures involving supernatural intrigue. Choose a single spell—such as @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.cg50KpBkBdPK6vPL]{Clairvoyance}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.JQyigMNPiDnGI18b]{Geas}, or @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.I2LUSF5ogc7Bj62e]{Speak with Dead}—and consider how to make it central to a plot involving secrets and mysterious revelations. Consider doing the same with a magic item such as a @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.items.Item.Aq1rhgcgFnwu2T4I]{Cloak of the Bat}, a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-dungeon-masters-guide.equipment.Item.dmgHagEye0000000]{Hag Eye}, or @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.equipment24.Item.dmgSendingStones]{Sending Stones}. Characters should earn rewards for using their own magic in clever ways.

Borca Campaign Arc

Campaigns set in Borca typically involve the domain’s noble families. A campaign here might follow this outline:

Levels 1–3. A scheming noble targets the characters, presenting them with an opportunity or exorbitant compensation for a simple task. In the aftermath, the characters discover they have given up something of great value or realize they look like criminals.

Levels 4–6. The scheming noble or a rival offers the characters a way out of their predicament, but this involves a dangerous confrontation or daring infiltration. Supernatural forces—such as jilted doppelgangers, cursed family members, or dead nobility—might aid the characters. Against expectations, the characters complete their mission and turn the tables on their foe.

Levels 7–8. The characters’ activities attract the attention of Ivana Boritsi or Ivan Dilisnya. The Darklord invites the characters to their estate or corresponds with them from afar, using them as agents in their rivalry. For example, either Darklord might task the characters with seeking out the missing will of Ivana’s father, Klaus.

Ivan Dilisnya.name: Ivan Dilisnya
Ivan Dilisnya.text:
Darklord of Childish Cruelty

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.f3omibsR81Ktc310 cite=false]

Ivan Dilisnya—a spoiled, selfish attention seeker—has the tastes of a child but a withered wreck of a body. Borcan society widely regards this recluse as an innovator and a genius. In truth, he is neither. Ivan is a wicked and off-putting manipulator who, despite his appearance and intellect, affects childlike behaviors and speech patterns for attention. He is alone—despite having been born with everything—and the solitude terrifies him.

By staying out of sight or using deceptive correspondence, Ivan makes his targets doubt reality and overestimate his control. He manipulates people into performing pointless errands for him or spying on his cousin, Ivana Boritsi, whom he believes mocks him and has caused his aged, squalid state.

Ivan surrounds himself with dangerous clock work toys and fawning, mechanical servants. While he goes through the motions of crafting such devices, the Dark Powers provide him with any creation he desires, no matter how bizarre.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwIvanDilisnya0 statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Ivan’s Tale

Treachery is tradition for the Dilisnya family. The land of Borca originates from the same world as Barovia’s conquerors, the von Zaroviches. Dilisnyas were at Castle Ravenloft the night of Strahd von Zarovich’s transformation.

Ages later, the siblings Boris and Camille Dilisnya plotted to steal the prestige of their cousins, the Boritsis. Camille manipulated her way into the Boritsi family, while Boris prepared his son, Ivan, to act as head of a united Boritsi–Dilisnya house.

Despite abundant opportunities, Ivan resented his family’s plans for him and grew to embody puerile vice and impulses. His family’s wealth and doting servants exacerbated this problem, even as Ivan fixated on childish toys and behaviors. Frequent “accidents” transpired around him, forcing his family to cover up the maimings and deaths of numerous pets and servants. Only his beloved sister, Kristina, remained immune to his wicked tricks. Although Ivan’s family grew unsettled by Ivan, they sheltered the future head of their house, reshaping their estate and investing in whimsical diversions for him.

On the same night Ivana Boritsi poisoned her family, Ivan learned his parents intended to send Kristina to a prestigious boarding school. Screams, cruel cackling, and clockwork screeching filled the estate that night. When it ended, Ivan Dilisnya was the last living member of his family, and the Mists had closed around Borca.

Ivan’s Lair

The Dilisnya estate, Degravo, is a confusing tangle of topiary gardens, hedge mazes, and neglected petting zoos that abut sheer cliffs. At the heart of the grounds sits a largely abandoned compound that includes the Laughing House, Ivan’s home.

The Laughing House

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.UNNwoTMSbpEBNjvM cite=false classes="float-right"]

The only occupied structure in Degravo, the Laughing House has been renovated and redecorated to suit a child’s whimsical tastes. The site is depicted on @UUID[Scene.6ka3ghFV5CtMIwD7]{map 2.4} and has the following features:

Ivan’s Playroom. On the second floor of the manor, a gallery of childish art leads to doors minded by a clockwork attendant that Ivan can taunt intruders through. At the hall’s end is a fake bedroom decorated for a neglected youngster. A hidden door there leads to Ivan’s true personal chambers. This playroom is a wonderland of ingenious toys, all of which are secretly deadly. Beyond the Darklord’s personal chamber lie secret rooms where he creates new playthings and discards toys he’s grown tired of.

Playhouse. Beyond a lobby attended by clockwork staff, a theater hosts brutal stage productions for Ivan’s amusement. Performers might be life-size toys or desperate prisoners.

Lethal Lavender.name: Lethal Lavender
Lethal Lavender.text:
@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.adventures.Adventure.eVcAaQNwvgS4aaVt]{Adventure for Level 5 Characters}

Situation. The human siblings @UUID[Actor.6LaA6BocXcCtQ7kC]{Eroso} and @UUID[Actor.fSzef2IqVqISvmaF]{Siobhan Manduchi} (Medium, Neutral Evil @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmNoble000000000]{Nobles}) are scions of a minor noble family who stole a rare plant, not realizing it belonged to the Boritsis. After discovering the owner, they feign their deaths to avoid reprisal.

Hook. Eroso and Siobhan seek out the characters to use as patsies in their staged murder.

Encounters

The adventure consists of these encounters.

@UUID[Actor.pgsmCyMOUgGUnt1G]{Dig Your Vibe.} Eroso and Siobhan Manduchi approach the characters and invite them to go carousing if the adventurers agree to share tales of their exploits. The two are Friendly and flattering.

@UUID[Actor.ogXXkAfgiDO93XDP]{Exquisite Corpse.} Soon after the characters leave the siblings, they hear a scream. If they investigate, they find Eroso’s stabbed corpse surrounded by guards. Siobhan accuses the characters of killing her brother. A group of four @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGuard000000000]{Guards} and one @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGuardCaptain00]{Guard Captain} (in the Manduchis’ employ) moves to kill the characters, while Siobhan vanishes into the night.

Wanted. More guards hunt for the characters but are easy to avoid. Before the characters flee the community, @UUID[Actor.doI3yXVzmCrHaXx2]{Nostalia Romaine} (Medium, Lawful Evil @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmAssassin000000]{Assassin}), who appears to be human, discovers them. She explains that she works for the Boritsi family, shares the details in “Situation” above, and insists the characters help her find the Manduchis.

@UUID[Actor.y1mKzgWlmfA6zYre]{Dead Walking.} Any character who spends a day searching for the Manduchis and succeeds on a [[/check insight persuasion 14 format=long]] learns Eroso’s or Siobhan’s whereabouts. If they investigate, they find a public site—like a theater or bar—where the noble is behaving recklessly. These aren’t the actual Manduchis, but @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwWaxwork000000]{Waxworks} (see chapter 5) striving to destroy themselves in public. The characters encounter at least two of these creatures, perhaps more at your discretion.

Flawed Perfection. After the characters encounter at least two self-destructive Manduchi waxworks, they find a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwWaxwork000000]{Waxwork} convinced that it is the new, improved Eroso Manduchi, and it doesn’t want to die. If the characters promise to let it go, the Construct leads them to Lethal Lavender, the secret club where the Manduchis are hiding.

@UUID[Actor.G9hCvHeoGj63m3gV]{Lethal Lavender.} The interior of the Lethal Lavender club is overgrown with bruise-colored fungus. A pair of fungus-covered @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwPlagueSpreade]{Zombie Plague Spreaders} (see chapter 5) haunts the bar. In the VIP lounge, the plant the Manduchis stole has bloomed into a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmVioletFungusNe]{Violet Fungus Necrohulk} composed of the real Manduchis and their remaining wax duplicates.

Ivana’s Favor. After the monster’s defeat, Nostalia finds the characters. She rewards them with a vial of @UUID[Item.NT6wnEM4or7kDeRK]{Boritsi perfume} worth 2,000 GP and a @UUID[Item.KUyN9bRCuGeWtcaa]{letter} from Ivana Boritsi inviting them to a private social event at Misericordia in a week’s time. Characters also earn a bonus [[/award 1100xp each]].

Borca.name: Borca
Borca.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.CWcr7ya2k7FMV4GD]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.Dbbz8lAa201f0ab3]

Domain of Desire and Deceit

Darklords: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwIvanaBoritsi0]{Ivana Boritsi} and @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwIvanDilisnya0]{Ivan Dilisnya}

Genres: Gothic horror and psychological horror

Hallmarks: Political intrigue, poison, revenge

Mist Talismans: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwDramofSweetSm]{Dram of sweet-smelling poison}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwFlameSingedLo]{flame-singed love letter}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwTarnishedSign]{tarnished signet ring}

Aligned Tarokka Card: Beggar

Opposed Tarokka Card: Tempter

The noble families of Borca engage in an endless war of conspiracies and lethal betrayals as they pursue influence, thrills, and age-old rivalries. People here value guile and callousness, and none embody those traits better than the domain’s two Darklords: the genius poisoner Ivana Boritsi and the childishly cruel stalker Ivan Dilisnya.

Beyond the fashionable holdings of Borca’s elite, commoners struggle against crime, poverty, and starvation. In scattered villages and tenement-filled cities, locals view nobles as celebrities, and the commoners’ idealized vision of noble life leads them to mimic the aristocrats’ callousness and appetite for empty fads. Desperate commoners are tempted into debt and depravity in pursuit of nobles’ favor and passing fancies. Those who don’t bend to the whims of Borca’s elite face humiliation before they’re crushed.

Domain Features

Borca is known for the following features:

Two Darklords. Ivana Boritsi and Ivan Dilisnya are both Darklords of Borca. They control the domain equally—one of countless facts that contributes to their mutual loathing.

Cruel Intentions. Borca’s most influential families are vicious rivals bound by blood, politics, and business. Their rivalries unfold in an endless shadow war of lies, shifting alliances, and assassinations. The nobles value appearances above all else; they favor stylish poisons, clever clockwork devices, and public embarrassment in their plots.

Desperate Sycophants. Commoners own little, renting homes in crowded cities or rural villages that serve noble interests. Many commoners desperately court the favor of the elite.

Noble Intrigue. Nobles control all aspects of Borcan society. They squabble over artistic, political, and religious influence to feed their gluttonous egos and fill their idle lives. The Boritsi family holds the greatest influence.

Locations in Borca

The impoverished lands of Borca languish in the shadow of opulent noble estates and garish monuments to greed. The exploited land bears the scars of age-old rivalries and the hiding places of noble secrets.

The capital of Levkarest is described below, and other notable locations are summarized on the Borca Locations table.

Levkarest

Borca’s capital of Levkarest stands at the pinnacle of culture and ostentatious power. Here, lesser nobles pursue fickle dramas and vogue trends while the populace labors amid workhouses and cultlike fashion houses. Commoners glimpse this world of toxic glamor only from afar or when nobles exploit street-level fads.

At the city’s heart rises the People’s Parliament, the land’s ineffective seat of government. Here, unpopular members of noble families squabble over meaningless legislation. The nation’s true rulers conduct business on noble estates, in avant-garde clubs, and in the offices of faceless mercantile ventures.

Near the People’s Parliament stands the Great Cathedral. Within, pious commoners and ostentatious aristocrats attend ceremonies sacred to the deity Ezra. Locals say the cathedral was created by nobles who believed they’d earned the ire of some great evil. Centuries later, these families still support the church, transactionally investing in dubious supernatural insurance.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.weZumGYPBmAzoA0v cite=false]

Borca Locations

Location

Description

Alichino’s Hall

No one knows who built the cursed mansion of Alichino’s Hall, but tales claim that devils gather here nightly to gamble. Allegedly anyone who bests the cheating devils is granted a wish.

House of Lament

The abandoned House of Lament abuts the tower of a ruined castle. Numerous spirits haunt this accursed place, which has been known to appear in the Mists and in other domains.

Lechberg

The residents of Lechburg pretend not to notice that many of their neighbors are clockwork creations of Ivan Dilisnya, fearing their unpredictable lord’s reprisal if they don’t play along.

Leoni

Owned by the Ocrotire family, Leoni has a baseless reputation for harboring bandits. Zealous circuit judges from Sturben often visit and arrest those they deem suspicious.

New Ilvin

After Ilvin burned, the residents rebuilt their homes to the east. The people of New Ilvin never speak of the tragedy that befell them and constructed a new road to avoid Old Ilvin.

Noble estates

The homes of Borca’s most influential families are detailed in “Nobles of Borca.”

Old Ilvin

Only ruins remain of the town of Ilvin. Hidden within sealed cellars lie the charred remains of many who now appear to reside in the town of New Ilvin.

Poison Orchard

Poisonous fungi and plants grow in the Poison Orchard—the deadliest grow at the forest’s heart. Several noble-backed companies use these plants as secret ingredients in their products.

Scholomance

Scions of Borca’s noble families viciously compete to attend the elite Scholomance school of magic. The school is home to the Rainmaker Society, which is said to control Borcan politics.

Sturben

Sturben is home to the Faceless Court, in which groups of five masked judges execute severe justice across social classes. Their iron-masked circuit judges travel widely.

Vor Ziyden

A hound-like horror appears nightly to terrorize the village of Vor Ziyden. Only one local knows the monster seeks the corpse of its brother, who died near a druidic site in the nearby woods.

Ivana and Ivan’s Domain.name: Ivana and Ivan’s Domain
Ivana and Ivan’s Domain.text:
Borca is altered by Ivana’s and Ivan’s presences, creating the following effects:

Closing the Borders. While in their domain, Ivana or Ivan can close or open the borders to Borca at will. While the borders are closed, the Mists surrounding Borca are filled with briar vines (Ivana) or barbed wire (Ivan). The Mists become Difficult Terrain, and whenever a creature moves into or within the Mists, it takes [[/damage 2d4 Piercing]] damage for every 5 feet it travels. Once Ivana or Ivan closes or opens the borders, neither Darklord can do so again until the next dawn.

Ivana’s Whispers (Ivana Only). Ivana can encode a message of 25 words or fewer into flowers in Borca at dawn. Until the next dawn, the first time a creature smells a flower, it hears the message in its mind, understanding the meaning of the message.

Mystery Letters (Ivan Only). Whenever Ivan writes a letter and addresses it to a creature in Borca whose name he knows and that he’s seen before, the letter appears near that creature at the next dawn. The letter appears in an unobserved but obvious space, such as a bedside table or among gear.

Spies. While in Borca, Ivana and Ivan can cast @UUID[Compendium.dnd-players-handbook.spells.Item.phbsplClairvoyan]{Clairvoyance}, requiring no Material components and using Intelligence as the spellcasting ability. To cast the spell, there must be a Large or bigger plant (Ivana) or a Tiny doll or similar toy (Ivan) within range from which the sensor appears.

If a Darklord of Borca dies, that Darklord’s effects end immediately. Those effects resume if that Darklord gains a new body (see Ivana’s and Ivan’s Darklord Restoration traits).

Campaign Characters.name: Campaign Characters
Campaign Characters.text:
Characters exploring the Mists are the protagonists of horror stories. Chapter 1 provides advice for players creating such characters, but you should also consider the following questions:

Character Origins. Do the characters originate from the Domains of Dread, or were they abducted from somewhere else?

Context for Surreality. Do the characters realize something is strange about the Domains of Dread, or is the Land of the Mists all they’ve ever known?

Scale of Experience. Do the characters know about a single domain—perhaps one they all call home—or do they have firsthand experience with multiple domains or other settings?

Characters’ Significance. Why are the characters special? Why did the Dark Powers, a Darklord, or something else take note of them?

Consistency of Experience. Do the answers to these questions apply to all of the characters?

Campaign Themes.name: Campaign Themes
Campaign Themes.text:
Explore the types of horror adventures you’re interested in by considering the following questions:

Horror Inspirations. What horror stories or media inspire your campaign?

Horror Icons. Is there a classic monster or villain that inspires the foes in your campaign?

Horror Genres. What genres of horror—detailed in chapter 4 or elsewhere—does your campaign focus on?

Domains of Dread. Which Domains of Dread or Darklords do you want to focus on? Or do you want to create a new Domain of Dread and Darklord following the methods in chapter 4?

Setting Mysteries.name: Setting Mysteries
Setting Mysteries.text:
The Domains of Dread function in unique ways, but if and how that matters is up to you. “Ravenloft Setting Models” later in this chapter details ways to shape the setting to suit your adventures. Review that section and the following questions as you consider how the nature of the Domains of Dread might shape your adventures:

Escape. Do you want characters to pursue escape from the Domains of Dread? Do they want to leave even if a domain has been their home?

Islands or Regions. Does your campaign focus on a single domain, or are there multiple domains you want to explore?

Metaphysical Scale. Do you want your campaign to explore the unusual nature of the Domains of Dread, or is that nature broadly accepted reality?

Stories and Secrets. Are tales of other domains or settings treated as facts, lore known only to a few, legends, or nonsense? Is that true for all places beyond a domain’s borders?

Traveling the Mists. Do people travel between domains? Is travel limited to wanderers such as Vistani, or are tools such as Mist talismans (see the introduction) common enough to allow for broader travel and trade?

Witnesses to the Surreal. How do domain residents react to supernatural events? Are they shocked, do they treat them as commonplace, or do they rationalize (or deny) abject horrors?

The answers to these questions are the ones that inspire you and your players.

Darklord Conflicts.name: Darklord Conflicts
Darklord Conflicts.text:
Each Domain of Dread is a reflection of and a prison for a Darklord. The tales and torments of those Darklords are fundamental to adventures within their borders. Consider the following questions when developing your campaign:

Darklord Plots. Will your campaign involve the history, torment, and potential defeat of a Darklord? Or would you rather keep a Darklord in the background and focus on other stories?

Darklord Downfalls. Can a Darklord be defeated, or are they an enduring and recurring threat? What happens when a Darklord dies?

Darklord Defiance. What does it mean to best a Darklord? Does it mean slaying them, forcing them to open their domain’s borders, undermining their plans, or something else?

Darklord Relationships. Does the Darklord want to destroy, conscript, or use the characters? Does the Darklord appear in person or communicate via proxies?

Darklord Rivalries. Does your campaign feature interaction between multiple Darklords? If so, how do they know about each other? What do they have to gain from their conflicts?

The “Using Darklords” section later in this chapter further explores ways to implement Darklords in your campaign.

Starting Your Campaign.name: Starting Your Campaign
Starting Your Campaign.text:
Start your campaign in a way that establishes an ominous tone or highlights the nature of the Domains of Dread. Consider the following questions:

Collective Misfortune. Do the characters gather to mourn the death of a loved one? Does fate bring them together as the survivors of a disaster, such as a shipwreck or massacre?

Darklord’s Summons. When a Darklord calls, the beckoned answer. Do the characters meet after being hired by a Darklord to perform some task or by attending a social event that turns ominous?

Present Terror. Do the characters meet amid terrifying circumstances, such as being prisoners or experiment subjects?

Shared Mystery. Do the characters gather due to a shared mystery? Are they led to the same place by an enigmatic message, or do they start cooperating as they flee being accused of murder?

Strangers in the Mists. Are the characters united by the Mists? Might they be traveling to other destinations when the Mists unite them?

During character creation, you can work with players to come up with reasons why the characters might be in a grim or unusual situation.

A horrific origin might also unite the party. Players might be interested in their characters sharing a species or a Dark Gift from chapter 1. A group composed entirely of Reborn or of characters who have the Aberrant Anatomy feat can provide ample inspiration for frightful adventures.

Campaign Considerations.name: Campaign Considerations
Campaign Considerations.text:
Think about how you might reinforce a frightful mood over the course of your campaign. Consider the following questions about topics likely to occur during a series of adventures:

Allies and Sanctuaries. Is it ever appropriate to harm characters’ allies or destroy places they consider safe? Is doing so worth making players feel like they’ve lost something they’ve invested in? See “Tragic Limits” in the Dungeon Master’s Guide for advice on handling these topics.

Altered States. How might characters change during a campaign? Do you want to encourage players to develop character changes resulting from their experiences—such as scars, phobias, or Dark Gifts?

Character Secrets. What sorts of secrets might a character have, and are those boons to the game or potential disruptions? You can use the advice in the Dungeon Master’s Guide on evil characters to incorporate characters with secrets into your game.

Death. What happens when a character dies? How might a character brought back from death be changed?

Fear. How do you expect characters to respond to frightful situations? Is fear reflected only by the Frightened condition, or would you like players to roleplay shock and revulsion?

Hope. How will you give characters hope and ensure they don’t feel like the campaign is pointless? What rays of light perforate the darkness?

New Characters. If a character dies and isn’t raised, how will you add a player’s new character to the game? Do the Mists reveal a new hero, or might someone in a domain be inspired to act by another’s sacrifice?

When thinking about the answers to these questions, avoid changing the rules of the game—these should remain the stable, agreed-on underpinnings of the game.

Ending Your Campaign.name: Ending Your Campaign
Ending Your Campaign.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.m2jGk5TiFtas8yMV caption=false classes="float-right size-three" inline]

Horror stories often don’t end well, but yours might be an exception. Have an idea of what makes a satisfying end for your horror campaign. Frame your thoughts on this by considering these questions:

Escape. Is the goal of your campaign for the characters to escape a domain or all of the Domains of Dread? Can they achieve this through the defeat of a Darklord, the will of the Dark Powers, or some other magical method?

Memorial. If a character dies, can they still contribute to a campaign’s culmination? Can an accidental death become a meaningful sacrifice? Is there important work only a spirit can do?

Survival. Do you expect any or all of the characters to survive the campaign? Will the players be satisfied if their characters’ stories end in defeat, transformation, or willing sacrifice?

Worthy Cause. What’s the goal the characters might risk everything to achieve? Is this protecting a person or place, or is it keeping something out of a Darklord’s hands? Is there a status quo that’s vital to restore or a wrong that needs to be undone?

Ensure you and the players agree on what makes a fulfilling end to a campaign. Don’t work toward a climax demanding a dramatic sacrifice that no one’s interested in making.

Further Development.name: Further Development
Further Development.text: Review your answers to the questions from this section to see what patterns emerge and what answers surprised or excited you. Let those answers develop into ideas for unique horror adventures and arcs for your own campaigns. Next, review the following sections in this chapter. These explore ways to employ elements of the Domains of Dread in your horror adventures and outline whole frightful campaign arcs.

Campaigns in the Mist.name: Campaigns in the Mist
Campaigns in the Mist.text:
The surreal natures of the Domains of Dread, their Darklords, the Mists, and the Dark Powers serve as tools in your frightful adventures. None of them are too important to use, too mysterious to reveal, or too fragile to pit against characters. This section explores how to use the most infamous features of the Domains of Dread to create frightful campaigns.

Before preparing horror adventures, review the “Ensuring Fun for All” section of the Dungeon Master’s Guide.

Ravenloft Adventures.name: Ravenloft Adventures
Ravenloft Adventures.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.CMsUuDwLt2MChd9a cite=false]

Drafting frightful encounters, adventures, and campaigns requires deliberate choices that cultivate an atmosphere of dread. This chapter explores how you, the DM, can use the Domains of Dread to create suspense during play and craft terrifying adventures.

Creating a Horror Campaign.name: Creating a Horror Campaign
Creating a Horror Campaign.text: When planning campaigns in Ravenloft, consider the guidance in chapter 5 of the Dungeon Master’s Guide, along with the following topics. Record the answers to the questions in this section to inspire your campaign.

Creating Domains of Dread.name: Creating Domains of Dread
Creating Domains of Dread.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.hqDIKk1gLYuUwifu]

Any fear might find terrible life amid the Domains of Dread. Each domain highlights its own distinctive terror or flavor of the macabre. Chapter 2 presents numerous Domains of Dread and their respective Darklords, but you can blend legendary evils, unexpected monstrosities, and grim settings into your own horrifying creations.

This chapter explores how to create a Domain of Dread, starting with defining its Darklord, the villain at the domain’s heart. Descriptions of various genres of horror also provide details to guide and inspire your creations.

Creating a Darklord.name: Creating a Darklord
Creating a Darklord.text:
Domains are reflections of and prisons for the Darklords they confine. To devise a Domain of Dread, first consider its central antagonist and prisoner: its Darklord. The following sections detail how to develop a Darklord as a focal antagonist in your horror adventures.

Building Your Darklord

A Darklord’s memories, desires, mistakes, and evil deeds shape a domain and its inhabitants. This chapter presents tables with suggested questions and motifs related to a Darklord’s past and personality. Choose or randomly generate prompts to develop your Darklord further, or use the table entries as inspiration.

To build a Darklord, follow these steps:

Step 1: Choose Genres. What horror genres does your Darklord fit into? Use those genres’ principles to help develop your Darklord.

Step 2: Determine Their Past. Who was the Darklord before they turned to irredeemable evil? What ambitions and flaws drove them to commit heinous acts? How did the Darklord first attract the attention of the Dark Powers? The “Darklord’s Past” section provides a multitude of prompts to inspire your Darklord’s origin story.

Step 3: Determine Their Identity. What monstrous form does your Darklord’s evil take? Turn to each genre chosen in step 1, then consult that genre’s respective “Villain Identities” and “Villain Torments” sections. Feel free to mix and match the tables from different genres to achieve your desired feel.

Genres of Horror

Horror stories span a wide variety of genres. One tale might explore the grotesque, warping flesh and bone into perversions of the living; another might be fantastical and philosophical, using restless ghosts to examine the nature of the soul.

Before you begin building a Darklord, consider which genres of horror you’d like to explore. The Genres of Horror table describes the six major genres of horror explored later in this chapter and their common motifs. If you’re uncertain where to start, use the table to identify which motifs interest, excite, or inspire fear in you. The sections later in the chapter also detail each genre’s core principles, which drive how you develop the Darklord, the domain, and the adventures within.

These genres aren’t mutually exclusive. Feel free to blend themes from different genres to create a unique and terrifying story. The end of this chapter briefly describes a handful of additional genres for consideration.

Genres of Horror

Genre

Characters in Stories of This Genre …

Common Motifs

Body horror

Explore the unpredictability of flesh.

Gore, loss of bodily autonomy, parasitic infestations

Cosmic horror

Face incomprehensible insignificance.

Faceless terrors, infinite voids, unblinking eyes

Dark fantasy

Battle grim evils and wicked magic.

Antiheroes, evil witchcraft, misty forests

Folk horror

Unmask ancient taboos and traditions.

Cryptic legends, masked cults, secluded towns

Ghost stories

Examine mortality and tragic histories.

Decrepit mansions, incorporeal haunts, old ruins

Gothic horror

Confront passions and evils within.

Ancient castles, strong emotions, tarnished splendor

Darklord’s Past

Every Darklord has committed irredeemable acts that attract the attention of the Dark Powers. Yet Darklords are still individuals who, at one point, had a home, childhood, and family. The following sections each examine a different aspect of the Darklord’s life prior to imprisonment.

Past Life

What circumstances and choices pushed the Darklord down their twisted path? Choose or randomly generate at least three questions from the Past Life table to answer. If you generate the same question again, use the question one above or below the entry instead.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwPastLiferI3jo rollable resultLabel="Question"]

Dark Catalyst

Negative emotions can twist one’s ideals into something sinister. Choose or randomly generate at least one prompt from the Dark Catalyst table to develop what encouraged the Darklord’s corruption.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwDarkCatalyssE rollable resultLabel="Something in the Darklord’s Past …"]

Wicked Ambitions

All Darklords have a core ambition or goal that drives them to commit evil acts. These unobtainable goals fueled their corruption and motivate the Darklords’ conflicts with adventurers in their domains. Choose or randomly generate such a goal from the Wicked Ambitions table.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwWickedAmbit7C rollable resultLabel="The Darklord …"]

Intervention of the Dark Powers

Consider how the Darklord attracted the attention of the Dark Powers. On the completion of an act of remarkable depravity, the Mists claim the Darklord—and perhaps their home—imprisoning them in a new Domain of Dread. The deed that ultimately causes a villain to be claimed by the Mists needn’t be devastating, but it should represent an evil novel enough for the Dark Powers to take note. Use the Darklords’ tales from chapter 2 to inspire how a Darklord might be claimed by the Mists.

Tarokka and Random Tables

This section provides random tables compatible with the tarokka deck (see appendix A). Here’s how to use the tarokka deck—or playing cards—instead of a d10 to generate random results:

1: Remove the High Deck. Remove all high deck cards, leaving only cards numbered one through nine plus the master of the suit.

2: Draw a Card. Ensure the deck is well shuffled, then draw from the top.

3: Consult a Table. Reference the card’s number to find your result on a table; in the tarokka deck, the master of a suit counts as a ten.

When using the tarokka deck, you can let the card’s suit add extra dimension to your results. The four of coins might encourage you to link that aspect of the Darklord to wealth, while the four of swords could provide an interpretation rooted in violence. Appendix A details interpretations of the tarokka deck’s cards.

Birth of a Darklord

Now trapped in a domain tailor-made to be their prison, the Darklord is immortal, and their greatest desire is just out of reach. At this point, you should have an idea of who the Darklord is, what they did, what form their evil takes, and how they came to the attention of the Dark Powers.

Now consider how the Darklord’s evil manifests in their domain and how it brings them into conflict with the adventurers and the domain’s inhabitants.

Monstrous Identities

Some Darklords undergo harrowing transformations on their imprisonment, as the Dark Powers warp those Darklords’ forms to reflect their evil. Alternatively, the Darklord might have been a monster to begin with, or perhaps they gained their form and powers through their deeds or a curse.

Consider how your Darklord is viewed by inhabitants of their domain. Reference the genres of horror that your Darklord fits into (later in this chapter), and use a genre’s “Villain Identities” and “Villain Torments” sections to shape the Darklord’s monstrousness. The “Villain Identities” sections provide options for the Darklord’s primary identity or form while within their domain, while the “Villain Torments” sections provide torturous twists that the Dark Powers might employ to torment the Darklord and reflect their evil.

Finishing Touches

The story of a Darklord entwines with those of the adventurers and the domain itself. As you finish fleshing out your Darklord’s details, consider the following options to further connect the Darklord to their domain and the adventures within it.

Endless Torment. In every domain, its Darklord’s greatest desire remains in view but ever out of reach. How does your Darklord’s torment remain central to and unignorable in the story?

Misty Borders. The Mists of Ravenloft surround each Domain of Dread. The Dark Powers afford Darklords some control over the Mists bordering their domains. Does your Darklord allow the Mists to function undisturbed, or do the Mists also reflect the Darklord’s nature, perpetuating the cycle of torment?

Supporting Cast. Consider what NPC types support the Darklord, exacerbate their threat, or oppose them. How do the inhabitants of the domain play into the Dark Powers’ plans, taking the Darklord’s situation from bad to worse?

Creating a Domain.name: Creating a Domain
Creating a Domain.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.MwxWf23opYat9wOK inline]

Once you’ve developed a terrifying Darklord, start shaping that Darklord’s unique Domain of Dread. Everything in this realm is inspired by the Darklord’s torment or personalized to it.

This section provides guidelines to help you create a suitably terrifying and tragic Domain of Dread.

Building a Domain

Similar to building a Darklord, you can choose or randomly generate prompts from the tables below to flesh out the domain’s details.

To build a Domain of Dread, follow these steps:

Step 1: Choose Genres. Most Domains of Dread are inspired by the same horror genres that shape their imprisoned Darklord, but feel free to mix in new genre elements.

Step 2: Develop Details. What are the distinctive elements of the domain? How do these elements reflect and twist the Darklord’s past, furthering the Darklord’s torment? The “Dread Details” section contains several tables with prompts to tailor your domain to its central prisoner.

Step 3: Create Adventures. What adventures will the characters have in this domain? Turn to the genres chosen in step 1, and consult their “Adventure Hooks” and “Adventure Settings” sections to inspire your adventures.

Step 4: Choose Monsters. The genres later in this chapter each include a table suggesting a plethora of monsters that suit the genre’s sensibilities. Consult those monster tables, and choose which terrifying creatures will haunt the characters in the domain. If none of the suggested monsters fit your ideas, you can reskin or alter the suggested monsters or choose ones that do.

Dread Details

The domain’s buildings, natural elements, landmarks, and creatures reflect the Darklord’s vile past. This section provides prompts for developing elements of your domain and connecting those specifics to the imprisoned Darklord.

Don’t get bogged down with the particulars of a working society. Things like weather, ecosystems, and agriculture needn’t follow logic in a domain. Mundane details can fade into the background as your adventures focus on the domain’s threats.

Domain Elements

Above all else, a domain is a torturous prison for its Darklord. Visceral sights, sounds, and smells within the domain should hint at the Darklord’s past villainy or serve as a means for the Dark Powers to torment the domain’s central prisoner.

Choose or randomly generate at least three questions from the Domain Elements table to answer. If you generate the same question again, use the question one above or below the entry instead. Don’t be afraid to combine details in unexpected ways—the more unnatural or off-putting, the better.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwDomainElemen3 rollable resultLabel="Question"]

Darklord’s Shadows

A domain’s features are constant, painful, and symbolic reminders of the Darklord’s wicked past. Choose or randomly generate at least one prompt from the Darklord’s Shadows table to develop how the domain reflects the Darklord’s history. Consider connecting the results to your answers to the questions in “Domain Elements.”

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwDarklordsSh3b rollable resultLabel="The Domain Reflects …"]

Unleash Your Horror.name: Unleash Your Horror
Unleash Your Horror.text: At this point, you should have a wealth of information about your Darklord and their domain. Review your notes, connect elements, establish recurring themes, and consider ways to foreshadow circumstances that adventures might focus on. You don’t need to have answers for every question now; you can define many details during adventures or by focusing on elements your players gravitate toward. For examples of fully detailed Darklords and domains, see chapter 2.

Fractures of Darkon.name: Fractures of Darkon
Fractures of Darkon.text:
The domain of Darkon has fractured into four regions: the Jagged Coast, Lychgate, the Mistlands, and Rexcrown. In Azalin’s absence, ambitious villains unleash terrors as they strive to dominate their piece of the domain. This section explores the horrors unfolding in each region of Darkon.

Jagged Coast

Stark vistas of sheer cliffs, mysterious islands, and knotted forests bleed from one to the next along the Jagged Coast. The shadow of Castle Avernus once loomed over the southern part of the region, enforcing grim peace on the small communities there. In the north, the thriving city of Martira Bay prides itself on its inhabitants’ wealth and reason.

Since Azalin’s disappearance, the Darklord’s secret police have maintained order here—obeying not the commands of the missing king, but rather the will of their regional leader, Alcio Metus.

Kargat

Azalin’s secret police, the Kargat, has long enforced the Darklord’s edicts and smothered any ember of rebellion in Darkon. Those “disappeared” by the Kargat are never seen again.

While all Darkon fears these dark-cloaked spies, few realize the Kargat is largely composed of vampires and other shape-shifting creatures. Members operate in cells assigned to particular tasks or locations, with each reporting to a regional leader. The highest-ranking members reported to Azalin. Serving the Kargat are the Kargatane, a cultlike rank of living informants and initiates who spy on their neighbors in hopes of earning supernatural power and induction into the larger organization.

The Hour of Ascension threw the Kargat into disorder. Taking advantage of this opportunity, Alcio Metus (Medium, Neutral Evil @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmVampire0000000]{Vampire}), a Kargat leader and criminal kingpin in Martira Bay, slew her peers and took over the western branch of the group. From the Black Tower in Martira Bay, Alcio spreads her Kargat spies across region and beyond.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.MVZhlNtNuTVr16NP cite=false]

Martira Bay

A foggy port on the Sea of Souls, Martira Bay is a cosmopolitan hub of trade and ideas. The populace prides itself on its worldliness and believes that the best of Darkon and lands beyond passes through the city. Martira Bay’s people appreciate reason and tangibility, and many have never left the city. This makes many residents incredulous of magic and the supernatural, even as séances and spiritualist practices flourish in posh salons.

Martira Bay is infamous for sensationalist murders. Newspapers such as The Bullseye update their devoted readership on the grisly details of sprees committed by killers such as the Midnight Slasher, the Spider, and the Weeping Woman. The overwhelmed Martira Bay constabulary works to counter these killers, as do professional detective agencies including the Ray Detectives (founded by @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwEnemiesandAll.JournalEntryPage.MistWanderersfYH#alanik-ray]{Alanik Ray}; see chapter 3) and the Dusklight Detectives.

Lychgate

Lychgate hides the magically shattered remains of Castle Avernus. Whatever ongoing magical disaster befell the castle during the Hour of Ascension has locked its ruins in time, freezing it in a semiexploded state. While Castle Avernus remains the lair of some of Azalin’s most dangerous servants, the surrounding wilderness has become overrun by shuffling corpses and mournful spirits drawn to the castle’s strange magic. The region is ruled by Ebbasheyth, Azalin’s @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmShadowDragon00]{Shadow Dragon} ally who maintains a lair in Castle Avernus.

Adventures in Lychgate likely relate to the mystery of Azalin’s disappearance. Roll on or choose a result from the Doom in Lychgate table to inspire adventures in this ruined realm.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwDoominLychgba rollable resultLabel="In Lychgate, Adventurers Might Seek …"]{Strahd’s Presence}

Mistlands

Even before the Hour of Ascension, western Darkon was a land of magical mysteries and strange creatures. No longer fearing the Darklord’s magic, ancient evils have crept from the shadows to terrorize and control the scattered communities.

In the town of Nevuchar Springs, an elf mystic and leader of the Eternal Order, Cardinna Artazas, works to turn back the Shroud. To do this, she sought to resurrect a being bonded to the land and capable of bending the Mists to its will. This resulted in the corruption of her order’s sacred pools and unleashed Darcalus Rex, a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwNecrichor0000]{Necrichor} (see chapter 5) that claims to be the first king of Darkon. Uncertain of what it has unleashed but desperate for salvation, the Eternal Order serves the whims of Darcalus Rex as the strange being grows in power.

Rexcrown

Residents of Rexcrown consider themselves the only true Darkonians due to their proximity to the capital, Il Aluk. Despite that prestige, Rexcrown is covered in deadly swamps, depleted mountains, and fields that produce disturbingly malformed crops.

The city of Il Aluk is built in a bend in the Vuchar River and rises in countless gothic spires. These mark the homes of the city’s elite, severe governmental buildings, and temples dedicated to dozens of grim deities. Serving the city are the vassal villages of Decimus, Desolatus, and Despondia, where peasants struggle to feed the city’s people and the decadent lifestyles of local nobles.

As the Shroud spreads fear among traders, Il Aluk suffers from shortages in food and other supplies. The city’s residents have grown desperate and resentful of the exploitative upper class. This has resulted in violence and the ransacking of wealthy homes. The city’s elite have rallied behind one of their eldest members, Lady Talisveri Eris.

Eris is a vain human who uses elaborate fashions and makeup to disguise the fact that her body is permanently imperceptible. Once each moon, she incites her fearful peers to go out at night and commit violence while using her high-priced Potions of Greater Invisibility. These magical attacks and Eris’s pompous, detached rhetoric exacerbate tensions in the city while enriching her nearly bankrupt family.

Adventures in Darkon.name: Adventures in Darkon
Adventures in Darkon.text:
Adventures set in Darkon’s disconnected regions often involve the mystery of Azalin’s disappearance, the domain’s dissolution, or the metaphysical nature of the Domains of Dread. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

Horror in Darkon

Beyond the adventures you might develop in Darkon’s disconnected regions, the following elements persist across the domain.

Azalin’s Fate

The events of the Hour of Ascension and the fate of Azalin loom over adventures in Darkon. The details of these mysteries are yours to decide. You can take inspiration from the following possibilities when determining Azalin’s fate.

Severed Darklord. During the Hour of Ascension, Azalin’s body was destroyed as he tore open portals to the Negative and Positive Energy Planes. The former portal lies at the heart of Castle Avernus, while the latter is known as the King’s Tear. The opposed planar energies are disrupting Darkon. Once the domain is destroyed, Azalin theorizes, his spirit jar will be released into the Shadowfell.

Shattered Darklord. The Hour of Ascension was Azalin’s failed attempt to become one of the Dark Powers. His essence is now trapped within his spirit jar, scattered pieces of his iron crown, and the floating amber sarcophagus known as the King’s Tear. Whoever frees Azalin’s essence will become the new Darklord.

Unmoored Darklord. During the Hour of Ascension, Azalin split his soul, leaving a piece in Darkon while the greater part escaped through a metaphysical rent between domains—the King’s Tear. Now, disguised as the scholar Firan Zal’honan, Azalin searches for secrets of the Dark Powers in other domains. He avoids entering the Mists without Mist-wandering guides, fearing the Dark Powers will find him otherwise.

The Dead Serve

In Darkon, Humanoid corpses reanimate as @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmZombie00000000]{Zombies} 24 hours after death. These corpses obey Azalin. While he remains missing, they roam in a winding path toward Castle Avernus. These zombies avoid other creatures, but they fight anything that blocks their path.

The Shroud

During the day, one can traverse the Mists surrounding Darkon as normal, allowing travel between domains or regions of Darkon. At night, the Mists surrounding the domain turn deadly and encroach on the land—sometimes by infinitesimal degrees, other times in unstoppable floods. A creature that touches the Shroud takes [[/damage 2d6 type=necrotic average]] and is &Reference[Frightened] until the end of its next turn. If a creature is slain by the Shroud or enters it entirely, the creature vanishes. What becomes of it is a mystery.

Darkon Campaign Arc

Campaigns set in Darkon likely involve the mystery of the Hour of Ascension. A campaign here might follow this outline:

Levels 1–3. The characters arrive in Darkon near Il Aluk, Martira Bay, or Nevuchar Springs. They soon make an ally among those who oppose the villains rising to replace Azalin: Alcio Metus, Darcalus Rex, or Talisveri Eris. The characters help defend their allies and learn of these villains’ methods.

Levels 4–15. The characters confront the region’s villain. Rather than fighting the characters, the villain entreats the group to help them establish control over the domain, which they believe will reunite Darkon’s regions and end the threat of the Shroud. To do so, they must defeat the domain’s other villains or recover the scattered remnants of Azalin’s power. The characters work to save Darkon while undermining their wicked patron.

Levels 16–18. The characters’ adventures take them to Castle Avernus, where they must either claim a remnant of Azalin’s might or learn his fate. In doing so, they can restore Azalin and bring his wrath down on those who would claim his throne.

Azalin Rex.name: Azalin Rex
Azalin Rex.text:
Darklord Who Won’t Be Caged

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwAzalinRex0000 statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Azalin’s Tale

Firan Zal’honan was a noble and heir to the city-state of Knurl on the world of Oerth. A student of magic, Firan adopted the title “Azal’Lan,” meaning “wizard-king,” when he came to power. Firan’s rule was marked by harsh laws and violent expansion, but his people flourished, as their lives were filled with magical wonders.

As Firan aged, he looked toward his succession. His son, Irik, was generous and charming but, in Firan’s eyes, soft and unfit to rule. No matter how Firan tried to teach his son of the world’s harsh realities, Irik’s kind spirit wouldn’t bend to his father’s cynical outlook. This difference came to a head when Firan caught Irik freeing political prisoners. Firan could have ignored the crime or pardoned his son. Instead, Firan personally wielded the axe during Irik’s execution.

In the years that followed, Firan was tormented by his son’s death, but he also became convinced that only he was fit to rule. Researching the mysteries of death, he discovered the secret to becoming a lich. After embracing the transition into undeath, Firan ruled for decades more, increasing Knurl’s power and earning himself many foes. Eventually, tales of magic that might restore Irik lured Firan from his home. During his search, a band of mercenaries ambushed the ruler. Firan fled into the mist to avoid the assassins. When the mist cleared, Firan found himself in Barovia.

In Barovia, peasants Firan encountered mistook his title and called him Azalin. Soon, Azalin encountered Barovia’s ruler, Strahd von Zarovich. The two came to respect each other, and Azalin taught Strahd much of the way of magic. Together, the pair learned a great deal about the nature of the Domains of Dread and made attempts to escape. Their plots failed, though, and resulted in other realms—such as the domain of Mordent—being drawn into Barovia’s orbit. Eventually, the two tyrants’ respect soured into loathing. Before Azalin and Strahd could destroy each other, Azalin entered the Mists, and the domain of Darkon spread before him.

Azalin’s Domain

Darkon is altered by Azalin’s presence, creating the following effects:

Closing the Borders. While in his domain, Azalin can close or open the borders to Darkon at will. While the borders are closed, undead terrors fill the Mists surrounding Darkon. Whenever a creature enters the Mists, one Hostile @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwZombieClot000]{Zombie Clot} (see chapter 5) appears in a random unoccupied space within 30 feet of that creature.

Lord of the Dead. While in his domain, Azalin can cast @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells24.Item.phbsplClairvoyan]{Clairvoyance}, requiring no spell components and using the same spellcasting ability as his Spellcasting action. When Azalin casts this spell in this way, he can place the sensor in any Undead in Darkon with an Intelligence of 6 or less, even if it is beyond 1 mile of Azalin. In addition, Azalin can speak through the Undead he placed the sensor in.

No Secrets. Whenever a creature in Darkon casts a spell using a level 5+ spell slot, Azalin becomes aware of that creature’s name and location. A @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells24.Item.phbsplNondetecti]{Nondetection} spell blocks this effect.

If Azalin dies, these effects end immediately.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.36tx37Hz4rtKZAoe inline classes="float-right size-three"]

Azalin’s Lair

During the Hour of Ascension, Castle Avernus—Azalin’s seat of rule and magical laboratory—erupted in a magical conflagration. Now, Castle Avernus stands frozen in time at the instant of its destruction, blasted to magically floating chunks of rubble. Its towers pitch wildly and crumble to pieces amid arcane flames, yet they don’t fall. Although time has halted in the ruins, Azalin’s servants who avoided the disaster have returned, unaffected by the castle’s frozen time. Meanwhile, the fortress’s magic attempts to heal the wreckage, slowly creating new halls and winding stairs between the fractured floors.

Castle Avernus

Amid its magical destruction, Castle Avernus is a vast structure filled with Azalin’s arcane creations and summoned servants. Prominent locations from this ruin are depicted on map 2.7 and detailed here:

Outer Bailey and Gallery. The castle’s entry, called the Maw by Azalin’s servants, remains partially intact. Undead from the surrounding wilds lurk here, waiting for their lord’s commands.

Hall of Records. A column of stationary but still deadly magical fire blasts through Avernus’s library. Scattered on ruined islands of rubble are the remains of a collection said to hold the biographies of all who have died in Darkon.

Tomb of Irik Zal’honan. Here stands the tomb of Azalin’s son, Irik. The @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGhost000000000]{Ghost} of Irik appears to those with benevolent intentions. Despite his tragic fate, he forgives his father—but he doesn’t want Azalin to harm anyone else. He can’t leave the crypt and knows little of Azalin’s plots.

Lair of Ebbasheyth. The @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmShadowDragon00]{Shadow Dragon} Ebbasheyth served as Azalin’s adviser and steed. While she has moved much of her hoard to Mount Nyid, she still watches over the treasures here.

Hall of Life. The Hall of Life has largely crumbled and is accessible only via gaps in the ceiling of the floor below. Within survives a circle of glass and emerald cylinders. These hold the remains of Azalin’s magical cloning experiments. A gigantic dragon skull made of gold has fallen into the midst of these failed experiments. This is Azalin’s original spirit jar, but the skull’s eyes, which once glowed with unholy flames, now look empty.

Heart of Avernus. Amid the tower’s wreckage glows the magical anomaly called the Heart of Avernus. This is the epicenter of the disaster that destroyed the castle during the Hour of Ascension. This mysterious force draws droves of the undead to feed on the ongoing magical reaction.

Secrets of Avernus.name: Secrets of Avernus
Secrets of Avernus.text:
@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.adventures.Adventure.suXm7L66cl9XQ5sg]{Adventure for Level 16 Characters}

Situation. The characters must learn the secret of Azalin’s disappearance.

Hook. The threat of the Shroud and those who would claim Azalin’s throne has grown too great to ignore. An ally directs the characters to the only place they can find answers regarding Azalin’s disappearance: Castle Avernus.

Scene Configuration Overview

  • If you encounter broken links below, they will appear once you install the adventure in your World.

  • The map is prepared for your adventures through Castle Avernus, with Global Illumination enabled by default.

  • Scene Regions are configured to transport tokens from the first to the second floor and from the eighth to the ninth floor. No other stairs are equipped with Scene Regions.

  • NPCs are placed in the scene and hidden until you are ready for PCs to interact with them.

Encounters

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.I4SY08Qvu1dx3HCd cite=false classes="float-right"]

This adventure uses map 2.7: Castle Avernus. Before starting, consult the “Azalin’s Fate” section and develop the details behind the Darklord’s disappearance. The floors of Castle Avernus are disconnected, allowing characters to access them in any order.

Lychgate Encounters. While traveling through Lychgate or between floors of Castle Avernus, roll on the following table to determine what the characters encounter.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwLychgateEncxk rollable resultLabel="Encounter" caption=false]

Into the Maw. Droves of animate corpses fill the outer bailey. A Spirit Naga named @UUID[Actor.Psqzzwws2CqdgEg3]{Lossa} lurks amid the rubble. If she spots the characters, she impersonates Azalin and commands the corpses to fight. They rise as three @UUID[Actor.aOjFgjify1SXj81B]{Zombie Clots} (see chapter 5).

The Archive. @UUID[Actor.wmVU3JkVBdSPdkES]{Elzarath}, an Indifferent Ghost with a floating severed head, laments the state of the archives. Elzarath shares that they once held the biographies of all who died in Darkon, but now the books are scattered. If asked about Azalin’s biography, the ghost searches and finds nothing. Elzarath doesn’t know Azalin’s actual name, but if specifically asked about the biography of Firan Zal’honan, the ghost finds the book. This biography relates the information in the “Azalin’s Tale” section and key details of the Hour of Ascension.

Crypt of Irik Zal’honan. The Ghost @UUID[Actor.thHYcvYOVaLgC6yD]{Irik Zal’honan} appears to any Good-aligned creature or those who call out to him. Irik can’t leave this area, but he aids those who seek to restore his father. He knows Azalin’s true name is Firan Zal’honan and shares that with anyone who destroys the corrupted experiment in the Hall of Life in the ruins above. Irik reveals that he is Azalin’s son only if asked directly.

Hall of Life. This room holds a dragon skull made of gold and a circle of glass and emerald cylinders. The dragon skull remains magically anchored so only Azalin can move it. The cylinders are cracked and filled with bubbling decay. If someone smashes all the cylinders, the cylinders fuse into a @UUID[Actor.RtmsUXTeYJLFLnjq]{Hostile Abomination} with the same stat block as a Lesser Star Spawn Emissary (see chapter 5).

Ebbasheyth’s Lair. @UUID[Actor.i59yIXsFEHRVd66e]{Ebbasheyth} the Shadow Dragon is in her lair, attended by a @UUID[Actor.YpPaAEjQ0mPQUHyY]{Kargat Messenger} (Medium Vampire Nightbringer). The monsters are Hostile, but any character who takes an Influence action and succeeds on a [[/check per dc=16 format=long]] can make them Indifferent. Characters who mention their desire to restore Azalin have Advantage on this check. If made Indifferent, Ebbasheyth tells the characters the archives might hold details of Azalin’s schemes. If asked what name to search for, she doesn’t know but relates that the spirit in the room below might.

Conclusion. The biography of Firan Zal’honan in the archive reveals the secret of Azalin’s disappearance. From this, the characters should know how to find or restore Azalin or how to recover his power. Characters also earn a bonus [[/award 9800xp each]].

Darkon.name: Darkon
Darkon.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.t0GOzYU2ooFb6YFX]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.bWB1KLCDfsPlUXJl]

Domain on the Brink of Destruction

Darklord: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwAzalinRex0000]{Azalin Rex}

Genres: Dark fantasy and disaster horror

Hallmarks: Fractured realm, magical ruins, ongoing supernatural catastrophe

Mist Talismans: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwAshesofaCorps]{Ashes of a corpse}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwCoin000000000]{coin} stamped with Azalin Rex’s face, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwTaintedSpring]{tainted spring water}

Aligned Tarokka Card: Necromancer

Opposed Tarokka Card: Donjon

Darkon is a sprawling realm of somber gothic cities, vast wildernesses, hidden secrets, and the dead. The ruins of forgotten magical empires scar the land. Undead creatures haunt the domain’s lonely reaches, and the living acknowledge that the land’s morbid magic might one day reanimate their corpses.

Yet, for all its mysteries, the domain of Darkon has failed. Across the realm, the Mists close in, inexorably overwhelming the land. These Mists turned ravenous are known as the Shroud, and what they consume doesn’t reemerge.

Darkon’s dissolution ties to the disappearance of its Darklord, Azalin Rex. During a disastrous event known as the Hour of Ascension, Azalin’s citadel, Castle Avernus, was destroyed, and a mysterious golden star called the King’s Tear appeared in the heavens. Since then, no one has seen Azalin, and the Shroud has eroded the domain.

Despite the encroaching annihilation, life in Darkon hasn’t changed. The residents of towns and cities far from the domain’s borders dismiss reports of vanishing frontiers as fearmongering. As Darkon splits into crumbling islands, ambitious individuals vie for Azalin’s power, each touting that they alone can save the land by becoming its tyrant.

Domain Features

Darkon is known for the following features:

Divided Domain. The Mists consuming Darkon have divided the land into four separate regions.

Hour of Ascension. Azalin Rex vanished during an event his servants call the Hour of Ascension.

Inevitable Undeath. The night after any Humanoid dies, its corpse reanimates and shambles into the night. Locals swiftly burn bodies to prevent this.

King’s Tear. Since the Hour of Ascension, a golden star called the King’s Tear hangs in the sky. The sun and moon pass behind this star in their orbits.

Secret Police. The Kargat, the domain’s secret police, remains active in Darkon’s largest cities: Il Aluk and Martira Bay.

Locations in Darkon

Darkon is a place of dark wonders and apathy. The ruins of impossible structures litter the landscape, but the domain’s jaded people ignore these marvels to focus on daily concerns.

The regions of Darkon are described below, and other notable locations are summarized on the Darkon Locations table.

Regions of Darkon

The Shroud has divided Darkon into four parts (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwDarkon0000000.JournalEntryPage.FracturesofDarko]{Fractures of Darkon}” for details):

Jagged Coast. Dense forests and shattered cliffs compose the Jagged Coast, skirting the Sea of Souls. Undead within the forests prey on isolated communities here.

Lychgate. Lychgate is ruled by the dead. Aimless undead creatures wander the forest, milling around amid the time-locked ruins of Castle Avernus and many of Azalin’s powerful servants.

Mistlands. Scattered communities dot the Mistlands, which were once Darkon’s wild eastern reaches. Strange monsters emerge from the forests and mountains to prey on the isolated lands.

Rexcrown. The heart of Darkon, Rexcrown has become a sagging bog. From it rise the spires and grim wonders of Il Aluk, Darkon’s capital, and the homes of the domain’s indifferent elite.

In Darkon, Azalin was a tyrannical wizard-king obeyed by the living and the dead. When he first appeared before his people, he adopted an illusion of the form he possessed in life: that of a middle-aged human man with dark hair and a superior demeanor. However, his true form was that of a moldering lich, eyes ablaze with blasphemous energy.

Since arriving in the Domains of Dread, Azalin has been unable to learn new magic. For a wizard who forsook his mortality to pursue arcane studies, this was the ultimate frustration. Despite the limits on his studies, Azalin numbered among the few Darklords who understood the mysterious nature of the Domains of Dread. For generations, Azalin struggled to escape Darkon.
