His magical atrocities reshaped Darkon and the Domains of Dread time and again. However, these struggles gave Azalin only a glimpse of freedom, and his metaphysical violations inevitably reverted to the Dark Powers’ original designs. Nevertheless, Azalin refused to stay a prisoner. His most recent plot culminated in the Hour of Ascension and the destruction of Castle Avernus. Since then, no one in Darkon has seen the Darklord, and his realm has begun deteriorating.

The stat block in this section presents the Darklord as he was prior to his disappearance and how he would be should he return. Azalin current spirit jar is a golden amulet he carries. If this amulet is destroyed, Azalin’s original spirit jar—an immovable dragon skull in the ruins of Castle Avernus—reactivates, and the lich’s spirit returns to Darkon if his body is destroyed.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.y65AWSa7ECAdGcYX cite=false]

Darkon Locations

Location

Description

Brautslava Institute

The Brautslava Institute hosts scholars of varied fields, from science and the arts to pseudoscience and fringe supernaturalism. The college of ethics is woefully underfunded.

Castle Avernus

The castle of Azalin Rex was known as a place of grim justice and mysterious magic. Now it stands locked in time, frozen in a moment of magical catastrophe.

Engel’s End

Most of Engel’s End’s residents have fled the Shroud, but a golden light shines from the belfry of the hamlet’s chapel. Since its lighting, the Mists haven’t encroached closer.

Il Aluk

With supplies dwindling, Darkon’s capital of Il Aluk is in disorder. The elite cling to lies of prosperity as they starve and steal from the populace. Riots and assassinations occur daily.

Karg

The small city of Karg is half-consumed by the Shroud. When residents began fleeing, the Kargat sealed the gates to prevent others from succumbing to “mass hysteria” and leaving.

Martira Bay

A city of fog and mysteries, Martira Bay is home to infamous serial killers and renowned detectives. Its busy port receives traders from other domains and even stranger lands.

Nartok

The town of Nartok keeps a strict curfew, barring its gates at dusk to keep out the forest’s undead. Many locals once served at Castle Avernus, which used to be visible in the distance.

Neblus

Built amid the trees, Neblus’s oldest structures are home to wealthy crime families. These criminals extort the locals and suppress their naturalistic traditions.

Nevuchar Springs

Nevuchar Springs’ hot springs have miraculous properties, but some bathers emerge with different personalities. The mystics of the Eternal Order research and protect the springs.

Rivalis

The fishing town of Rivalis embraces its quaintness and avoids speaking of the monsters lurking in the nearby lakes. The untended estates of old, wealthy families lie outside town.

Vradlock

Vradlock’s dragonborn, drow, and goliath citizens claim descent from the spirits of Mount Nyid. Since the Shroud cut off the town, the mountain experiences frequent quakes.

Watchers’ Stronghold

Within the vault-like Watcher’s Stronghold, monastic members of the Order of the Guardians (see chapter 3) contain and protect cursed or dangerous magical oddities.

Adventures in Dementlieu.name: Adventures in Dementlieu
Adventures in Dementlieu.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.FH6FI7Gp81yw5g71 inline]

Adventures set in Dementlieu feature mystery and deception, often involving illusions and fairy-tale grandeur masking depravity and murder. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

Horror in Dementlieu

Adventures in Dementlieu often include the following elements.

Lies and Liars

In Dementlieu, one can take little at face value. Terrible food might be called gourmet, while horrendous art might be dubbed avant garde. Those who resist such claims might find themselves mocked for not understanding a thing’s appeal. This can lead to challenges that range from the comical to the surreal. Roll on or choose a result from the Dementlieu Deceptions table to inspire overlooked events that might lead to broader consequences.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwDementlieuDvX rollable resultLabel="A Dementlieuse Liar …"]{Dementlieu Deceptions}

Scourge of the Red Death

Between masquerades, Saidra d’Honaire fixates on her past and her fear that people suspect her ignoble origins. At these times, her glamorous facade fades, reducing her to a crimson spirit. In this guise, she slips into the night and scours Port-a-Lucine, targeting the sick, poor, or uncouth; those who disparage nobles; and those she suspects of investigating her past. The resulting life-drained bodies are attributed to a notorious figure locals call the Red Death.

Using the Red Death. While Port-a-Lucine’s residents ignore the Red Death’s victims—potentially chiding corpses for their laziness or public drunkenness—the characters are unlikely to overlook bodies so clearly drained of life. Use such corpses to remind the group that the lies of Dementlieu’s people are dangerous denials of reality.

Dementlieu Campaign Arc

Campaigns set in Dementlieu often involve the discovery and unraveling of a conspiracy. A campaign here might follow this outline:

Levels 1–3. Someone asks the characters in Port-a- Lucine for help finding a missing friend, opera performer, or noble. Following the clues, the characters learn this is but one of a rash of disappearances involving dangerous creatures hiding in plain sight—such as @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGargoyle000000]{Gargoyles}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWererat0000000]{Wererats}, or @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwWaxwork000000]{Waxworks} (see chapter 5).

Level 4–5. The mystery points toward an elite member of society. The characters can’t gain access to this individual, but they know the noble attends the Grand Masquerade of Duchess d’Honaire. The characters must prepare for and infiltrate the event (detailed below) to learn more.

Levels 6–8. Learning that the suspicious noble supports or benefits from the disappearances, the characters investigate the noble’s home or business (such as the opera house or House of Wax). There, they discover and disrupt the creatures behind the disappearances, likely finding that the vanished people have been turned into monsters. If the noble escapes, an unrefusable invitation to the Grand Masquerade might draw them out, resulting in a showdown during that event—and potentially attracting the ire of Duchess d’Honaire.

Saidra d’Honaire.name: Saidra d’Honaire
Saidra d’Honaire.text:
Darklord Who Rules Illusions

Duchess Saidra d’Honaire knows her life isn’t her own—she knows she isn’t alive at all—yet she ignores that for the sparkle of empty celebrations and a parody of her ideal life. The duchess terrorizes Dementlieu, forcing the realm to conform to her desires. Those who expose the flaws in her fantasy and remind her of the desperation she once endured, she slays using her life-sapping power.

All can plainly see Duchess d’Honaire isn’t one of the living. Nevertheless, servants and sycophants flatter her poise, beauty, and array of lavish masquerade costumes. When not hosting decadent social events, Saidra removes her mask and slips into the night. Through the streets and alleys of Port-a-Lucine, the Darklord prowls as the Red Death: a murderous spirit that preys on liars, the struggling, and all who remind her of her humble origins.

Saidra is overbearing and elitist, despite being unworldly. She fears being exposed as a fraud and projects this fear onto others, violently accusing them of being liars on the barest evidence or sign of vulgarity. Yet, in mysterious letters and rumors, Saidra receives evidence that her own simple, spiteful family dwells somewhere beyond her city. Their confessions could bring all her deceptions to light.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwSaidradHonair statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Saidra’s Tale

Despite being raised in poverty, Saidra d’Honaire was an arrogant bully whose father led her to believe she was secretly royalty. When her father remarried, Saidra suffered slights and mockery at the hands of her callous stepmother and stepsisters.

The night her father revealed that Saidra was only distantly related to nobility, she fled into the night. At her mother’s grave, Saidra’s curses were overheard by a mysterious stranger. This grandmotherly figure offered to grant Saidra any wish. When the stranger heard Saidra’s desire, she cackled and dressed the girl in rubies and a crimson gown. Resplendent, Saidra set off to the masquerade ball celebrating the coronation of the land’s new duke.

At the masquerade, Saidra charmed the duke and his court. Yet, as bells tolled midnight, the gems on her dress evaporated, releasing a magical poison. The evil magic killed everyone at the masquerade, a massacre that would have fulfilled Saidra’s murderous wish and left her within reach of the throne. As Saidra toured a palace full of the dying, clouds of spreading poison rose in a mysterious mist.

When the mist cleared, Saidra found herself in a grand estate in Port-a-Lucine. The estate’s servants acknowledged her as their duchess, but the lethal poison had dissolved Saidra’s body into a deathly vapor. As none seemed to notice, Saidra embraced the fantasy that she had achieved her vain desires.

Saidra’s Domain

Dementlieu is altered by Saidra’s presence, creating the following effects:

Closing the Borders. While in her domain, Saidra can close or open the borders to Dementlieu at will. While the borders are closed, the countryside called Chateaufaux appears in the Mists surrounding Dementlieu. Creatures in the Mists get lost amid mazelike hedgerows and fogbanks, only to reappear on the outskirts of Port-a-Lucine 2d6 hours later.

Empowered Actors. Humanoids add [[/r 1d8#Add to Deception or Performance checks]] to Charisma (Deception or Performance) checks made while wearing a mask.

Invitation. At dawn, Saidra can choose a creature whose name she knows or that she’s seen before. A simple mask and an invitation to the next Grand Masquerade appear to that creature. The letter and mask appear in an unobserved but obvious space, such as a bedside table or among gear.

If Saidra dies, these effects end immediately. The effects resume if Saidra gains a new body (see her Darklord Restoration trait).

Saidra’s Lair

The duchess dwells at the d’Honaire Estate. This lavish manor and its manicured grounds cover the largest island in Lucine Bay.

D’Honaire Estate

The d’Honaire Estate is well lit and filled with expensive decor and artwork—some of it quite good, but most is of middling quality. The estate is depicted on map 2.9 and has the following features:

Grand Entry. A grand staircase sweeps from the estate’s entry to airy balconies above. This broad space also serves as a ballroom and is the focus of the duchess’s social events.

Salons. Elegant rooms with distinct themes fill the estate. Popular spaces include the Skobeloff Salon (themed like a sunken ship), the Sulfur Salon (with dizzying wallpaper), and the Taupe Salon (filled with underwhelming taxidermies).

Sea Caves and Docks. Those who travel to the island estate by gondola dock in this sculpted sea cave. Stairs rise from the docks to the main estate.

Second Floor. The halls remain locked and guarded. Within are the duchess’s quarters, a joyless, hollow space that the Darklord haunts between stalkings and social events.

The Grand Masquerade.name: The Grand Masquerade
The Grand Masquerade.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.ycltR1wPqvcNfNY3 classes="float-right" cite=false]

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.adventures.Adventure.EckLRVvrcmCNVAot]{Adventure for Level 4 Characters}

Situation. The Grand Masquerade is an opportunity for characters to meet Dementlieuse nobles and gain information they can’t otherwise access.

Hook. The date of the next masquerade is public knowledge. To attend, the characters need to create their own costumes and invitations.

Encounters

The adventure uses @UUID[Scene.CDAaJW86SpWiM3Ib]{map 2.9} and consists of these encounters.

Dressed to Kill. Attendees at the Grand Masquerade are expected to sport lavish costumes featuring fanciful masks. Any character not wearing an appropriate Costume, a mask, and at least 100 GP worth of jewelry (or appropriate magical alternatives) has Disadvantage on [[/check cha format=long]]{Charisma checks} made during this adventure. Armor can’t be worn with a nonmagical costume. If a character becomes &Reference[Bloodied] while wearing a nonmagical costume, their costume is destroyed.

@UUID[Actor.sHPa24FIxrk3bQLT]{Arrival.} At the entrance to the estate, servants check the characters’ invitations. A character with a forged invitation must succeed on a [[/check dec dc=10 format=long]] to be admitted. Those who fail are asked to leave. If they refuse, four @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGhoul000000000]{Ghouls} wearing formal wear and masks fight them.

Masquerade. The Grand Masquerade unfolds in three tableaux. During each, the characters can decide to dance, gossip, flirt, or do something else. Each character can earn renown for their group with the ball’s attendees and host by making positive impressions on other guests (see chapter 3 of the Dungeon Master’s Guide for details on @UUID[Compendium.dnd-dungeon-masters-guide.content.JournalEntry.dmgDmsToolbox000.JournalEntryPage.QnYXlS4TpZlmqSt2]{renown}). Each positive interaction can increase the group’s Renown Score by 1 per character per tableau.

Duchess d’Honaire has yet to appear, but hundreds of other costumed nobles mingle during the masquerade. These masked guests might share their names, but a character must succeed on a [[/check prc dc=10 format=long]] to identify even someone they’re familiar with. The following guests pay particular attention to the characters:

@UUID[Actor.Quez7pakN8moyQ5O]{Alexandre du Cire} (Medium @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwWaxwork000000]{Waxwork}; see chapter 5) poses as a human and runs the House of Wax. He urges the characters to visit his establishment. His costume is a suit on one side and a ball gown on the other. He is Friendly.

@UUID[Actor.Yvwmy0oBhCyoqokF]{Angel Pajaro} (Medium @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWerewolf000000]{Werewolf}) is a famed diva of the Port-a-Lucine opera and is Hostile. She likes to humiliate her fans but loves flattery from other performers. She’s dressed as an arctic fox.

@UUID[Actor.zKeA9kKvwKRKsrhQ]{Julienne d’Honaire} (Medium, Neutral Good @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmBandit00000000]{Bandit}) is a Friendly human. She’s bubbly and behaves in an unrefined manner. She claims to be related to the duchess but confides that she wasn’t invited. Julienne wears a secondhand ballerina costume.

@UUID[Actor.Y6MFTKUdwmMbx6gE]{Lord Governor Marcel Guignol} (Medium @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmNoble000000000]{Noble}) is Indifferent and didn’t want to attend the event. The elderly human is jittery and hates loud noises, and he seeks an excuse to leave. He’s dressed as a smiling jester.

@UUID[Actor.fgKDS397JbX0tEBE]{Raoul Rameau} (Medium @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGuardCaptain00]{Guard Captain}) is a member of the city watch. He tries to guess who people are and why they’ve been invited—his hunches are poor. He’s dressed as a toy monkey with cymbals.

@UUID[Actor.OSa4JVhcvJkWwO4O]{Uncouth Exchange.} After the second tableau, two revelers wearing the same costumes as two nearby characters spot their lookalikes. Outraged by this faux pas, these @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmBerserker00000]{Berserkers} fight them, wielding sharpened candelabras (these function as Greataxes). If the characters defeat their attackers, their Renown Score increases by 2.

The Duchess Appears. After three tableaux, @UUID[Actor.QJDOIEk5igERfuM7]{Saidra d’Honaire} (see @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwDementlieu000.JournalEntryPage.SaidradHonaire9J]{earlier} in this chapter) appears. She is fabulous and terrible as she drifts over the flattering crowd. Nearing the characters, she halts, gasps in revulsion, and points. “You! Never have I seen such an audaciously incompetent impostor! Who do you think you are to violate these hallowed halls?!” Near a character, Julienne d’Honaire begins to reply, and the duchess instantly disintegrates her. A ghoulish servant soon appears with a dustpan to clean the spot where Julienne stood. If party members spent two or more tableaux with Julienne, the group’s Renown Score decreases by 1.

@UUID[Actor.ARaXFDr4mt40JmYG]{Conclusion.} If the group’s Renown Score is 5 or less, the duchess shrieks, outraged to find more unworthies. Right away, four @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGhoul000000000]{Ghoul} servants appear and fight the characters; two Ghouls arrive every round until the party flees the estate. The duchess doesn’t fight.

If the characters’ Renown Score is at least 6, the duchess compliments one of their costumes as she drifts by in time with the music. If the characters came to the masquerade to meet or influence someone, that individual appears soon after, impressed by the duchess’s compliment. Other members of the city’s nobility or individuals the characters met earlier in the night also might approach them with invitations or offers of employment. Characters earn a bonus [[/award 600xp each]].

Dementlieu.name: Dementlieu
Dementlieu.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.H2v8Rg8sceJHTvrT]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.MANJjyRegTN6E8Jl]

Domain of Decadent Delusion

Darklord: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwSaidradHonair]{Saidra d’Honaire}

Genres: Dark fantasy and psychological horror

Hallmarks: Masquerades, decadent aristocracy,
social decay, illusions, impostor syndrome

Mist Talismans: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwJeweledMask00]{Jeweled} or @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwFeatheredMask]{feathered} mask, article of @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwWornFineCloth]{well-worn fine clothing}, shoe made of @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwGlassShoe0000]{glass} or @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwGoldShoe00000]{gold}

Aligned Tarokka Card: Charlatan

Opposed Tarokka Card: Seer

Composed of the city of Port-a-Lucine and its shining, fog-shrouded outskirts, Dementlieu is a realm where the truth is smothered by collective delusion. Everything in Port-a-Lucine appears to be more than it is, and people behave as if the few examples of grandeur and prosperity were the norm. Beneath its facade, the city is rotten.

Despite pervasive claims of happiness and success, the people of Dementlieu fight to survive and maintain social appearances. Every individual struggles to hide their struggles. Lying is central to life in the domain, creating a breeding ground for con artists and smiling villains.

Behind the domain’s denial of reality is its Darklord, the phantomlike Duchess Saidra d’Honaire. The cynosure of high society, this masked duchess ensnares the city’s upper class in her exaggerated ideas of luxury and civility. During her weekly masquerade balls, Saidra’s guests must perform to her expectations. Those who fail to charm her are decried as impostors and reduced to ash by her magic. Saidra’s terror also grips the city’s desperate. Her murderous, unmasked form—the Red Death—stalks the benighted streets, preying on any who tarnish her preposterous vision of a perfect society.

Domain Features

Dementlieu is known for the following features:

City of Lies. Nothing in Port-a-Lucine is as it appears. Everyone pretends to be more successful than they are. Lies great and small fill everyday communication. All residents know these truths, but none dares speak of them.

Grand Masquerade. Every week Duchess d’Honaire holds a Grand Masquerade. Invitations are coveted, and invitees who don’t attend rarely survive for long.

Lord Governor. Administration of Port-a-Lucine rests in the hands of Lord Governor Marcel Guignol. He is counseled by aristocratic advisers, including the duchess.

Masks. Decorative masks are common accessories, worn at social events and in daily life.

Locations in Dementlieu

Port-a-Lucine’s grand structures and garish promenades dazzle viewers, distracting most from the grimy streets and cramped homes beyond. The countryside of Chateaufaux is described below, and other notable locations are summarized on the Dementlieu Locations table.

Chateaufaux

The people of Port-a-Lucine speak of the countryside, Chateaufaux, where nobles summer at their estates and where idyllic villages send goods for sale into the city. But no one travels far beyond the city walls, no goods arrive from beyond, and Chateaufaux doesn’t actually exist. The Mists creep close to Port-a-Lucine, occasionally exposing patches of forests and meadows but nothing more. Nevertheless, Chateaufaux remains a convenient explanation for whatever mysteriously appears or disappears.

Duchess Saidra d’Honaire knows her life isn’t her own—she knows she isn’t alive at all—yet she ignores that for the sparkle of empty celebrations and a parody of her ideal life. The duchess terrorizes Dementlieu, forcing the realm to conform to her desires. Those who expose the flaws in her fantasy and remind her of the desperation she once endured, she slays using her life-sapping power.

All can plainly see Duchess d’Honaire isn’t one of the living. Nevertheless, servants and sycophants flatter her poise, beauty, and array of lavish masquerade costumes. When not hosting decadent social events, Saidra removes her mask and slips into the night. Through the streets and alleys of Port-a-Lucine, the Darklord prowls as the Red Death: a murderous spirit that preys on liars, the struggling, and all who remind her of her humble origins.

Saidra is overbearing and elitist, despite being unworldly. She fears being exposed as a fraud and projects this fear onto others, violently accusing

them of being liars on the barest evidence or sign of vulgarity. Yet, in mysterious letters and rumors, Saidra receives evidence that her own simple, spiteful family dwells somewhere beyond her city. Their confessions could bring all her deceptions to light.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.oXZuIjdcx6PBALic cite=false]

Dementlieu Locations

Location

Description

Bonniville Museum of the Sea

Green windows tint this museum’s collection of aquatic oddities and preserved sea creatures in drowned light. A display of the wolfish masthead of the S.S. Bonniville is said to be cursed.

D’Honaire Estate

The home of Saidra d’Honaire and the site of her Grand Masquerades is accessible only by the nobles’ stylish gondolas or larger ships (see “Saidra’s Lair”).

Great Library

The Great Library’s supposedly peerless collection houses fewer than a thousand titles, but hundreds of copies of each. A few rare tomes hide among the unremarkable copies.

House of Wax

Alexandre du Cire’s House of Wax displays wax copies of nobles and celebrities. In the basements, those who’ve been replaced by lifelike wax constructs remain imprisoned.

Lord Governor’s Manor

The lord governor’s home is the largest and most ornate in the city. The staff maintains only a portion of the estate, as the rest is said to be haunted by the city’s former rulers.

Mother of Tears Cathedral

The Ezra-worshipping clergy of the grandiose Mother of Tears Cathedral weep for all the suffering and horror of the world but take no action to cure or combat it.

Palace of Enlightenment

The seat of Port-a-Lucine’s government is filled with empty council chambers. A few caretakers mind the gardens and the collections of art, civil records, and architectural plans.

Phlegethan Hospital

A patient with a preternatural ability to bend others to his will has taken over the Phlegethan Hospital. He controls its doctors and claims to be the rightful Duke d’Honaire.

Port-a-Lucine
Opera House

The opera is Port-a-Lucine’s hub of culture and boasts ornate galleries, but the backstage areas are a labyrinth where crew members occasionally go missing—victims of fierce feuds.

Red Widow Theater

A sculpture of a giant crimson spider looms over the lively Red Widow Theater. Patrons seem undeterred by stories of shape-shifting spiders that hunt the theater’s clientele.

Three Odd Gables

A coven of godmotherly @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGreenHag000000]{Green Hags} lives in three crooked houses on Mill Street. The hags fulfill their guests’ dreams, but their contracts always have a hidden catch.

University of Dementlieu

The University of Dementlieu is largely occupied by narcissistic nobles and their fatuous students. Some true scholars conduct actual research hidden from their clout-chasing peers.

Zuvich Hospital

Zuvich Hospital offers skilled, science-based medical care. Dr. Doloros Zuvich recognizes that some of her patients’ accounts describe encounters with supernatural beings.

Using This Book.name: Korzystanie z tej książki
Using This Book.text:
This book explores the Domains of Dread and helps you create frightful adventures and heroes ready to confront horrors. It requires the Player’s Handbook, Dungeon Master’s Guide, and Monster Manual.

This introduction provides an overview of Ravenloft and its mysteries.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwHauntedHeroes]{Chapter 1} presents @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwSubclasses000]{subclasses}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwBackgrounds00]{backgrounds}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwSpecies000000]{species}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwFeats00000000]{feats}, and more for characters preparing to face nightmarish adventures.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwTarokkaandthe]{Chapter 2} details prominent Domains of Dread, along with their Darklords and adventures within.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwCampaignsinth]{Chapter 3} provides tools for Dungeon Masters running horror adventures, including ways to develop frightful campaigns and rules for Haunted Bastions.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwCreatingDomai]{Chapter 4} explores how Dungeon Masters can create their own Domains of Dread.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwHorrorMonster]{Chapter 5} reveals allies and enemies that might appear in terrifying tales in the Mists or beyond.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwTarokkaDeck00]{The appendix} details the tarokka deck and how to use it in your adventures.

Secrets of Ravenloft.name: Secrets of Ravenloft
Secrets of Ravenloft.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.eW3PEQGfTEP4sIbM inline classes="float-right"]

Untold mysteries pervade the Domains of Dread, but these truths underpin the setting:

1: Ravenloft Is Malleable. The domains of Ravenloft are disconnected demiplanes. They don’t share a world or a certain reality. Few truths connect them, and even inviolate facts—such as time or death—might change. The only constant is fear.

2: The Dark Powers Reign. Sinister forces known as the Dark Powers manipulate the Domains of Dread. Unknowable and omnipotent, the Dark Powers torment their captives for unfathomable reasons.

3: Domains Imprison Darklords. Each of Ravenloft’s demiplanes imprisons a Darklord, a villain tormented by the Dark Powers. Domains ironically twist Darklords’ desires, trapping these villains in cycles of dread and despair.

4: The Mists’ Clutches. Constant fogs known as the Mists encircle each domain and might rise anywhere within. The Mists trap individuals within domains and make travel between domains uncertain. They sometimes drag inhabitants of other worlds into the Domains of Dread.

5: Heroes Are Few. Few oppose the villainy of the Darklords. The domains don’t contain benevolent nations or untarnished heroic orders. Those who oppose evil can rely only on themselves, and they might inspire others or attract greater terrors.

6: Isolated Islands. The Domains of Dread are isolated from one another and the rest of the multiverse. Magic that facilitates travel between planes doesn’t function unless the Dark Powers allow it, and most inhabitants know little of realms beyond. Nevertheless, anything from across the multiverse might appear in Ravenloft, albeit with an unsettling twist.

7: Rule of Fear. The mysteries of the Dark Powers and the Mists facilitate frightful adventures. Whatever inspires terror and aids your horror adventures is the will of the Dark Powers.

Domains of Dread.name: Domains of Dread
Domains of Dread.text: The Mists reach from the darkest shadows of the multiverse, condemning the wicked and the brave to endless nightmares as prisoners of the Domains of Dread. These grim netherworlds compose the Ravenloft setting and are horrific backdrops for Dungeons & Dragons adventures steeped in fear. In these haunted realms, adventurers might oppose villainous Darklords, seek escape from the Mists, or experience any terrifying adventure you can imagine.

Mist Wanderers.name: Mist Wanderers
Mist Wanderers.text:
Individuals who travel the Mists might ally with characters or share mysteries that lead to adventures elsewhere in the Domains of Dread.

Alanik Ray

The Great Detective

Alanik Ray is a peerless, elf investigator renowned in multiple domains as a master sleuth and solver of confounding mysteries. Alanik resides in Port-a-Lucine in Dementlieu with his husband, Arthur Sedgwick, but he investigates mysteries and aids sleuths across the Domains of Dread.

Arthur Sedgwick

Pragmatic Investigator

Together with his husband, Alanik Ray, the physician Arthur Sedgwick investigates criminal cases and paranormal mysteries. The practical half of the detective duo, Arthur chronicles their adventures, having published two volumes: The Life of Alanik Ray and The Casebook of Alanik Ray.

Ez d’Avenir

Fearless Adventurer

As a former pupil of @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwBestiary00000.JournalEntryPage.Om48cbwOqTCzzexH]{Dr. Rudolph van Richten}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwBestiary00000.JournalEntryPage.z6uqE33VKZYCFwee]{Ez d’Avenir} (detailed in chapter 5) is no stranger to the dangers haunting the Mists. Since venturing out on her own, Ez has gained a reputation as a skilled monster hunter and foe of evil. She often allies herself with other adventurers to put an end to evils.

Firan Zal’honan

Scholar of Sinister Secrets

The arrogant scholar Firan Zal’honan seeks the secrets underpinning the Domains of Dread. He plots to use his discoveries to escape the Mists into the “true realities” beyond. Firan hires agents to aid in his expeditions or to ferry him through the Mists—which he claims want to disrupt his efforts. Secretly Firan has ties to Azalin Rex, the missing Darklord of Darkon, or might be the Darklord in disguise (see chapter 2 for details).

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.q0ary9jzyqy1gToH inline]

Larissa Snowmane

Domain-Traveling Riverboat Captain

Larissa Snowmane ferries travelers between the Domains of Dread aboard her riverboat, River Dancer. Larissa protects those traveling with her. In the most dire circumstance, she uses her mysterious magic, the Dance of the Dead, which can control the dead at the risk of slaying the living. Larissa learned her magic in part from Anton Misroi, Darklord of Souragne. She avoids that swampy domain at all costs.

Savra Sunstar

Dhampir Vampire Hunter

Savra Sunstar is a monster hunter and the dhampir daughter of Jander Sunstar, a vampire hero who saved Savra’s mother from an early demise. For her part, Savra despises her father and seeks to slay him. She has devoted her life to hunting vampires and allies herself with like-minded adventurers.

Weathermay-Foxgrove Twins

Paranormal Investigators

The twins @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwBestiary00000.JournalEntryPage.jx6nt2igoSYndEf5]{Gennifer and Laurie WeathermayFoxgrove} (detailed in chapter 5) are tenacious adventurers who follow their family’s tradition of slaying monsters and defending the innocent. They reside in Mordent and have learned much from their surrogate uncle, Rudolph van Richten.

Organizations.name: Organizations
Organizations.text:
These organizations span multiple domains.

Church of Ezra

Goal: Seek mercy from an ambivalent god

Adventure Themes: Apathetic congregations, superstitions, witch hunting

Pious souls in multiple domains pray to Ezra, an aloof god who personifies the Mists. Ezra’s worshippers appear in congregations with varied (and sometimes conflicting) practices. The church serves largely as a formalization of local superstitions, whether in modest rural temples or urban cathedrals. When common folk give voice to their hopes or seek to ward off evil, they often pray to Ezra.

The Circle

Goal: Vanquish evil through acts of self-sacrifice

Adventure Themes: Bravery, hubris, proof of heroism, quests against evil, tragedy

Bold knights of the Circle quest from the Shadowlands in search of evil. More often than not, though, they find tragic fates. Even successful knights often venture back to the Shadowlands with evidence of their victories, potentially carrying evil back to their homeland. The knights of the Circle provide bold but reckless assistance to other would-be heroes, endlessly seeking to prove their valor.

Keepers of the Feather

Goal: Discover the paranormal and dispel evil

Adventure Themes: Paranormal investigations, occult mysteries, mysticism

The founding members of the Keepers of the Feather are @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwBestiary00000.JournalEntryPage.JOHHCyQJJIznQb27]{wereravens} (see chapter 5) in Barovia who oppose Strahd and whose reach extends to other domains. These Keepers are cautious, hiding their nature and guiding others so as not to jeopardize themselves or the mystical secrets they’ve gathered over generations. Many aided by these wereravens aren’t ever aware of their assistance.

In other realms, mysteries and talismans of the Keepers of the Feather have fallen into the hands of socialites and spiritualists. In social clubs, curious occultists pursue the mysteries of séances, fortune telling, metaphysical theories, and tales of the macabre. Such dabbling is as likely to cause catastrophes as it is to help those in need. These Keepers identify themselves with amulets that mimic the shape of a Barovian relic known as the Holy Symbol of Ravenkind.

Both factions maintain a network of trained ravens that send messages between group members in different domains.

Order of the Guardians

Goal: Destroy or contain cursed items and phenomena

Adventure Themes: Ending curses, exorcisms, supernatural investigations, vault defense

A network of monastic occultists, the Order of the Guardians hunts and destroys dangerous supernatural objects, cursed items, and stranger anomalies. If the Guardians can’t destroy dangerous relics, they contain them within hidden, heavily warded vault-monasteries. The Guardians might share goals with characters trying to prevent supernatural dangers, but they might also consider characters who employ dangerous magic to be threats.

Priests of Osybus

Goal: Release Strahd von Zarovich

Adventure Themes: Cult rituals, necromancy, secrets of the Domains of Dread

The @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwBestiary00000.JournalEntryPage.MbIrzHB8WXl84fWa]{Priests of Osybus} follow the teachings of a depraved lich and steal souls to transcend death. With their foul immortality, they seek to free @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwBarovia000000.JournalEntryPage.StrahdvonZarovic]{Strahd von Zarovich }from the Mists, an act they believe will cause the dissolution of the Domains of Dread. This inspires the priests to learn all they can about the deepest mysteries of the Mists. In shadowy cults, they conduct rituals aimed at escaping the Mists. These priests are detailed further in chapter 5.

Ulmist Inquisition

Goal: Destroy the evil that dwells in wicked hearts

Adventure Themes: Ends justifying the means, hunting evil, psionic interrogation

The three branches of the Ulmist Inquisition employ psionic powers to stamp out evil, but their zeal and willingness to peer into others’ minds cause people to fear the inquisitors as much as the villains they oppose. Cells of the Ulmist Inquisition often ally with the Church of Ezra and oppose the priests of Osybus. Ulmist inquisitors might side with adventurers against evil, but they often test the virtue of their allies’ souls. These inquisitors are detailed further in chapter 5.

Vistani

Goal: Find a way home, preserve age-old traditions, predict and avoid dangers in the Mists

Adventure Themes: Reliable guides, stories from various lands, travel between domains

Known throughout the Land of the Mists, Vistani (singular: Vistana) have a unique understanding of the Domains of Dread and the hidden paths between them. Following itinerant traditions, many Vistani and their adopted family members travel between domains, learning of distant lands, the many faces of evil, and strange wonders. Vistani never linger in one domain for long, as they endlessly seek a path to their legendary homeland.

Enemies and Allies.name: Enemies and Allies
Enemies and Allies.text: This section presents individuals and groups who might appear anywhere in the Lands of the Mists. Such individuals and organizations might create continuity between domains, facilitate travel through the Mists, or provide support to characters. Alternatively, they might serve as opponents capable of confounding characters in multiple domains.

Escaping Ravenloft.name: Escaping Ravenloft
Escaping Ravenloft.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.CuaTwnmTIIE1lW0t inline]

The Domains of Dread are as escapable as you please. The Mists might claim characters for a single adventure, then return them home. The characters might be residents of the Lands of the Mists, making escape meaningless. Or escape from Ravenloft might motivate an entire campaign. If you make escape a focus of your campaign, consider the following questions:

Possibility. Is it possible to escape the Domains of Dread against the will of the Dark Powers?

Experts. Who might have knowledge regarding the nature of Ravenloft? What might the characters do to convince them to share their knowledge?

Methods. What methods might allow the characters to escape? Is powerful magic enough, or does the nature of Ravenloft need to be altered? What costs might such methods have?

Ramifications. Will the characters’ escape only affect them, or will it have ramifications across the Domains of Dread (or the entire multiverse)?

Will. Do the Dark Powers have a role in the characters’ escape? Must characters win these mysterious entities’ favor or find ways to defy them?

Roll on or choose a result from Escape Adventure Seeds table to inspire adventure or campaign plots involving escape from the Domains of Dread.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwEscapeAdvenQq rollable resultLabel="To Escape, the Characters Must …"]

Adventures in Falkovnia.name: Adventures in Falkovnia
Adventures in Falkovnia.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.olQq4Qu3GUvJlTdP inline classes="hide-in-embed"]

Adventures set in Falkovnia involve zombie hordes, cruel soldiers, and Drakov herself. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

Horror in Falkovnia

Adventures in Falkovnia often include the following elements.

Zombie March

Zombies emerge from the Mists on the night of the new moon. This surge brings a recurring threat to besiege the walls of Lekar, but it also makes encountering droves of zombies more likely throughout the domain. Use the zombies from chapter 5, the Monster Manual, or other sources to create surprising and terrifying encounters.

The Falcon’s Talons

Drakov’s military forces are both completely overwhelming and entirely outnumbered. Though relatively few in number, the Talons are organized enough to oppress Falkovnia’s survivors and enforce Drakov’s totalitarian laws. In the face of zombie hordes, though, the Talons are fighting a losing battle. Talon soldiers are @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWarriorInfantr]{Warrior Infantry}, while officers are @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWarriorVeteran]{Warrior Veterans}.

Martial Law

Lekar is a martial dictatorship. Drakov’s troops carry out example-setting impalings as punishment for even the slightest crimes. These executions impose order through terror and make it clear that zombies aren’t the realm’s only monsters.

Only Bad Choices

There is one simple truth in Falkovnia: The dead are going to kill everyone. This sets the stage for countless bad decisions, from leaders making terrible sacrifices or permitting immoral acts to individuals making fatalistic choices. Locals behave as if it’s their last day alive—because it probably is.

Trapped with the Dead

Falkovnia’s borders are usually closed, and Vladeska Drakov has no control over when they open. Anyone who enters the Mists surrounding Falkovnia encounters endless numbers of zombies. Travelers who somehow avoid these shambling corpses emerge from the Mists back in Falkovnia, pursued by the undead. For one week following the night of the new moon, the Mists are empty of zombies and the borders open. During this time, Drakov sends patrols of Talons to sweep the edges of the Mists for deserters. These patrols are difficult to avoid, and battles with them often attract zombies.

Falkovnia Campaign Arc

Falkovnia is one of the few domains where the characters and the Darklord might be on the same side, united against unrelenting hordes of zombies. The characters might try to defend Falkovnia’s people alongside Drakov, but as the Darklord’s obsession with victory becomes clear, they must balance keeping her trust and upholding their principles. A campaign here might follow this outline:

Levels 1–4. The characters save a group of commoners and outmatched Talons from Zombies. The thankful band takes the characters to Lekar, where people hail them as heroic “Trueborn of Falkovnia.” Vladeska Drakov recruits them to bring scattered pockets of common folk to safety in Lekar.

Levels 5–8. The characters witness the suffering in Lekar and see some of those they’ve saved impaled for disloyalty. Rebels entreat them to help smuggle people out of Lekar to hidden encampments in the mountains.

Levels 9–12. The characters are captured by Talons, but Drakov offers them freedom if they defend the city’s southern gate, the Gate of Torches, from the impending zombie siege. Unknown to the characters, Drakov plans to sacrifice the gate as detailed in the “Siege of Lekar” adventure.

Vladeska Drakov.name: Vladeska Drakov
Vladeska Drakov.text:
Darklord Who Wars Against the Dead

General of a demoralized occupying army, Vladeska Drakov rules what little of Falkovnia hasn’t been overrun by the dead. A cunning authoritarian, she believes any tactics—no matter how immoral—are permissible in the pursuit of victory. The Mists torment her by keeping her ever on the edge of defeat, exhausting her and forcing her to watch her precision fail in the face of an endless string of emergencies and an undefeatable enemy horde. Drakov is ever being pushed to the edge. Hunger, sleep deprivation, and the endless demands of terrified troops and civilians drive her toward outbursts of paranoia and violence.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwVladeskaDrako statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Drakov’s Tale

In the world of her birth, Vladeska Drakov was known as the Crimson Falcon, leader of a peerless mercenary army called the Falcon’s Talons. In a land of bitterly feuding royal families, she and her troops executed a thousand ancient grudges and petty atrocities. Drakov amassed a fortune and planned to retire young and buy a title and a realm to rule.

The sacking of Yeivere changed her plans. During that city’s razing, Drakov’s mercenaries went too far. The Falcon’s Talons killed a unique soul during the slaughter. None can say who the soul was—a royal, a saint, a witch, an angel—but with its death, the world turned against them.

The ruling families united to hunt the Talons. Thanks to her tactical genius, Drakov repelled her pursuers and went on the offensive. One by one she attacked her former benefactors, burning villages, pressing citizens into service, and impaling anyone with a drop of noble blood. Over years of work, Drakov forged a bloody empire. But while sacking the burning city of her last remaining foe, Drakov and her troops were engulfed by smoke. When it cleared, everything they knew was gone.

Transported to an unfamiliar land, Drakov and her troops wasted little time subjugating it. This unprepared realm fell quickly, almost willingly. On taking the city of Lekar, Drakov named herself ruler of her new empire: Falkovnia.

However, under a moonless sky, the dead rose against Drakov, claws scraping against Lekar’s walls before alarms could be raised. Drakov’s brilliant and desperate strategies eventually turned back the zombies during the days-long First Siege of Lekar, but victory came at the cost of most of her veteran troops.

Since then, Drakov has nearly lost her nation. Every moon, a new desperate battle unfolds. She prepares ceaselessly for each one, certain of her ultimate victory but doubtful of her troops’ mettle. With threats and public impalings, she keeps her people more terrified of her than of the undead. And with every new moon, she faces an unconquerable foe, never revealing a secret only she knows: that each zombie bears the face of an innocent her soldiers once put to the sword.

Drakov’s Domain

Falkovnia is altered by Drakov’s presence, creating the following effects:

Authoritarian Regime. Drakov’s allies have Advantage on Wisdom saving throws.

Closing the Borders. Drakov can’t close or open the borders of her domain. Falkovnia’s borders are usually closed, but they open one week every month starting on the night of the new moon.

Endless War. Whenever a creature other than Drakov or her allies finishes a Long Rest in the domain, roll [[/r 1d20#On a 1, the creature doesn’t remove any Exhaustion levels as a result of finishing that rest]]. On a 1, the creature doesn’t remove any Exhaustion levels as a result of finishing that rest, and the creature gains 1 &Reference[Exhaustion] level.

If Drakov dies, these effects end immediately.

Drakov’s Lair

When she conquered the city, Vladeska Drakov claimed Lekar’s seat of governance, Custodian’s Hall, as her army’s headquarters. Since the First Siege of Lekar, one emergency after another has forced Drakov and her Talon troops to repurpose the building’s chambers for military purposes.

Custodian’s Hall

The polished marble and hopeful mosaics of Custodian’s Hall now bear the dirt and scars of invaders’ boots and tools of war. The site is depicted on map 2.11 and has the following features:

Chamber of Peers. The chamber of Lekar’s dissolved senate now serves as a command center for Drakov’s officers.

Drakov’s Chambers. Drakov has repurposed offices at the heights of Custodian’s Hall as her personal chambers. Her bedroom is cold and simple and features little more than a hard cot. Nearby, she maintains a war room full of maps and reports where she obsesses over desperate battle scenarios. Across the hall is a well-used chamber full of torture devices, many of her own design.

Plaza Stairs. Daily, Talons disseminate meager rations to Lekar’s citizens while other soldiers execute supposed traitors by impaling them on pikes.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.XpAzpZGHGUseXbqv cite=false classes="hide-in-embed"]

Falkovnia.name: Falkovnia
Falkovnia.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.Nq3PbGPiSlehAV9n]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.z6BkgOPoLaVBo4LQ]

Domain Besieged by the Dead

Darklord: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwVladeskaDrako]{Vladeska Drakov}

Genre: Disaster horror

Hallmarks: Dwindling resources, impending disaster, suspicion, totalitarianism, zombies

Mist Talismans: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwLetterfromLek]{Correspondence} from the capital, Lekar; gear marked with the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwBloodFactionG]{Blood Falcon};
@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwZombieFlesh00]{zombie flesh}

Aligned Tarokka Card: Dictator

Opposed Tarokka Card: Executioner

Falkovnia is a land besieged. Empty countryside surrounds ruined or crumbling cities. Only a few desperate pockets of civilization survive, carrying on not out of hope, but out of fear of the land’s merciless soldiers. Led by General Vladeska Drakov, Falkovnia’s military organizes a desperate and occasionally effective defense against an implacable foe: the ever-growing armies of the dead.

Every moon, a new zombie legion emerges from the Mists. Never appearing from the same place twice, the horde sweeps across the land, drawn to the densest populations of the living. That always includes the Falkovnian capital of Lekar, where unfit and underfed conscripts defend crumbling walls alongside General Drakov and her crimson-armored elite soldiers, the Talons. Causalities mount during the zombie sieges, but miracles and moments of desperate valor haven’t abandoned Falkovnia. The people’s numbers dwindle, but they soldier on.

In the aftermath of a zombie attack, the Falkovnians burn their dead, repair what they can, and whisper that now might be the time to flee the city. Invariably, someone speaks too loudly, and soldiers drag off the so-called traitor to be impaled as an example. The people might want to abandon their homes, but Vladeska Drakov refuses to accept defeat.

Domain Features

Falkovnia is known for the following features:

Army of the Dead. Every night of the new moon, thousands of ravenous zombies emerge from the Mists to feed on the living.

Capital Punishment. There is only one punishment for any crime in Falkovnia: impalement.

Rotten Remnants. Ruins cover Falkovnia—towns and villages crushed by zombie hordes and haunted by undead stragglers.

Talon Soldiers. Falkovnian soldiers known as Talons patrol the land, hunting for deserters and strangers to press into service. Talon officers bear prominent tattoos of the Blood Falcon, the symbol of Vladeska Drakov.

Locations in Falkovnia

Falkovnia is an unnervingly still domain, where people and animals hide to avoid attracting the attention of zombies and Drakov’s brutal Talons. The city of Lekar is described below, and other notable locations are summarized on the Falkovnia Locations table.

Lekar

The only city of the living left in Falkovnia, Lekar bears the brunt of the zombie invasion. The city has outgrown its walls multiple times since its founding, leaving its districts divided by fortifications. During the first zombie siege, General Drakov sacrificed the northern River Ward, leaving hundreds of souls to be slaughtered but allowing the remaining defenders to turn back the zombie horde.

Now the city’s survivors face harsh conditions. Talon soldiers keep the peace in the cramped slums. Troops lead civilians outside the walls to scavenge from ruins and work the fields of abandoned farms, but their harvests are never enough for the city’s survivors. Though bitter and desperate, soldiers live better than civilians, assured of rations and housing in the Bastion Ward—the Talons-only district around General Drakov’s command center at Custodian’s Hall. From there, Drakov orders daily public executions as conscripts dispense supplies to the suffering masses.

General of a demoralized occupying army, Vladeska Drakov rules what little of Falkovnia hasn’t been overrun by the dead. A cunning authoritarian, she believes any tactics—no matter how immoral—are permissible in the pursuit of victory. The Mists torment her by keeping her ever on the edge of defeat, exhausting her and forcing her to watch her precision fail in the face of an endless string of emergencies and an undefeatable enemy horde. Drakov is ever being pushed to the edge. Hunger, sleep deprivation, and the endless demands of terrified troops and civilians drive her toward outbursts of paranoia and violence.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.Il0wfdxqH7V0lwC4 cite=false]

Falkovnia Locations

Location

Description

Aerie

Swarms of zombified birds of prey attack any living creatures near the village of Aerie, especially those who use the cable ferries crossing the Archer River.

Delmunster

Zombies avoid the idyllic village of Delmunster. People say that it is protected by a fallen star; Few suspect the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwBodytakerPlan]{Bodytaker Plant} (see chapter 5) that has claimed the community.

Fort Watten

A force of Talons under the command of Major Olana Crepeau was ordered to guard Falkovnia’s southern border at Fort Watten. No one in Lekar has heard from them for moons.

Hatzimvas
Hospital

The doctors of the secluded Hatzimvas Hospital seek a medical or supernatural cure to zombification. Mysteriously, Drakov allows the hospital to work unimpeded.

Morfenzi

The ruins of Morfenzi hide a Talon outpost. Here the amoral scientist Vjorn Horstman conducts monstrous experiments, creating living weapons using “primal serum.”

Silbervas

Fractious bands of survivors sworn to silence scavenge the ruined city of Silbervas. They avoid the ominously lit Dekovan Palace, which overlooks the ruins.

Stangengrad

The silent, scarred spirits of commoners and Talons haunt the ruined fortress-city of Stangengrad. Vladeska forbids entry to the city so no one can learn of the atrocity that took place there.

Stonespine Mountains

The knight Gondegal leads rebels opposed to the Talons. Her stronghold is said to be hidden in the Stonespine Mountains, but none who join Gondegal are ever seen again.

Three Traitors

Between the three lakes known as the Three Traitors is a muddy patch where Vistani often camp. These travelers have a knack for avoiding Talon patrols.

Vigila Forest

In the dense Vigila Forest stand towering black trees called Sentries of Death. Tales say the wood is haunted and these trees’ roots reach into the realm of the dead.

Willow Watch

The hastily constructed Willow Watch tower was built on the ruins of a church of Ezra. The Talons here report strange lights in the nearby forest and sounds from the catacombs below.

Siege of Lekar.name: Siege of Lekar
Siege of Lekar.text:
@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.adventures.Adventure.2f4mJQgTdqJiLGcs]{Adventure for Level 9 Characters}

Situation. Zombie hordes attack Lekar. Vladeska Drakov seeks to defeat them by sacrificing the characters and a district of Lekar.

Hook. The characters are ordered by Drakov or entreated by citizens to defend the Gate of Torches.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.maZrMHPwzWoHehnb cite=false]

Lekar Gates

This adventure takes place at a gate in Lekar’s southern wall, the Gate of Torches, and a second gate to Lekar’s central district, Loyalist’s Gate. The Gate of Torches shows the scars of countless zombie claws, but otherwise the gates are identical. Both have the following features:

Fortifications. The walls of Lekar are 30 feet tall, with the gatehouse’s towers rising 40 feet. Crenelations atop both provide defenders with Three-Quarters Cover from beyond the wall.

Gates. The gatehouse’s @UUID[Actor.gqMfTmOsFqQLDrf3]{battle-scarred gates} are Huge objects with AC 19, 72 HP, and Immunity to Poison and Psychic damage. Any character adjacent to the mechanism over the gate can open or close the gate in 1 round.

Encounters

These encounters begin at the Gate of Torches, which uses @UUID[Scene.02OLCd82tzGjPVru]{map 2.12.} Characters can position themselves where they please. Describe inexperienced soldiers guarding other posts or zombies beyond the map, but don’t track their actions in battle. When you’re ready to begin the siege, the Siege Events by Round table outlines what occurs each round. The following sections further detail these events.

Siege Events by Round

Round

Siege Event

1

@UUID[Actor.4Vgt8cQO2Evz93VL]{Encounter:} Eight @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmZombie00000000]{Zombies} and six @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmOgreZombie0000]{Ogre Zombies} appear.

3

@UUID[Actor.4FDR39eAcw4H1zWt]{Encounter:} Four @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmOgreZombie0000]{Ogre Zombies} and a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmBeholderZombie]{Beholder Zombie} appear.

5

@UUID[Actor.NyYcZqaDbukPus9L]{Encounter A:} Two @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwZombieClot000]{Zombie Clots} (see chapter 5) appear.

@UUID[Actor.Vi54pj3Ju9NFYg27]{Encounter B:} Talons roll a wagon to the gate from the north.

6

[[/item Actor.WzumJ3Hgvb7Yrgvd.Item.yu0IWKKlshAPbWbf]]{Event:} Talons light fuses on the wagon.

7

@UUID[Actor.3gFA0MBfpe0mH5c2]{Encounter:} Four @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmOgreZombie0000]{Ogre Zombies} appear. Talons flee.

8

[[/item Actor.WzumJ3Hgvb7Yrgvd.Item.ewceC5BYmy3M1l4y]]{Event:} Wagon explodes (if not prevented).

9

Explosion (see “Siege Climax”).

Zombie Attack. Zombies shamble from the south and attack the gatehouse’s gates. Rather than having foes make individual attacks on the gates, assume the gates take [[/damage 4d6]] damage every time a round ends with a zombie adjacent to them. When reduced to 0 Hit Points, the gate is destroyed. If this occurs, troops flee as detailed in “Siege Climax.”

Drakov’s Talons. Starting on round 5, any character on or behind the walls who succeeds on a [[/check prc dc=12 format=long]] notices @UUID[Actor.8uXizdyiHCyGbDO6]{Talons} (four @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWarriorVeteran]{Warrior Veterans}) behind the gate. The Talons are supposedly allied troops, so this shouldn’t cause concern. The Talons behave as detailed on the Siege Events by Round table. They flee if fought.

Talon Wagon. This wagon contains kegs of gunpowder. A character adjacent to the wagon who spends a round investigating discovers these explosives. On round 6, any character who has noticed the Talons and is within sight of them sees them setting fuses. If no one prevents the soldiers from lighting the fuses or extinguishes the lit fuses, the wagon explodes at the end of round 8. Creatures within 30 feet of it when it explodes make a [[/save dex 12 format=long]], taking [[/damage 12d6 fire average]] damage on a failed save or half as much damage on a successful one. The explosion destroys the gatehouse’s gate, regardless of its remaining Hit Points.

Siege Climax. The siege continues until either the gatehouse’s gate is destroyed or the end of round 9. At the end of round 9, an explosion hundreds of feet to the west destroys part of the city wall, and @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmZombie00000000]{Zombies} pour into Lekar. Surviving soldiers abandon their posts—it’s clear the Gate of Torches will be overrun. These survivors race through the slums to Loyalist’s Gate.

Loyalist’s Gate. Loyalist’s Gate is identical to the Gate of Torches and uses @UUID[Scene.1SdGuh3FOSOEdGvV]{map 2.12: Lekar Gate.} When the characters arrive from the south, the gate is closed. Hundreds of surviving soldiers and slum residents scream for the guards to let them through. Talon soldiers (eight @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWarriorInfantr]{Warrior Infantry}) and @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwVladeskaDrako]{Vladeska Drakov} (see earlier in this chapter) guard the gate. Drakov thanks the panicking assemblage for their sacrifice and reveals her plan to set the slums ablaze once the zombies flood in. The characters can open the gate to admit the crowd using the mechanism in the gatehouse, but Drakov and her troops fight to prevent this.

The characters have 4 rounds before @UUID[Actor.is2AQmWDsF7gF6Fy]{groups} of [[/r 1d6#The number of zombies that appear]] @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmZombie00000000]{Zombies} begin appearing from the south at the end of each round. Narrate these zombies attacking the crowd, but don’t track the crowd’s Hit Points. If the characters open the gate before the zombies appear, they earn [[/award 5900xp each]].

Drakov’s Defeat. If Drakov is defeated, Falkovnia’s borders open, and no zombies appear from the Mists for several moons. Some people rebuild, others flee into the Mists. After this time, though, Drakov reappears and slowly retakes control of Falkovnia. The borders close, and zombies return soon afterward.

Feats.name: Atuty
Feats.text: This section presents new feats tailored for characters adventuring through Ravenloft. Included are a handful of new feats in the Origin category, as well as a new type of feat: Dark Gift feats, which represent sinister bargains a character might make with mysterious forces.

Origin Feats.name: Atuty pochodzenia

These feats are in the Origin category.

Dark Gifts.name: Dark Gifts
Dark Gifts.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.FGS8lPtklO23cc6R inline classes="float-right size-three"]

The Dark Powers influence many who struggle within their clutches, tempting both the innocent and the ambitious with whispered promises. These sinister bargains come in dreams or mysterious visions. Their terms are always clear and their prices terrible.

With the DM’s permission, whenever you would gain a feat from the Origin category, you can instead gain a Dark Gift feat. A DM can bestow a Dark Gift feat on a character during the course of an adventure, representing an insidious deal with a Darklord or some other malevolent force.

Body Horror.name: Body Horror
Body Horror.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.7xr7eTNBIhlegiKz classes="float-right size-three" inline]

Body horror examines the fear of our failing anatomies. Tales in this genre explore the unpredictability of flesh—that robust health might be an illusion and that we might be incubating what will devour us. Examples include Alien and The Fly.

Body Horror Principles

Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains with body horror themes:

Depth of Transformation. Physical transformations are common and might affect more than bodies. Objects, architecture, and the natural world might be also anthropomorphized.

Flesh Aberrations. Characters suffer aberrant or excessive growth, whether of organs, discordant appendages, or unnatural materials.

Gore Is a Byproduct. Though gore might take center stage in body horror stories, it’s often a side effect of stories about fear of change, difference, mortality, physical harm, or disease.

Gore Isn’t Required. Body horror doesn’t need to be messy. Swapping bodies, turning to stone, or gaining one’s ideal form might feature in this genre.

Real Disabilities Aren’t Horrifying. Body horror has a history of portraying disability as monstrous. Avoid this trope.

Body Horror Villains

A physical transformation doesn’t make someone a villain. Villains in body horror adventures cultivate evil in tandem with the aberrant transformations afflicting their form and surroundings.

Villain Identities

Villains of this genre are often tragic figures, whether they’re cultists enduring forced metamorphosis or scientists trying to extend their lives. Choose from or roll on the Body Horror Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwBodyHorrorVGs rollable resultLabel="The Villain Is …"]

Villain Torments

Villains in this genre simultaneously draw power from and are tormented by supernatural and physiological aberrance. Choose from or roll on the Body Horror Torments table to determine what abhorrent affliction plagues the villain.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwBodyHorrorTFQ rollable resultLabel="The Villain …"]

Body Horror Adventures

Adventures in body horror realms often become tests of endurance, whether characters are attempting to hold out until rescue arrives or endeavoring to mount a rescue themselves. Alternatively, adventures might have an investigative feel, requiring the heroes to uncover the dark secret that lies beneath the mundane.

Adventure Hooks

Body horror plots often explore the unpredictability of life and discordance between mind and body. Choose from or roll on the Body Horror Plots table to inspire your body horror adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwBodyHorrorPG8 rollable resultLabel="Adventure Plots"]

Adventure Settings

Body horror can occur anywhere, from mundane settings to unsettling locales. Choose from or roll on the Body Horror Settings table to inspire locations for your adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwBodyHorrorSb9 rollable resultLabel="The Adventure Takes Place In …"]

Body Horror Monsters

Any monster might become the focus of a body horror plot. Know what your players anticipate so you can distort those expectations. A poised elf revealed to be a husk puppeted by sapient worms can prove as frightening as any eldritch horror. The Body Horror Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the Monster Manual.

Cosmic Horror.name: Cosmic Horror
Cosmic Horror.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.dKB3alY6qwWClybG classes="float-right size-two" inline]

Cosmic horror revolves around the fear of personal insignificance. The genre, inspired by stories of the Cthulhu Mythos, is predicated on the idea of entities so vast and so genuinely beyond our comprehension that we can’t fathom their simplest motivations. To see them is to become lost in their magnitude and the evidence that we have never and could never matter to the cosmos at large. Examples include Annihilation and Stalker.

Cosmic Horror Principles

Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains with cosmic horror themes:

Fear the Unknown. Be vague. Cosmic horror emerges through imagination and the indescribable, not through details.

Iconography. Cults, forbidden books, and strange symbols form the cornerstones of cosmic horror.

Inscrutable Forces. Cosmic horror is about alien forces driven by motivations humans can’t understand. There is no good or evil, no law or chaos.

Marginalization Isn’t Horror. The genre has a history of framing marginalized demographics as monstrous and stigmatizing mental illness. Avoid those tropes.

Remove the Familiar. Cosmic horror makes characters feel gradually unmoored from reality.

Cosmic Horror Villains

Despite the genre’s grand magnitude, villains of cosmic horror are still often individual beings. Godlike terrors and inscrutable forces might pull the strings with their unknowable goals, but the antagonists the characters face are concrete, fallible people.

Villain Identities

Cosmic horror villains often perpetrate horrors as they strive for outcomes they can’t articulate. Choose from or roll on the Cosmic Horror Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwCosmicHorroDN rollable resultLabel="The Villain Is …"]{Cosmic Horror Villains}

Villain Torments

Villains in this genre are endowed with unnatural powers stemming from their studies or granted by alien forces. Choose from or roll on the Cosmic Horror Torments table to determine what alien or psychological torment plagues the villain.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwCosmicHorrotB rollable resultLabel="The Villain …"]{Cosmic Horror Torments}

Cosmic Horror Adventures

Adventures in cosmic horror realms are bleak, desperate affairs, where the best one can hope for is to survive relatively intact. There is no stopping the ultimate evil, but characters might aspire to seal it away temporarily.

Adventure Hooks

Cosmic horror adventures often deal with how alien forces might alter us, perverting our expectations and separating us from what makes us human. Choose from or roll on the Cosmic Horror Plots table to inspire your cosmic horror adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwCosmicHorro37 rollable resultLabel="Adventure Hook"]{Cosmic Horror Plots}

Adventure Settings

Cosmic horror frequently takes place in settings that imply access to hidden knowledge or in unnatural, logic-defying dimensions far removed from reality. Choose from or roll on Cosmic Horror Settings table to inspire locations for your adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwCosmicHorroLT rollable resultLabel="The Adventure Takes Place In …"]{Cosmic Horror Settings}

Cosmic Horror Monsters

Monsters in cosmic horror adventures are often strange beings that enact bizarre changes on their victims. The Cosmic Horror Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the Monster Manual.

Dark Fantasy.name: Dark Fantasy
Dark Fantasy.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.XSRYrLRPyEqCIsuB classes="float-right size-three" inline]

The dark fantasy genre features fantasy worlds where grim themes, nihilistic plots, or horrifying elements inform their stories. Any tale featuring both supernatural elements and horror themes might constitute dark fantasy, whether it’s a fantasy story steeped in dread or an otherwise realistic world that features a supernatural terror. Examples include The Dark Tower and Pan’s Labyrinth.

Dark Fantasy Principles

Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains with dark fantasy themes:

Evil Sometimes Triumphs. Good doesn’t always win. Evil individuals with great power and unopposed schemes might prosper.

Low Magic. Magic and magic items might be rarer or require a bargain or sacrifice to use.

Morally Gray. The lines between good and evil are blurred. Choices involve deciding on the least bad outcome available.

Nefarious Deeds. Corruption and suspicion flourish among organizations and individuals.

Reluctant Heroes. Antiheroes are common—characters tainted by the world’s evil or those who refuse to be considered heroes.

Dark Fantasy Villains

Sinister individuals leave their marks on dark fantasy domains, whether with oppressive architecture, unavoidable propaganda, or scars on an exploited environment.

Villain Identities

Villains occupy places of influence in dark fantasy domains. Choose from or roll on the Dark Fantasy Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwDarkFantasyX5 rollable resultLabel="The Villain …"]{Dark Fantasy Villains}

Villain Torments

In dark fantasy adventures, a villain’s power is often rooted in the villain’s own suffering. Choose from or roll on the Dark Fantasy Torments table to determine how this power tragically works against the villain.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwDarkFantasy3D rollable resultLabel="The Villain …"]{Dark Fantasy Torments}

Dark Fantasy Adventures

Adventures in dark fantasy domains involve desperate or reluctant heroes resisting evil. Even in the grimmest settings, desperation and hope fan the flames of resistance.

Adventure Hooks

Plots in dark fantasy adventures range from street-level depravity to world-ruining conspiracies. Choose from or roll on the Dark Fantasy Plots table to inspire your dark fantasy adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwDarkFantasyIC rollable resultLabel="Adventure Hook"]{Dark Fantasy Plots}

Adventure Settings

Evil is entrenched in dark fantasy domains, reflected by their grandiose adventure locales. Choose from or roll on the Dark Fantasy Settings table to inspire locations for your adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwDarkFantasyhU rollable resultLabel="The Adventure Takes Place In …"]{Dark Fantasy Settings}

Dark Fantasy Monsters

Any monster can find a place in a dark fantasy plot. D&D’s most iconic threats—such as dragons and beholders—are particularly appropriate to horror-tinged tales, as are any other supernatural foes. The Dark Fantasy Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the Monster Manual.

Dark Fantasy Monsters

CR

Folk Horror.name: Folk Horror
Folk Horror.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.RUKE3JO8rtmSGRtJ classes="float-right size-three" inline]

Folk horror adventures involve traditions, beliefs, and perceptions that are passed down through generations and take terrifying twists. For those who ascribe to hidden traditions, sacrifices to strange gods or placations to lurking monsters are everyday events. For outsiders, though, these practices reveal the subjectivity of normalcy and societal truth. Examples include Midsommar and The Wicker Man.

Folk Horror Principles

Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains with folk horror themes:

Isolation Begets Weirdness. Strange and potentially dangerous traditions flourish in isolated or private communities.

Perversion of Community. Communities in folk horror stories often are a grim mirror of an aspect of accepted society. They often dramatically use outsiders or purge nonbelievers.

Relative Truth. Beliefs highlighted in folk horror stories might not be true.

Secret Traditions. Community members typically hide their beliefs, perhaps by physically obscuring them or manipulating others in power.

Strangeness in Specificity. Art, symbols, tools, celebrations, and other trappings of belief make a community’s traditions more specific and eerie.

Folk Horror Villains

Antagonistic forces in folk horror usually are community leaders—paragons of the strange beliefs central to the horror’s narrative. Outsiders might have a chance to adopt the community’s ways, but otherwise locals consider them heretics or corrupting elements.

Villain Identities

Folk horror villains are manipulative, leading others to follow inscrutable traditions. Choose from or roll on the Folk Horror Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwFolkHorrorVuZ rollable resultLabel="The Villain Is …"]{Folk Horror Villains}

Villain Torments

Folk horror villains have been consumed by the traditions or land they embody, causing them to fail in their duties or their rites to spin out of control. Choose from or roll on the Folk Horror Torments
to determine how these twisted traditions torment the villain.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwFolkHorrorTG2 rollable resultLabel="The Villain …"]{Folk Horror Torments}

Folk Horror Adventures

In folk horror tales, characters often discover that their beliefs aren’t as universally held as they assumed—and provide no defense against those who reject those beliefs.

Adventure Hooks

Folk horror plots often involve outsiders to a group discovering unsettling practices or traditions. Choose from or roll on the Folk Horror Plots to inspire your folk horror adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwFolkHorrorP3Z rollable resultLabel="Adventure Hook"]{Folk Horror Plots}

Adventure Settings

Folk horror stories often take place in isolated or rural areas, but they could be set anywhere insular communities thrive or traditions stagnate. Choose from or roll on the Folk Horror Settings to inspire locations for your adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwFolkHorrorS4U rollable resultLabel="The Adventure Takes Place In …"]{Folk Horror Settings}

Folk Horror Monsters

Eerie traditions and unnatural alliances with monsters fill folk horror tales. When certain behaviors have taken hold over generations, even the strangest practices and most dangerous beings might be accepted as part of society. The Folk Horror Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the Monster Manual.
