Ghost Stories.name: Ghost Stories
Ghost Stories.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.Ig5lafZSeawr3T9v classes="float-right size-three" inline]

Ghost stories number among the more psychologically elaborate genres of horror. Only by revealing tales of tragedy and past wrongs can heroes bring peace to forces that share their suffering with the living. Examples include The Woman in Black and The Haunting of Hill House.

Ghost Story Principles

Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains inspired by ghost stories:

Mirror of Humanity. Ghosts are a way to examine the nature of the soul, mortality, and history.

Naive Heroes. Heroes are purehearted or unsuspecting individuals whose resolve is shaken by the story’s events. Look for ways to test heroes’ psychology with your hauntings.

Resolving a Haunt. Heroes need a way to put spirits to rest. Once you reveal the story, ensure the way to combat the haunting is clear.

Specificity. Even the smallest details can reveal a haunting’s story. A simple locket or portrait might contain clues that explain a haunting.

Ties to the Living. Personal ties give ghost stories weight. Consider connecting the heroes to spirits in ways they won’t predict, such as making a phantasmal villain a hero’s ancestor.

Ghost Story Villains

In ghost stories, spirits often represent instruments of supernatural justice, causing those responsible for their deaths to suffer. Spirits also can represent grief and the need for closure.

Villain Identities

While the villains in ghost stories are often spirits or haunted places, they also could be the individuals who provoked a haunting to begin with. Choose from or roll on the Ghost Story Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwGhostStoryVgP rollable resultLabel="The Villain Is …"]{Ghost Story Villains}

Villain Torments

Whether they’re living monsters or ghosts, villains in ghost stories are the architects of their own tragedies. Choose from or roll on the Ghost Story Torments table to determine how the villain’s past painfully manifests.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwGhostStoryTyQ rollable resultLabel="The Villain …"]{Ghost Story Torments}

Ghost Story Adventures

Ghost story adventures deal with learning the story behind a haunting and ultimately resolving it. They benefit from preserving the mystery behind a spirit’s motivations until the heroes discover the truth.

Adventure Hooks

Though the structure—learn about a haunting and resolve it—might seem straightforward, ghost story plots can explore philosophical issues like the burden of ancestry and the nature of the soul. Choose from or roll on the Ghost Story Plots table to inspire your ghost stories.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwGhostStoryPdX rollable resultLabel="Adventure Hook"]{Ghost Story Plots}

Adventure Settings

Ghost stories are intensely personal, and adventures within this genre take place in a setting dripping with tragic history. Choose from or roll on the Ghost Story Settings table to inspire locations for your adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwGhostStorySGa rollable resultLabel="The Adventure Takes Place In …"]{Ghost Story Settings}

Ghost Story Monsters

Creatures that embody or serve as a response to past tragedies often feature in ghost stories. Don’t limit yourself to incorporeal Undead when creating your own hauntings. The Ghost Story Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the Monster Manual.

Gothic Horror.name: Gothic Horror
Gothic Horror.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.cjYZFFDDs5TYhAAt classes="float-right size-three" inline]

Gothic horror deals with the terror within. It shatters the illusion of humanity by holding a mirror up to us and forcing us to examine what we truly are and what we pretend to be. In gothic horror, we—not some distant entity—are the architects of our destruction. Examples include Dracula and Frankenstein.

Gothic Horror Principles

Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains with gothic horror themes:

Brave Heroes. Gothic heroes are often virtuous, deeply passionate, or courageous. Adventures should test characters’ beliefs and morality.

Foreboding. Atmosphere and a sense of dread are key. Locations are often decrepit or foreboding.

High Emotions. Gothic stories include intense, exaggerated emotions. Romances, rivalries, and life-changing events are common.

Sacrifice. Sacrifices feature prominently in gothic stories. Give characters heart-wrenching choices.

Unassuming, Unrepentant Villains. Gothic villains are unrepentantly evil, but this shouldn’t be immediately obvious. Drop hints about your villain’s awful secrets before revealing them fully.

Gothic Horror Villains

Though thoroughly wicked, villains in gothic horror tales act reserved or unassuming until they reveal their true nature. Any intriguing figure with a dark secret can serve as a gothic horror villain.

Villain Identities

Gothic villains are most effective when they are slowly revealed as shockingly cruel, immoral, or the antithesis of good. Choose from or roll on the Gothic Horror Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwGothicHorroSh rollable resultLabel="The Villain Is …"]{Gothic Horror Villains}

Villain Torments

Self-debasement and self-loathing lurk at the heart of gothic villains’ evil, whether they indulge in vices or are consumed with misanthropy. Choose from or roll on the Gothic Horror Torments table to determine how temptation and dramatic emotions torment the villain.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwGothicHorroJy rollable resultLabel="The Villain …"]{Gothic Horror Torments}

Gothic Horror Adventures

The themes of gothic horror adventures center around shattering the lies we trick ourselves into believing. Ominous history, poetic tragedy, and supernatural forces are staples of gothic horror.

Adventure Hooks

The dichotomy between goodness and evil, reckonings for wicked deeds, and dire choices are strong themes in gothic horror plots. Choose from or roll on the Gothic Horror Plots table to inspire your gothic horror adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwGothicHorrolG rollable resultLabel="Adventure Hook"]{Gothic Horror Plots}

Adventure Settings

Gothic horror settings have an underlying sinister air, and encounters often take place in forlorn structures where depravity runs rampant. Choose from or roll on the Gothic Horror Settings table to inspire locations for your adventures.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwGothicHorroli rollable resultLabel="The Adventure Takes Place In …"]{Gothic Horror Settings}

Gothic Horror Monsters

Creatures imbued with tragedy or abominable origins work well in gothic horror adventures. The Gothic Horror Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the Monster Manual.

Disaster Horror.name: Disaster Horror
Disaster Horror.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.KB6JVYpGB6J8KD8E classes="float-right size-three" inline]

In disaster horror, the world has fallen to ruin or it’s getting there fast. The unthinkable has happened, and society is collapsing. In these tales, you might replace a monster or villain with cascading catastrophes like those brought about by a meteorite strike or volcanic eruption. Regardless of a hero’s power, the best-timed strike or the most powerful spell might not fix a city ruined by an earthquake or a world ravaged by plagues.

Disaster Horror Features

Consider the following principles when creating adventures with disaster horror themes:

Nature of Disaster. The disasters in your horror adventures need not be natural. Magical disruptions, temporal shifts, and violent interplanar rifts might sow supernatural chaos. Wars and relentless monster hordes—including legions of zombies or demons—also can cause domain-changing ruin.

Society’s Downfall. In disaster horror, society breaks down. Inns are closed, healers are few or overtaxed, and anarchy rises as governments crumble.

What It Takes to Survive. Scared innocents, opportunistic scavengers, and leaderless institutions (military or otherwise) seek to survive. The hopeless acts of others might prove more horrifying than the disaster.

Occult Detective Stories.name: Occult Detective Stories
Occult Detective Stories.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.juXukX58SEbTgDlK classes="float-right size-three" inline]

Occult detective stories straddle the line between fantasy, mystery, and horror. For the occult detective, solving paranormal mysteries is part of the job. In these adventures, supernatural criminals or monsters pursue nefarious ends while the characters act as detectives: interpreting events, seeking patterns, deducing goals, and ultimately putting an end to the threat. Investigation and cleverness take center stage in these adventures, although—as in the noir stories that inspire the genre—there’s plenty of room for action.

Occult Detective Story Features

Consider the following principles when creating adventures with occult detective themes:

Lighter Frights. Occult detective adventures can be just as terrifying as other horror adventures but might be lighter in tone. Adventurers might encounter the results of horrific deeds rather than experiencing them firsthand and might face a foe only once they’re prepared.

Supernatural Twists. Consider how monsters, magic, or other supernatural forces might lend a thrilling twist to criminal enterprises. How will the characters catch an invisible assassin or a thief who pilfers extradimensional spaces?

Threading Hints. Once you know the villain’s mode of operation, consider how the villain might give themself away. Even if you’re not sure, listen to the characters’ plans and give them opportunities to feel brilliant in the course of their investigations.

Psychological Horror.name: Psychological Horror
Psychological Horror.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.LXw2wwjjKhE9cw79 classes="float-right size-three" inline]

Psychological horror stories create suspense by heightening or calling into question characters’ states of mind, emotions, and perceptions. These tales often highlight the difference between what characters think and how they behave.

Psychological Horror Features

Consider the following principles when creating adventures with psychological horror themes:

Fears as Metaphors. Common anxieties and fears can become metaphors for antagonists in psychological horror. Fear of judgment by one’s peers might manifest as a jury of nothics, while fear of change could be represented by a medusa that petrifies those who threaten the status quo.

Mental Illness Isn’t Horror. Some classic psychological horror tales stigmatize mental illness. Avoid this trope in your adventures.

Unreliable Information. Uncertainty and paranoia shape psychological horror stories. Characters might experience a deadly encounter only to wake up at the end, not having realized they were asleep. Before undermining characters’ senses, identify what’s driving these skewed perceptions and how characters might overcome them.

Slasher Horror.name: Slasher Horror
Slasher Horror.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.FWcTCiOmxxLiPIJP classes="float-right size-three" inline]

Slasher horror contends with relentless killers, which might be people or monsters. These adventures revel in grisly details, the suspense of an impending showdown with a powerful foe, and the fear that death waits right around the corner.

Slasher Horror Features

Consider the following principles when creating adventures with slasher horror themes:

Solo Antagonist. Slasher horror stories typically include one major antagonist that threatens a group. This might be a particularly large or cunning beast, a murderer who terrorizes a neighborhood, a monster stalking a town, or a supernatural menace that spreads a signature cause of death.

Targets. Consider who the villain’s targets are and why the villain is preying on that group. Is it out of hunger or for revenge, or does the creature have a deep-seated need to kill?

Violence and Infamy. Antagonists in slasher horror are often infamous, akin to urban legends, with numerous victims. Consider how the killer avoids capture and what makes the killer’s actions warrant their infamy.

Genres of Horror.name: Genres of Horror
Genres of Horror.text: The following sections provide overviews of several genres of horror. Use these to inspire adventures, Darklords, and Domains of Dread of your own creation or to further detail the domains presented in chapter 2. Mix and match elements from different genres to create your own uniquely terrifying combinations.

Ankhtepot.name: Ankhtepot
Ankhtepot.text:
Darklord Who Pursues Death

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.HARM68j3eJf6VX1m cite=false]

Ankhtepot has known treachery and conquest. He has known divinity and rule. But now he knows simply boredom and despair. He desires only to recover his lost ka, which he knows lies hidden in Har’Akir. With it, he hopes to become mortal again, die, and face his gods’ judgment. Whether this means peace or oblivion matters not to him. Ankhtepot seeks only an end, and he and his followers will be a plague on Har’Akir until he finds it.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwAnkhtepot0000 statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Ankhtepot’s Tale

In a nation called the Land of Reeds and Lotuses, Ankhtepot served the royal family as high priest. As old pharaohs died and new ones rose, the influential priest came to believe that the gods wanted a wiser leader to rule. On the day of a new pharaoh’s ascension, Ankhtepot rallied his priests and murdered their liege. But he had misjudged the people’s will, and an outraged mob rose up and executed the traitorous priests.

Moreover, Ankhtepot had misjudged the will of the gods. As he stood before them in death, the immortals forsook him, cursing him and denying him entry to the afterlife. They returned him to the world but stripped away a piece of his soul, his ka—the vital essence that inspires all living beings.

Ankhtepot awoke, trapped within his corpse, as he was mummified along with his treacherous followers. Untold centuries passed, but on the day the last memory of Ankhtepot faded from the Land of Reeds and Lotuses, the high priest’s bindings rotted away, and Ankhtepot crawled forth from his sarcophagus. He emerged from his tomb in an unfamiliar land: Har’Akir.

In this new land, Ankhtepot found a pious people devoted to the same gods he once served. Over generations, he wiped out that religion, replacing it with false divinities of his own imagining for whom he alone could speak. Ankhtepot also resurrected the priests buried alongside him as mummy lords, replacing their heads with those of beasts and monsters holy to his new faith. These Children of Ankhtepot served him as they did in life, and together the dead conquered the souls of Har’Akir.

In the ages since, Ankhtepot hasn’t forged the land of his ambitions. His servants scheme against him, his people don’t flourish, and his monuments crumble to dust in mere centuries. Ankhtepot’s ambitions have withered. Now he seeks only to reclaim his soul and use it to put an end to his accursed immortality.

Ankhtepot’s Domain

Har’Akir is altered by Ankhtepot’s presence, creating the following effects:

Blistering Sun. Any outdoor area of Bright Light in Har’Akir is an area of extreme heat (see the Dungeon Master’s Guide).

Closing the Borders. While in his domain, Ankhtepot can close or open the borders to Har’Akir at will. While the borders are closed, whirling sands fill the Mists surrounding Har’Akir. A creature that enters the Mists for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there takes [[/damage 2d6 type=slashing]].

Control Undead. Undead of CR 5 or lower in Har’Akir have the Charmed condition and obey Ankhtepot’s orders.

If Ankhtepot dies, these effects end immediately.

Ankhtepot’s Lair

A mountain-size black pyramid, Ankhtepot’s tomb—called Pharaoh’s Rest—looms over the City of the Dead. It can be seen from all across Har’Akir.

Pharaoh’s Rest Features

Ancient and ornate, Pharaoh’s Rest holds countless carvings of mysterious deities and Ankhtepot as an idealized pharaoh. A labyrinth of trapped tombs, embalming rooms, and treasure chambers fills the majority of this massive pyramid. Broad paths used by Ankhtepot’s servants lead past deadly side passages to the heights of the monument. Prominent locations are depicted on map 2.14 and have the following features:

Ankhtepot’s Tomb. The burial treasures and reliefs lining Ankhtepot’s tomb’s walls are shattered, having suffered ages of Ankhtepot’s frustration. Hidden behind a bejeweled throne is a thoroughly defaced sarcophagus. Within, Ankhtepot awaits word of his ka’s discovery, rising only when disturbed by trespassers or at the call of servants from the funeral barge chamber below.

Funeral Barge. Ankhtepot envisions his trip to the afterlife taking place on a river. In an ornate ritual site, he has prepared a funeral barge and other ceremonial trappings necessary for his journey after his ka’s recovery.

Lesser Tombs. Throughout the pyramid are shrines, mummification chambers, and the crypts of Ankhtepot’s servants. These include the burial chambers of several Children of Ankhtepot and the Darklord’s son, Thutepot.

Shrine of Ankhtepot. A vast, open shrine holds ominous statues of Har’Akir’s gods and columns etched with exaggerated and fictitious events from Ankhtepot’s rule. On the night of the full moon, a hidden magical elevator activates, granting access to the tombs above.

Har’Akir.name: Har’Akir
Har’Akir.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.u2aJvvPtCWIXkhId]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.FjAusthr6x0O8vor]

Domain of the Ancient Dead

Darklord: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwAnkhtepot0000]{Ankhtepot}

Genre: Dark fantasy

Hallmarks: Ancient tombs, desert perils, lost gods, mummies

Mist Talismans: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwCanopicJar000]{Canopic jar}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwLapisLazuliSc]{lapis lazuli scarab}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwScrollofHiero]{scroll of hieroglyphics}

Aligned Tarokka Card: Monk

Opposed Tarokka Card: Raven

Untold generations of tombs and monuments choke on the obsidian sands of the desert domain of Har’Akir. Here, past splendors lie forgotten, and the living bow in service to the mummified god-king, Ankhtepot.

From a golden pyramid in the City of the Dead, Ankhtepot reigns as speaker for the gods and immortal pharaoh. The deathless ruler cares nothing for his domain or mortal concerns. Rather, he relentlessly sends servants in search of his one desire: his ka, the missing piece of his fractured soul.

As the pharaoh obsesses over his lost soul, his servants plague the domain in his name. In hidden tomb-courts, mummies known as the Children of Ankhtepot dominate cults as emissaries of mysterious gods. And in the mud-brick city of Muhar, Ankhtepot’s priests oversee all aspects of life, apportioning food to the worthy and punishing blasphemers. But all the pharaoh’s servants also pursue the Darklord’s quest for his mysterious ka, though only his Children know the truth of this “treasure” he seeks. Each of them is desperate for some clue to placate Ankhtepot and spare them from his wrath.

Domain Features

Har’Akir is known for the following features:

Distant Pharaoh. The people of Har’Akir know their ruler is an immortal who resides in the golden mountain called Pharaoh’s Rest. Only priests can enter this massive pyramid.

Extreme Environs. Intense heat and winds are common in Har’Akir (see the Dungeon Master’s Guide), and water is scarce.

Forgotten Ruins. The deserts of Har’Akir are littered with the remnants of a strange and glorious empire. The origins of these ruins remain a mystery.

Mysterious Gods. Har’Akir’s people worship a pantheon of false or silent gods. Manipulative or deluded priests oversee all aspects of society in these gods’ and the pharaoh’s names.

Preserved Dead. Akirrans mummify their dead so the pharaoh might call them to service. It is a crime to burn the dead.

Locations in Har’Akir

Har’Akir is dominated by treacherous, surreal deserts that obey Ankhtepot’s will. The Labyrinth and city of Muhar are described below, and other notable locations are summarized on the Har’Akir Locations table.

Labyrinth

Har’Akir’s countless trap-laden ruins interconnect beneath the desert in a hidden maze called the Labyrinth. Monstrous @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmMummy000000000]{Mummies} and stranger creatures stalk these sprawling tunnels. Nevertheless, some priests use the few mapped sections to move about the domain unseen.

Muhar

The city of Muhar is the only permanent settlement in Har’Akir. Here, pious people thank the gods for the water and food of the Muhar Oasis. From the city’s largest structure, the Temple of Ese, High Priest Isu Rehkotep speaks for Pharaoh Ankhtepot in all matters; her word is considered both royal decree and divine edict. Ankhtepot doesn’t care how Isu runs Muhar so long as she maintains order and uses her influence to continue his unending search. Those who displease Isu or oppose her priest-guards are thrown into the Mouth of Oru, a pit that connects to the Labyrinth.

Ankhtepot has known treachery and conquest. He has known divinity and rule. But now he knows simply boredom and despair. He desires only to recover his lost ka, which he knows lies hidden in Har’Akir. With it, he hopes to become mortal again, die, and face his gods’ judgment. Whether this means peace or oblivion matters not to him. Ankhtepot seeks only an end, and he and his followers will be a plague on Har’Akir until he finds it.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.wHhc7Sfv5KKT1PjI cite=false]

Har’Akir Locations

Location

Description

Bent Pyramid

This bent, black granite pyramid has no interior or obvious purpose. Some Akirrans claim it holds the magic of a forgotten god that one can manipulate using ancient music.

City of the Dead

Massive statues and the ornate tombs of heroes and priests compose the City of the Dead. These crypts climb toward Ankhtepot’s mountain-size golden pyramid, Pharaoh’s Rest.

Kharafek’s Excavation

The historian Kharafek has discovered numerous tombs at this excavation. Each unleashes a curse once opened, so Kharafek regularly hires new laborers to bear the brunt of these curses.

Muhar Oasis

Muhar Oasis supplies the city of Muhar and attracts predators such as @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmChimera0000000]{Chimeras} and @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGiantScorpion0]{Giant Scorpions}. During droughts, the water recedes, revealing a lapis lazuli tomb along the banks.

Ousa’s Pyramid

Ousa’s Pyramid holds a gate to the afterlife, but only Thute—a jackal-headed Child of Ankhtepot—knows how to enter. Thute’s jackal-masked followers seek to sacrifice all strangers.

Path of the Asp

Deadly serpents inhabit the canyon known as the Path of the Asp. The spirits of those slain by these snakes can appear to anyone they know and speak ten words before vanishing.

Red Oasis

Akirrans say that the gods gave the Red Oasis to animals and that its waters are poison to people. Supernaturally cunning lions hunt in the area and offer sacrifices to a nearby monument.

Sands of Sute

Within the desert rage two vast sandstorms: the Breath of the Forgotten and the Breath of the False. The storms impose tests that punish those whose lives lack consequence or faith.

Sek’s Tears

People say the waters of Sek’s Tears can heal any affliction, but grim @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmSphinxOfValor0]{Sphinxes} guard the oasis. The answers to the sphinxes’ riddles are usually found in other domains.

Senmet’s Tomb

The Child of Ankhtepot named Senmet seeks to steal the pharaoh’s throne. From his tomb, Senmet navigates the Labyrinth and stokes rebellion among the living and the dead.

White Oasis

The White Oasis is considered holy. A nearby abandoned quarry called River’s Shelter houses numerous cursed and dying hermits who seek to cleanse themselves in the oasis.

Adventures in Har’Akir.name: Adventures in Har’Akir
Adventures in Har’Akir.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.lGFK5tpq0XYGJH8a inline]

Adventures set in Har’Akir involve ancient secrets, manipulative cultists, the ancient dead, and the search for Ankhtepot’s missing ka. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

Horror in Har’Akir

Adventures in Har’Akir often include the following elements.

Children of Ankhtepot

Ankhtepot resurrected the priests who died alongside him as @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmMummyLord00000]{Mummy Lords}, granting each the head of an animal or monster corresponding to the fictitious gods he imposed on Har’Akir. The Children of Ankhtepot style themselves as harbingers of these gods while they manipulate cults or the domain’s priesthood. Some have grown tired of Ankhtepot’s detachment and seek to depose the pharaoh—particularly the treacherous mummy lord Senmet.

Gods of Har’Akir

Ankhtepot has destroyed all evidence of the deities the people of Har’Akir worshipped in ages past, replacing that pantheon with gods of his own creation, as summarized on the Gods of Har’Akir table.

Gods of Har’Akir

God

Animal

Concern

Anu

Jackal

Judging the dead

Ese

Oryx

Life and the living

Neb

Rust monster

Passage from life to death

Oru

Hydra

Cosmic order

Ousa

Raven

Death and the dead

Sek

Dragonfly

Healing and life

Sute

Three-eyed cat

Despair and discord

Pharaoh’s Priests

The priests of Har’Akir work Ankhtepot’s will, not realizing their deities are false. They keep alert for strangers and omens related to the treasure their pharaoh covets without knowing its true nature. Promising strangers are brought to High Priest Isu Rehkotep at the Temple of Ese in Muhar. The decadent high priest wants to possess what her pharaoh seeks, planning to use it to manipulate Ankhtepot.

Pharaoh’s Soul

Ankhtepot seeks his ka, the missing piece of his soul. It’s up to you what form this takes. Roll on or choose a result from The Darklord’s Soul table to determine the form of Ankhtepot’s ka.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwTheDarklordP2 rollable resultLabel="Ankhtepot’s Ka Is …"]{The Darklord’s Soul}

Once the Darklord recovers his ka, Ankhtepot can restore his soul by completing an elaborate 1-hour ritual involving the sacrifice of a living person. Only then might he journey to the afterlife—whether he succeeds is up to the Dark Powers.

Har’Akir Campaign Arc

Campaigns set in Har’Akir often involve the domain’s treacherous priests, its ancient tombs, and the hunt for Ankhtepot’s ka. A campaign here might follow this outline:

Levels 1–4. The characters help a priest named Basras search a trap-laden, buried palace complex for a mysterious treasure. Within, they discover a set of oversize canopic jars. They take them to Isu Rehkotep in Muhar, but the high priest deems them worthless. Basras is thrown into the Mouth of Oru.

Levels 5–10. Isu Rehkotep hires the characters to search for the pharaoh’s missing treasure. She sends them to Senmet’s Tomb to recover an ark full of ancient scrolls, claiming these are what the pharaoh desires. (She doesn’t actually know the object of the pharaoh’s search.)

Levels 11–15. After the characters return the scrolls to Isu, Basras (now an Undead), reveals that the high priest seeks to use them to control a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmMummyLord00000]{Mummy Lord} and oppose Ankhtepot, as detailed in the “Scrolls of the Dead” adventure.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.o6Gpz4hVBez6ZNk8 cite=false]

Scrolls of the Dead.name: Scrolls of the Dead
Scrolls of the Dead.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.YEudF0c8dEtybnQ5 cite=false classes="float-right"]

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.adventures.Adventure.6rTS47QMBouR7rS0]{Adventure for Level 12 Characters}

Situation. High Priest Isu Rehkotep claims to have found the treasure Ankhtepot has been searching for and prepares to offer it to him during a ritual at the pyramid of Pharaoh’s Rest. In truth, she plans to use a scroll that controls the Child of Ankhtepot called Senmet to destroy the pharaoh and claim his throne.

Hook. The priest De’rah fears the pharaoh will vent his wrath on the people of Muhar when the high priest is discovered. She offers the characters [[/award 5000gp]] and a random magic item from the Relics—Rare table in the Dungeon Master’s Guide if they stop Isu.

Encounters

This adventure uses @UUID[Scene.TRM2bDCTbAGLbLAA]{map 2.14} and consists of these encounters.

Exploring the Pyramid. Rehkotep’s ritual takes place during the full moon, when the entrance to Pharaoh’s Rest stands open. Pharaoh’s Rest is a vast structure, but the path Rehkotep and her followers followed through the thick dust and sand of the lower reaches is obvious. Details of the pyramid’s lower levels are beyond the scope of this adventure.

Pyramid Heights. When the characters reach the Shrine of Ankhtepot in the upper levels of Pharaoh’s Rest, and when they enter the Lesser Tombs, roll on the following table to determine what lurks within.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwPyramidHeigw1 rollable resultLabel="Encounter" caption=false]

Ritual Site. On the funeral barge level of the pyramid, @UUID[Actor.rw7JNHFG3BGTTQeu]{Isu Rehkotep} (Medium, Lawful Evil @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmArchpriest0000]{Archpriest}) readies to call forth Ankhtepot from his tomb using a ritual involving a gong on the ceremonial ship. Meanwhile, two @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmPriest00000000]{Priests} and two @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmDeathCultist00]{Death Cultists} defend her. Isu avoids battle, but if any of her allies fall, she produces an @UUID[Actor.rw7JNHFG3BGTTQeu.Item.0x8uJNrXXuk4WZX0]{ancient papyrus scroll} and [[/item Actor.rw7JNHFG3BGTTQeu.Item.0x8uJNrXXuk4WZX0 activity=s7VY8ijHewjIEQZr]]{reads it}, summoning Senmet.

The Mummy. If Isu reads her scroll, 1 round after her allies are defeated, the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmMummyLord00000]{Mummy Lord} @UUID[Actor.bvkPlapVd8AXy92m]{Senmet} appears. Isu orders him to destroy the characters, and he obeys. Senmet fights until he or the scroll the high priest holds is destroyed. Isu doesn’t join the battle and attempts to escape if Senmet is destroyed.

The Scroll. A character adjacent to Isu can steal the scroll from her by succeeding on a [[/check slt dc=16 format=long]] or by taking an action if she has the &Reference[Incapacitated apply=false] or &Reference[Restrained apply=false] condition. Any character can take an action to destroy the scroll but can’t use it to command Senmet. If it is destroyed, Senmet turns Hostile toward Isu.

The Pharaoh. Once Isu and Senmet are dealt with, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwAnkhtepot0000]{Ankhtepot} (see earlier in this chapter) appears at the path leading up to his tomb. He demands the characters depart immediately but return with his missing ka. If they do so, fabulous riches will be theirs. If they don’t, they will know his wrath. Characters who escape the tomb after the encounter with Ankhtepot earn a bonus [[/award 4700xp]].

Haunted Bastions.name: Haunted Bastions
Haunted Bastions.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.7WOjvK5bD8a43ggy inline]

Haunted Bastions are afflicted by unusual supernatural events. These might be unpredictable annoyances at first, but clever characters might turn hauntings to their advantage.

If you use this system, the first special facility characters create in their Bastion is Haunted. A Haunted special facility has the following rules:

Irreplaceable. A Haunted special facility can’t be replaced until it is no longer Haunted.

Repeating. While there is at least one Haunted special facility in a Bastion, whenever a character gains a new special facility or replaces a special facility with a different one, roll any die; on an even result, that new facility is Haunted.

When a character issues an order to a Haunted special facility or a Maintain order to their whole Bastion, roll 1d6; on a 1, roll on the Bastion Hauntings table and refer to the “Haunting Descriptions” section to see what happens. A Bastion can experience only one haunting per Bastion turn.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwBastionHaun5X rollable resultLabel="Haunting" classes="caption-top float-right"]{Bastion Hauntings}

Haunting Descriptions

The hauntings from the Bastion Hauntings table are detailed here in alphabetical order.

Cursed Hireling. One of your Bastion’s hirelings is cursed. As long as the hireling remains cursed, it won’t leave the special facility it manages, and any work conducted in that facility takes twice as long to complete.

Free-Roaming Spirit. A random special facility in your Bastion becomes Haunted. If all the special facilities in the Bastion are already Haunted, the DM rolls again on the Bastion Hauntings table.

Friendly Ghost. A benevolent spirit possesses a hireling of the DM’s choice. If the special facility where the hireling works produces items or Gold Pieces, it produces twice as much as usual as long as the hireling remains possessed. The spirit departs after 14 days.

Hidden Room. The spirits reveal a hidden closet within your Bastion. Add a 5-foot-square space to your Bastion. Something might lie inside, at the DM’s discretion.

Lightning Storm. Trade orders issued this turn have no effect (including one that triggered this haunting, if applicable). If you have a Lightning Generator special facility, this Bastion Haunting has no effect.

Living Dead. A group of mindless undead shamble from the Mists. The Attack Bastion event occurs, but roll 3d6 for that event rather than 6d6.

Mist Wanderers. The Mists rise and 1d4 strangers emerge, seeking shelter. If there is room in your Bastion, they join as hirelings. Otherwise, they linger for 1d4 days before vanishing into the Mists.

Mysterious Priest. A hooded priest appears at the Bastion, attracted by its haunting. Roll any die. One of the following occurs based on whether you rolled an even or an odd number on the die:

Inquisitor (Even). The priest is a witch hunter. If you have an Ancient Altar, Arcane Study, Cabinet of Curiosities, or Séance Parlor in your Bastion, the priest destroys it and you must replace it with a different special facility. If your Bastion has none of these facilities, the Extraordinary Opportunity Bastion event occurs.

Exorcist (Odd). The priest is an exorcist. If you have a Reliquary, Sacristy, Sanctuary, or Sanctum in your Bastion, the priest offers to remove a haunting from one facility. If you agree, choose one Haunted facility; that facility is no longer Haunted.

Ill Luck. Accidents and bad luck plague your Bastion. Harvest orders issued this turn have no effect (including one that triggered this haunting, if applicable).

Poltergeist. Disruptive happenings plague the Bastion. Empower and Research orders issued this turn have no effect (including one that triggered this haunting, if applicable).

Spatial Warp. The nature of a Bastion special facility changes. Roll 1d4. On a 4, replace one of your Bastion’s special facilities with another for which you qualify; otherwise, increase the size of a Cramped special facility in your Bastion to Roomy or a Roomy one to Vast, at no cost.

Spectral Vision. A ghostly vision of a past event plays out. You gain some insight into the spirits that haunt your Bastion or the history of the surrounding land, as though affected by the Legend Lore spell.

Vistani Visitors. Vistani travelers visit your Bastion, drawn by tales of the spirits that inhabit it. In return for a night’s shelter, they offer to reveal your fate via a casting of the Augury spell or through a tarokka reading (see appendix A). If you turn them away, they depart, but the highest-level special facility in your Bastion that isn’t already Haunted becomes Haunted.

Bastions in the Mists.name: Bastions in the Mists
Bastions in the Mists.text:
Bastions in the Domains of Dread work well in campaigns that focus on a single domain or where Darklords aren’t likely to seal their domains’ borders and cut characters off from their Bastions.

Characters can create Bastions in any domain. The domains of Borca, Darkon, Lamordia, Mordent, and the Shadowlands are well suited to hosting Bastions due to their large size or aloof Darklords. Consider having characters inherit one or more existing structures—such as a ruined fortress, secluded tower, or house recently scoured of ghosts. Should characters adopt such a structure, they can refurbish it to their specifications.

How Darklords respond to Bastions is up to you, but don’t create plots that are hostile to Bastions. Darklords might ignore a Bastion, be curious about it, or use it as one more way to bind characters within their domains.

Special Facilities.name: Special Facilities
Special Facilities.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.5sjMhZF0u35Cp32n classes="float-right size-three" cite=false]

Characters can create the following special facilities in addition to those in the Dungeon Master’s Guide. These facilities are summarized on the Special Facilities table.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.bastions.Item.rhwAncientAltar0]{Ancient Altar}

Level 9 Bastion Facility

Prerequisite: None

Space: Cramped

Hirelings: 1

Order: Empower

An Ancient Altar is a solemn chamber where you can make offerings to mysterious forces.

Empower: Purge Haunted Facility. You commission your hireling to lead a ritual that takes 7 days, during which time you must remain in the Bastion. At the end of the ritual, you must offer a worthy sacrifice, which can be monetary (1,000 GP) or blood (the fresh corpse of at least one Large creature or four Medium creatures that aren’t Constructs or Undead). If you make a blood sacrifice, roll on the Bastion Hauntings table. Once the ritual is complete, the sacrifice is destroyed. Choose a Haunted special facility in this Bastion or in a Bastion connected to this one; that facility is no longer Haunted.

Blasphemous Charm. After spending a Long Rest in your Ancient Altar, you gain a magical Charm that lasts for 7 days or until you use it. You can use the Charm as a Magic action to cast Hunger of Hadar without requiring Material components. You can’t gain this Charm again while you have it.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.bastions.Item.rhwCabinetofCuri]{Cabinet of Curiosities}

Level 9 Bastion Facility

Prerequisite: None

Space: Cramped

Hirelings: 1

Order: Trade

A Cabinet of Curiosities is a small salon, closet space, or antechamber where you can keep and catalog peculiar objects and fascinating oddities.

Trade: Acquire Curios. When you issue the Trade order to this facility, you supply this facility’s hireling with a certain amount of gold and commission them to purchase strange items from unusual sources. It takes 28 days for the hireling to return to the Bastion with one or more magic items for you. Your investment determines the rarity of these items, and the quantity of magic items depends on the number of Haunted special facilities in your Bastion at the time of the hireling’s return, as shown on the Curio Investment and Curios Collected tables.

To determine how many magic items your hireling brings back, count the Haunted special facilities in your Bastion, then roll the die indicated in the Magic Items Acquired column on the Curios Collected table. The DM rolls on the Cursed Item Chance (1d100) column to determine whether one of the magic items carries a curse. The DM chooses which item is cursed and establishes the nature of the curse.

For each magic item acquired, roll on a table in the “Random Magic Items” section in the Dungeon Master’s Guide of the corresponding rarity to determine what the item is. (Select the treasure theme randomly.)

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.bastions.Item.rhwInfirmary0000]{Infirmary}

Level 9 Bastion Facility

Prerequisite: Proficiency in the Medicine skill

Space: Roomy

Hirelings: 2

Order: Craft

An Infirmary provides emergency healing and continual care to injured or sick Bastion occupants.

Craft Options. When you issue the Craft order to this facility, choose one of the following options:

Craft: Antitoxin. You commission the facility’s hirelings to craft two vials of Antitoxin. The work takes 7 days and costs no money. You can spend 50 GP during the creation process to enhance one of the vials of Antitoxin, or 100 GP to enhance both. An enhanced Antitoxin ends the Poisoned condition on the creature that drinks it. An enhanced Antitoxin loses this enhancement after 28 days.

Craft: Enhanced Potion of Healing. You commission this facility’s hirelings to increase the potency of up to two Potions of Healing. You must provide the potions to be enhanced, and the process takes 14 days to complete. A Potion of Healing becomes a Potion of Healing (greater). When you reach level 13 you can provide up to two Potions of Healing (greater), each of which becomes a Potion of Healing (superior).

Triage. When your Bastion is attacked and you roll a number of dice to determine how many Bastion Defenders are lost, you can reroll a number of those dice equal to the number of hirelings this facility has. You must use the new roll.

Enlarging the Facility. You can enlarge your Infirmary to a Vast facility by spending 2,000 GP. If you do so, the Infirmary gains two hirelings, and the number of Antitoxins or Potions of Healing you can enhance at a time increases to four.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.bastions.Item.rhwLightningGene]{Lightning Generator}

Level 13 Bastion Facility

Prerequisite: None

Space: Roomy

Hirelings: 1

Order: Empower

A Lightning Generator can transform atmospheric electricity to serve a variety of useful functions.

Charm of Electromancy. After spending a Long Rest in your Lightning Generator, you take 8d6 Lightning damage and you gain a magical Charm that lasts for 7 days (see the Dungeon Master’s Guide for rules on Charms). The Charm has 3 charges. You can expend 1 charge from the Charm to cast the Telekinesis spell. Once all its charges are expended, the Charm vanishes from you.

Revivification. When you cast the spell Raise Dead and Resurrection while in this facility, the cost of that spell’s Material components are reduced by 1,000 GP (minimum of 0).

Empower: Lightning Life. The facility’s hireling channels the Generator’s energy to cast Animate Objects, targeting one Large or Huge object of your choice in the Bastion. The spell’s duration is 7 days and doesn’t require Concentration.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.bastions.Item.rhwLiminalSpace0]{Liminal Space}

Level 17 Bastion Facility

Prerequisite: None

Space: Vast

Hirelings: 1

Order: Recruit

Your Bastion becomes connected to an unfathomable demiplane filled with vast, empty spaces and strange beings. This special facility doesn’t take up space in your Bastion; instead, the entrance to this facility appears in the closets, crawlspaces, and shadows of your Bastion’s other basic and special facilities. The hireling in charge of this facility protects the Bastion’s inhabitants from stray entities that slip out from the demiplane. This facility always counts as Haunted.

Dark Bargains. You can enter this facility and wander within for 7 days, consulting and bargaining with the strange entities there. If you leave the Bastion during this time, you gain no benefit. At the end of the seventh day, you gain a Dark Gift feat of your choice (see chapter 1). You can only have one feat from this benefit at a time; if you use this benefit when you already have a feat from it, the feat you gain at the end of the seven days replaces any feat previously gained from use of this facility.

Recruit: Stranger. When you issue the Recruit order to this facility, you instruct the facility’s hireling to conduct a ritual and petition an entity from the demiplane for aid. This ritual takes 7 days and costs 1,000 GP. At the ritual’s end, roll any die. On an odd number, the summoned entity refuses your request and kills the hireling, and this facility can’t be used until your next Bastion turn, at which point the hireling is replaced at no cost to you; on an even number, the summoned entity agrees to your request and takes up residence in your Liminal Space.

While residing in your Liminal Space, an entity counts as a Bastion Defender, and if your Bastion is attacked, each entity in your Liminal Space reduces by 1 the number of dice you roll to determine how many Bastion Defenders are lost in the attack. You can choose not to count the entity as a Bastion Defender, in which case you can’t call on it to defend the Bastion; instead, the entity acts according to its nature, using the Unspeakable Horror stat block (see chapter 5). You can have up to three entities in your Liminal Space at a time. Deduct any you lose from your Bastion Defenders roster.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.bastions.Item.rhwRookery000000]{Rookery}

Level 5 Bastion Facility

Prerequisite: None

Space: Cramped

Hirelings: 1

Order: Craft

A number of ravens equal to your character level roost in a Rookery. Whenever a creature enters the facility, the birds uncannily track its movement, an unfathomable intelligence behind their beady eyes.

Craft: Mist Talisman. When you issue the Craft order to this facility, you can commission the hireling to send ravens to various contacts in a Domain of Dread you have either visited previously or heard about from a creature that has been within said domain. This work takes 21 days, during which time no other activity can occur within this facility.

At the end of this period, the hireling produces a Mist talisman (see the introduction) linked to the chosen Domain of Dread.

Messenger Ravens. Whenever you finish a Long Rest in your Bastion, you can send one raven bearing a written message of 25 words or fewer to a creature you have met or a creature described to you by someone who has met it. After receiving this message, the recipient can reply in a like manner, attaching their response to the raven. Regardless of whether or not the recipient responds, the raven returns to the rookery after 7 days have passed.

You can send a message via raven in this way across any distance, though the message recipient must be on the same plane of existence as your Bastion or in a demiplane linked to yours by the Mists of Ravenloft. A messenger raven delivers the message regardless of any magical wards or other interposing forces, including the Mists and closed borders of a Domain of Dread. If the raven is unable to deliver your message, it returns to your rookery after 7 days with your original message still attached.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.bastions.Item.rhwSeanceParlor0]{Séance Parlor}

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.vNihgDczIqzbUcBa cite=false classes="float-right size-two"]

Level 5 Bastion Facility

Prerequisite: None

Space: Cramped

Hirelings: 1

Order: Research

The veil between the world of the living and the dead grows thin in a Séance Parlor.

Research: Fortune Telling. When you issue the Research order to this facility, you commission its hireling to read your fortune by casting bones, interpreting tea leaves, performing a tarokka reading, or using some other means. After your fortune is read, you gain Heroic Inspiration. You can’t gain this benefit again until you finish a Long Rest.

Speak with Dead. After spending a Long Rest in your Séance Parlor, you can cast Speak with Dead immediately without expending a spell slot or needing Material components. When you cast it in this way, you can target any deceased creature whose name you know, as long as you have a piece of its remains (such as ashes or a bone) or an object that was important to the creature in life (such as a brooch or signature weapon). The target speaks through an implement in the Séance Room such as a crystal ball or spirit board.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.bastions.Item.rhwSpiritVault00]{Spirit Vault}

Level 13 Bastion Facility

Prerequisite: None

Space: Roomy

Hirelings: 1

Order: Research

A Spirit Vault is magically warded to imprison and exorcise malevolent spirits and other supernatural forces. Haunted portraits, cursed relics, and other eerie curios line the shelves of this dark vault. This facility always counts as Haunted.

Esoteric Knowledge. After spending a Long Rest in your Spirit Vault, you gain one of the following magical Charms:

Charm of Exorcism. You can use the Charm as a Magic action to cast Dispel Evil or Good or Remove Curse without requiring Material components.

Charm of Malediction. You can use the Charm as a Magic action to cast Hex (level 3 version) or Bestow Curse without requiring Material components.

The Charm lasts for 7 days or until you use it. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for the Charm’s spells (choose each time you gain this Charm).

Research: Ask the Occult. When you issue the Research order to this facility, you or its hireling study the various relics and question the imprisoned entities within your Spirit Vault. This work takes 7 days. At the end of this time, roll 1d6. On a 1, the work is psychically taxing; if you did the research, you take 6d6 Psychic damage, and if the hireling did the research, they immediately die, and this facility can’t be used again until your next Bastion turn. Otherwise, you or the hireling gain information as if you had successfully cast the Contact Other Plane spell. The hireling shares any information they learned with you the next time you speak.

Haunted Heroes.name: Haunted Heroes
Haunted Heroes.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.fpJiGMoTcRRDprti cite=false classes="float-right size-three"]

This section highlights ways you can create a character prepared to participate in horror adventures.

Before You Begin.name: Before You Begin
Before You Begin.text: Who is a horror adventure supposed to be scary for: you as the player or your character? Is it suspenseful like a mystery story or frightening like a slasher movie? What do you not want to see in a horror adventure? Align with your group’s DM and other players on topics like these before participating in fear-focused adventures. Horror tales cover broad swaths of content. What some might find unsettling, others might shrug off. If you are the DM, consult the “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-dungeon-masters-guide.content.JournalEntry.dmgEnsuringFunFo]{Ensuring Fun for All}” section of the Dungeon Master’s Guide for guidance on setting expectations about your game.

Building a Horror Hero.name: Building a Horror Hero
Building a Horror Hero.text: While playing a horror adventure, you participate in building a sense of dread. If your character laughs in the face of every danger, they undermine the adventure’s atmosphere. Consider how past events might haunt your character and contribute to the mood of horror adventures.

Prepare to Be Scared.name: Prepare to Be Scared
Prepare to Be Scared.text:
Knowing what frightens your character provides insight into their past and can motivate their behavior. A character’s fear of cats might stem from a terrible sight they witnessed at their grandmother’s home, while a fear of earthquakes might tie back to the experience of being trapped after a tremor. Consider two or three things that unsettle your character and how those fears shape your character’s past and present.

Courage isn’t the absence of fear, but how one deals with and overcomes it. Consider this in the context of your character. How might they react to a startling scene before rallying to overcome it? Does your character scream and flee, freeze in place, or jump recklessly into battle? Record a default fear reaction on your character sheet to help you respond when shocking events occur.

Fear Stays in the Game.name: Fear Stays in the Game
Fear Stays in the Game.text: Understand the difference between scaring characters and scaring players. For example, if you know another player has a fear of spiders, never employ that knowledge when contributing to a creepy scene. The Dungeon Master’s Guide offers guidance (see “Hard and Soft Limits”) to ensure your group has a method for raising concerns mid-game, in the event that things become too intense or go in directions you don’t want to explore. If something in the game leads you to consider scaring or otherwise involving another player’s character, ask that player’s permission before acting. Their enjoyment is more important than shock value.

Sinister Secrets.name: Sinister Secrets
Sinister Secrets.text:
Many characters in horror adventures have shadowy pasts: a harrowing experience that haunts their dreams, a bargain with sinister forces they keep hidden from others, or some other nefarious secret. How a character engages with such internal darkness can make for exciting conflicts. Be sure that your choices allow your character to remain a reliable part of your adventuring group, though, and not a near-villain other heroes only tolerate.

If you’d like your character’s secret to have mechanical implications, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwFeats00000000.JournalEntryPage.DarkGiftshT3zjqR]{Dark Gifts} (detailed later in this chapter) grant characters supernatural abilities in return for sinister consequences.

Enjoying the Struggle.name: Enjoying the Struggle
Enjoying the Struggle.text:
Not everyone can escape a horror story unscathed. While your character should do everything they can to triumph over challenges, any scars they gain along the way are part of what makes the horror meaningful and memorable.

Consider your goals as a participant in a horror adventure. Are you aiming to ensure the party’s survival or just your character’s? Or do you simply want to create a memorable experience for the other players? Let your goals evolve during an adventure. If these goals lead to greater tension, drama, or sacrifice in ways that echo your favorite horror tales—and that don’t undermine other players’ enjoyment of the game—embrace them. Your character’s actions have great influence in creating exciting and impactful horror adventures.

Character Options.name: Character Options
Character Options.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.wsphIxaln6oeDHdw cite=false]

Whether for a night or an eternity, the Mists of Ravenloft pit heroes against their greatest nightmares. This chapter presents resources for players creating and playing characters in horror adventures, including new subclasses, backgrounds, species, and feats. These character options supplement the rules presented in the Player’s Handbook.

Adventures in Hazlan.name: Adventures in Hazlan
Adventures in Hazlan.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.Gwub5dNmmmdnbQJQ inline]

Tales in Hazlan involve radical magical experiments, supernatural environments, rivalries between mages, and magical surveillance. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

Horror in Hazlan

Adventures set in Hazlan often include the following elements.

Eyes of Hazlik

Hazlik spies on his domain’s inhabitants using the Eye of Hazlik. This eyelike sigil adorns buildings, clothing, talismans, and creatures across the domain. Locals claim the symbol protects them from dangerous magic and creatures. Hazlik uses the eyes to spy on useful or threatening mages and to reinforce his reputation as an ever-present overlord.

Hazlik often sends loyal apprentices or magical creatures to capture those whose behavior witnessed through the eyes offended him. He turns such individuals into fodder for experimentation in Sly-Var or Veneficus.

Hazlan Wild Magic

As a result of unchecked arcane experimentation, magic functions erratically in Hazlan. Whenever a creature in Hazlan expends a spell slot to cast a spell or activates a magic item, it rolls [[/r 1d10]]. On a 1, it unleashes a random magical effect. Roll on the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-players-handbook.tables.RollTable.phbWildMagicSurg]{Wild Magic Surge} table in chapter 3 of the Player’s Handbook to determine the effect. Consider describing these results in unsettling ways that don’t change their effects, as in these examples:

  • Summoned creatures use the proscribed stat blocks but are cosmetically warped to resemble undead or aberrant creatures.

  • Effects that teleport the caster relocate the caster into a misty expanse full of threatening shapes.

  • Effects on creatures cause unsettling sensations, such as the feeling of earwigs wriggling beneath one’s skin or one’s teeth struggling free.

Anyone who openly wears an Eye of Hazlik can choose not to roll on the Wild Magic Surge table in this way once per day.

Hazlik’s Apprentices

Dozens of mages serve as Hazlik’s apprentices. Between infrequent, demeaning group lessons with the Darklord, these apprentices pit themselves against one another to impress Hazlik by pushing the boundaries of magical innovation. Those who gain Hazlik’s favor are promised arcane secrets known only to the Darklord—secrets that might not exist. This creates a cadre of Hazlani mages who recklessly seek to impress Hazlik by the following means, among others:

  • Changing the nature of reality to suit their whims

  • Creating monuments in Hazlik’s honor

  • Devising new magic items, spells, or supernatural materials

  • Forging unique magical servants for Hazlik

  • Stealing rare magic items

  • Unleashing dramatic displays of magical might

Hazlik’s current favorite apprentices include the masked wizard Yhal the Skygazer, the shadow-cloaked Ruzelo, and the daring genius Eleni.

Hazlan Campaign Arc

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.3xefSBm8DsiflYNv classes="float-right" cite=false]

Campaigns set in Hazlan often involve mages abusing their power to garner Hazlik’s fickle favor. A campaign here might follow this outline:

Levels 1–3. The characters arrive in Toyalis, where servants of the mage Khrinitov abduct locals with supposed psychic potential. Eleni, an apprentice of Hazlik, believes Khrinitov’s experiments are pointless. She hires the characters to rescue the villagers from Khrinitov’s tower amid the Orbitoclasts. In doing so, the characters reveal Khrinitov to be an @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmIntellectDevou]{Intellect Devourer}, a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwBraininaJar00]{Brain in a Jar} (see chapter 5), or another aberrant being.

Levels 4–8. Eleni reveals that she’s trying to undermine Hazlik and protect the people of Hazlan. She tells the characters of a magic mineral in the Mound of the Worm that @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmPurpleWorm0000]{Purple Worms} pursue. She encourages the party to collect the mineral and use the purple worms to destroy locations like the Marks. As the characters do so, they face magical guardians and hazards. During their travel, they’re exposed to strange magic and might gain @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwFeats00000000.JournalEntryPage.DarkGiftshT3zjqR]{Dark Gift} feats (see chapter 1).

Levels 9–12. Eleni asks the characters to come to Sly-Var to plan a raid on Hazlik’s palace. There, she reveals that she’s been exposing them to dangerous magic from across the domain. She entraps them with a portal that leads to arena-like laboratories connected to Veneficus, where Hazlik pits them against terrors his other apprentices have created. The characters must escape, potentially facing Eleni and Hazlik along the way.

Hazlik.name: Hazlik
Hazlik.text:
Darklord of Desperation and Magical Disaster

The paranoid, egomaniacal archmage Hazlik clings to his power despite his magical ambitions being supernaturally smothered. Since Hazlik entered Hazlan, his magic has become unstable. Spells he casts behave erratically and fade swiftly—most lasting no more than an hour—while his magical creations fall apart in about a day. The idea of others learning this terrifies the Darklord, which leads him to claim others’ works as his own, spy on potential rivals, and order reckless research into the nature of magic and his domain.

Hazlik suffers vivid nightmares wherein past rivals taunt and torment him. The Darklord avoids sleeping, employing magical tinctures to stay awake, but this causes him to vacillate between bouts of exhaustion, jitters, and paranoia.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwHazlik0000000 statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Hazlik’s Tale

On the world of Toril, Hazlik rose among the ranks of the merciless Red Wizards of Thay. Arrogant and convinced mortality was a weakness, he sought hidden truths of magic, ways to visit realms within the psyche, and command of reality.

As Hazlik’s power increased, he developed rivalries with other Red Wizards, many of which ended with his foes magically scarred. But one rival Hazlik couldn’t defeat: the wizard Indreficus. Hazlik gradually came to obsess over Indreficus, viewing him as his only worthy opponent. Indreficus shared Hazlik’s genius and arrogance, and they spent seasons as vicious adversaries and passionate lovers. They drove one another to feats of magical brilliance both revolutionary and unspeakable. This culminated when Hazlik discovered that Indreficus planned to betray Hazlik to garner the favor of Thay’s ruling zulkirs. Heartbroken and enraged, Hazlik captured Indreficus and subjected him to nightmarish magical manipulations that permanently transformed him into a pain-wracked, living magical portal. Hazlik presented his creation to the zulkirs, hoping to gain their patronage

The zulkirs revealed that Indreficus hadn’t betrayed Hazlik. Rather, the ruling mages had watched the pair’s ascent and, wary of their ambitions, undermined them before they became threats. Moreover, they deemed Hazlik’s transformation of Indreficus an abomination. As punishment, the zulkirs decreed that every mage Hazlik had scarred could do the same in return to him, marking him with their personal symbols. As the first brand-wielding member of the mob of Hazlik’s rivals closed on the wizard, Hazlik fled through the groaning portal that was Indreficus.

Hazlik emerged amid the pain-wracked dreamscapes of Indreficus. Unwittingly, Hazlik had entered the Domain of Dread known as the Nightmare Lands, where Indreficus’s shrieking and condemnation hounded him. After months of torment, Hazlik escaped into the Mists and eventually emerged into a realm that knew nothing of magic. His Thayan tattoos had turned gold and warped into symbols meaning “traitor.” Undaunted, Hazlik used his magic to dominate the people of this new realm, claiming the title of zulkir and dubbing his realm Hazlan.

Hazlan’s Domain

Hazlan is altered by Hazlik’s presence, creating the following effects:

Closing the Borders. While in his domain, Hazlik can close or open the borders to Hazlan at will. While the borders are closed, the Mists surrounding Hazlan are filled with monstrous magical experiments. A creature that enters the Mists for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there must succeed on a [[/save format dex 15 format=long]] or take [[/damage 1d10 force average]] damage and have the &Reference[Restrained] condition until the start of its next turn. A creature makes this save only once per turn.

Frayed Magic. Whenever a creature (excluding Hazlik and his allies) casts a spell using a level 5+ spell slot, it rolls on the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-players-handbook.tables.RollTable.phbWildMagicSurg]{Wild Magic Surge} table in the Player’s Handbook to create a magical effect.

Magic Nightmares. While in his lair, Veneficus, Hazlik can cast @UUID[Compendium.dnd-players-handbook.spells.Item.phbsplClairvoyan]{Clairvoyance}, requiring no spell components and using the same spellcasting ability as his Spellcasting action. When the spell is cast this way, its range is all of Hazlan, Hazlik can see but can’t hear through the sensor, and the sensor can appear only on an Eye of Hazlik.

If Hazlik dies, these effects end immediately. The effects resume if Hazlik gains a new body (see his Darklord Restoration trait).

Hazlik’s Lair

Hazlik’s crimson-towered palace, Veneficus, stands atop the jagged mesa called the Red Rise. The Darklord is attended by a staff of magical creations and his most accomplished apprentices.

Veneficus Features

Veneficus is littered with frivolous magic effects and magic items that no longer keep Hazlik’s attention. The site is depicted on map 2.16 and has the following features:

Halls of Triumph. Multiple audience chambers, display galleries, and pleasure gardens rival one another in decadence, each exhibiting grotesque artwork and trophies of Hazlik’s achievements.

House of Solitude. The secluded House of Solitude is magically locked, and Hazlik forbids anyone from entering. At night, muffled screams and flickering lights emanate from within.

Red Tower. Hazlik’s personal chambers fill the Red Tower. Magical portals connect the untold numbers of demiplanar chambers that make up the tower. Somewhere within lies Hazlik’s orrery, a multistory magical device that allows the Darklord to see through any Eye of Hazlik in the domain.

Eyes of the Darklord.name: Eyes of the Darklord
Eyes of the Darklord.text:
@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.adventures.Adventure.yxHU0qBTM3nXgczX]{Adventure for Level 10 Characters}

Situation. Subjects of magical experimentation seek to escape Hazlan, but every time they try, they’re spotted through the Eyes of Hazlik, and the Darklord’s apprentices hunt them down.

Hook. Allies hire the characters to help them escape Hazlan by destroying Hazlik’s orrery in Veneficus. They offer to pay the party [[/award 4000gp]] once they’re guided to another domain.

Scene Configuration Overview

  • If you encounter broken links below, they will appear once you install the adventure in your World.

  • The interior map is fully prepared for your adventures in a desert palace, complete with walls and lighting. Global Illumination is enabled by default.

  • In this scene, minimal area lighting is utilized to prevent overwhelming the visual presentation. The pre-configured lighting mainly serves for area effects.

  • The main floor of the palace is situated approximately 20 feet above the ground, as indicated by the stairwell leading to the main entrance. The external walls and windows on the terrace are configured with ground-level visibility in mind.

  • Encounter actors corresponding to each roll result on the Galleries of Suffering table are ready to be placed in the scene from the World's actor directory.

  • NPCs specifically mentioned in the encounters are placed in the scene, but remain hidden until you are ready for the PCs to interact with them. The exception is the Archmage apprentice that shows up when detected by one of the many Eyes of Hazlik. The only exception is the Archmage's apprentice, who appears when one of the many Eyes of Hazlik detects their presence. That actor can be placed on the scene from the Adventure's Actors directory.

  • The Entrance to the Veneficus Teleport region can only be transported to; it will not send you anywhere else on the map.

  • Teleport Scene Regions are configured to send tokens to various areas of the map.

    • Each Teleport Region is initially disabled, allowing you to control where your party is sent.

    • According to the adventure guidelines, the destinations are determined by the number of portals used and a roll of a d8 before the party reaches Hazlik's Orrery, which is an optional destination for every teleport region.

    • Additionally, teleportation to the Main Floor portal and the entrance to Veneficus is also an optional destination.

    • The linear teleportation destinations are as follows, so every area is visited before reaching Hazlik's Orrery if you wish:

      • Main Floor Portal

      • Apprentice Chambers North Portal

      • Apprentice Chambers South Portal

      • Laboratory North Portal

      • Laboratory South Portal

      • Lecture Hall North Portal

      • Lecture Hall South Portal

      • Hazlik's Orrery Portal

      • Main Floor Portal

Encounters

The adventure uses map 2.16 and consists of these encounters:

Greeting at Veneficus. The rooms within Veneficus (see map 2.16) and the walls outside all bear at least one Eye of Hazlik. If the characters are spotted, one of Hazlik’s former apprentices (Medium, Chaotic Evil @UUID[Actor.xqDsVY4Fd4wnyzPx]{Archmage}) appears. This apprentice has been transformed to look and behave like the Darklord. The duplicate belittles the characters and fights to the death.

Galleries of Suffering. As the characters explore Veneficus, roll on the following table to determine what they find within.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwGalleriesofMV rollable resultLabel="Encounter" caption=false]

Flawed Magic. Among mundane decorations, this room contains a random magic item. Roll on the Arcana—Common table in chapter 7 of the Dungeon Master’s Guide. The item functions once, causes a wild magic surge (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwHazlan0000000.JournalEntryPage.AdventuresinHazl#hazlan-wild-magic]{Hazlan Wild Magic}”), and then crumbles to dust.

Red Tower. Shifting portals connect the passages in the Red Tower. Every time the characters pass through a portal in the tower, roll [[/r 1d8]]. If the number rolled is lower than the number of portals passed through, the characters arrive in the orrery chamber. On an 8, the characters appear at the entrance to Veneficus.

Orrery Chamber. This room is dominated by a magical orrery only Hazlik can use. It is defended by a @UUID[Actor.dx7eSFYffWmoI8H0]{Relentless Juggernaut} (see chapter 5). The orrery has AC 19, HP 25, and Immunity to Psychic damage. Every time the orrery takes damage, roll on the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-players-handbook.tables.RollTable.phbWildMagicSurg]{Wild Magic Surge} table in chapter 3 of the Player’s Handbook. Negative effects affect the character who damaged the orrery, while beneficial effects affect the juggernaut.

Hazlik. After the orrery is destroyed, @UUID[Actor.jpa8pnJdVxiZJGfk]{Hazlik} (see earlier in this chapter) appears and offers a magic-using character power and influence as his apprentice. If the character accepts, he commands that character to murder the others. If the character declines, Hazlik mocks the party. The Darklord uses magic to flee if he takes any damage, swearing revenge. Characters who destroy the orrery and escape Veneficus earn a bonus [[/award 3100xp]].

Hazlan.name: Hazlan
Hazlan.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.ZadMN10a5fqoHCSb]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.k2ndz83zDcvUX5GR]

Domain Doomed by Magic

Darklord: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwHazlik0000000]{Hazlik}

Genres: Dark fantasy and disaster horror

Hallmarks: Amoral spellcasters, magical experiments, magic-ravaged environment, wild magic

Mist Talismans: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwEyeofHazlikAm]{Eye of Hazlik amulet}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwGremishkaFoot]{gremishka foot}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwRedRobeScrap0]{scrap from a red robe}

Aligned Tarokka Card: Diviner

Opposed Tarokka Card: Ghost

Hazlan is a domain drained of vitality, frayed by magical disasters, and overrun with abominations. Here the amoral, spellcasting elite have so exploited magic that it’s turned erratic and dangerous to use.

Encouraging these magical abuses is Hazlik, the domain’s zulkir, a title meaning wizard-overlord. The Darklord postures as the domain’s most powerful wizard and views all others as either apprentices or test subjects. All the while, Hazlik hides the fact that he’s lost the ability to learn new magic. Paranoid and controlling, Hazlik constantly spies on those in his domain through the Eyes of Hazlik. These pervasive, eye-shaped symbols appear on structures, decorations, clothing, and individuals across the domain—and through these eyes, the Darklord watches. Hazlik surveils his domain endlessly, reveling in the fear of his people, encouraging rivalries between his apprentices, and searching for magical innovations he can claim as his own.

Domain Features

Hazlan is known for the following features:

Eye of Hazlik. Inhabitants claim the eyelike design called the Eye of Hazlik bears Hazlik’s blessing and wards off dangerous magic. In truth, the symbol is a sensor for Hazlik’s magical surveillance.

Magocracy. The wizard Hazlik rules Hazlan. His apprentices have free rein to exploit the land and its people to further their magical experiments.

Shattered by Magic. The effects of magical disasters disfigure the domain, poisoning rivers with alchemical runoff, scarring the land with magical blasts, and unleashing unpredictable hazards.

Wild Magic. Magic is unreliable in Hazlan, resulting in dangerous side effects (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwHazlan0000000.JournalEntryPage.AdventuresinHazl#hazlan-wild-magic]{Hazlan Wild Magic}”).

Locations in Hazlan

Magical disasters and supernatural environments make Hazlan’s arid landscape even deadlier and its natural rock monuments even stranger. The settlements of Sly-Var and Toyalis are described below, and other notable locations are summarized on the Hazlan Locations table.

Sly-Var

In Sly-Var, mages obsessed with earning the Darklord’s favor raise architecturally discordant towers that defy physical laws. The residents magically create whatever they need, resulting in little need for trade. This leaves the labyrinthine streets and knotted bridges between intertwined compounds eerily empty. The few common folk who traverse these surreal streets often disappear—the victims of magical guardians or of wizards who see the magically deficient as stock for their experiments.

Toyalis

The largest town in Hazlan, Toyalis boasts a population of poor, hardworking commoners united by terror of the domain’s magic-using elite. Colorful depictions of magical creatures, arcane symbols, and the Eye of Hazlik cover the town’s rough wooden and adobe structures. Every few weeks, one of Hazlik’s apprentices leads a procession of attendants and magical wonders to Toyalis in search of replacement servants, new apprentices, or stock for magical experiments. A carnival atmosphere ensues, with the apprentice displaying their magical accomplishments and setting up competitions to assess the locals’ capabilities—magical or otherwise. When a mage’s procession leaves, it takes townsfolk deemed valuable with it.

The paranoid, egomaniacal archmage Hazlik clings to his power despite his magical ambitions being supernaturally smothered. Since Hazlik entered Hazlan, his magic has become unstable. Spells he casts behave erratically and fade swiftly—most lasting no more than an hour—while his magical creations fall apart in about a day. The idea of others learning this terrifies the Darklord, which leads him to claim others’ works as his own, spy on potential rivals, and order reckless research into the nature of magic and his domain.

Hazlik suffers vivid nightmares wherein past rivals taunt and torment him. The Darklord avoids sleeping, employing magical tinctures to stay awake, but this causes him to vacillate between bouts of exhaustion, jitters, and paranoia.

Hazlik knows that his domain is being ripped apart by magical disasters—many of his own creation. He openly denies the realm’s devastation, though, his fear of cascading ruin clashing with his inability to admit he’s wrong.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.1xgQoP79KmBIKrZY cite=false]

Hazlan Locations

Location

Description

Balsam Wood

A plant at the center of the dense Balsam Wood can cure any illness. Unnervingly, the forest is alive, aware, and prone to agony, screaming if someone disturbs so much as a leaf.

The Brew

Alchemical pollution from the village of Ramulai has poisoned the toxic marsh now known as the Brew. Creeks and bogs often erupt with violent fungal creatures and magical eﬀects.

Fallen Sisters

The Fallen Sisters are three mountains that have collapsed into triangular, bottomless pits. Vistani often camp here.

Fleshless Forest

Plants and creatures in the Fleshless Forest were turned to stone. The condition is infectious.

The Lacuna

The Lacuna is a region of knee-high mists that reach toward places of powerful magic, such as Hazlik’s palace, Veneficus. Anyone who steps into these mists vanishes, and few reemerge.

The Marks

The sigils of Hazlik’s mightiest apprentices mark the land in giant, magic-enhancing geoglyphs. The Lacuna grows toward these Marks—and a fourth, mysterious glyph.

Moonstone Valley

Meteors and otherworldly creatures crash into this wasteland during every new moon.

Mound of the Worm

Earth from deep underground rose to form the Mound of the Worm mesa, unleashing dozens of @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmPurpleWorm0000]{Purple Worms}. The aged, albino worm Gravedrinker dominates this region.

Orbitoclasts
