The Orbitoclasts are floating rock formations that resemble lobotomy tools. Their pointed ends align toward the nearest magic-user.

Philosopher’s Egg

A cult of magic-warped beings worships an eight-story, citrine egg. The cult searches for a prophet who’s never been touched by magic to release the antimagic entity within.

The Playas

Former lakes, the Playas are now quicksand-riddled @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmPurpleWorm0000]{Purple Worm} feeding grounds.

Ramulai

Mines near the village of Ramulai yield rare magical elements. Refineries process these volatile resources, but in so doing, they magically sicken the people and the land.

Seething

The lifeless Seething region crumbles away into the Mists. A heavy footfall can break its surface.

Horror Atmosphere.name: Horror Atmosphere
Horror Atmosphere.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.15HMQl43qkNad04k inline classes="float-right size-three"]

Frightful D&D adventures benefit from a game atmosphere that reinforces the horrifying plot. Consider these elements to influence your game’s atmosphere, customizing what you can and not fretting about what you can’t:

Location. Strive to play in an area with minimal distractions that all players are comfortable in. Reduce distracting stimuli by turning off unnecessary screens and removing nongame materials.

Lighting. Dimming the lights can cause distractions to fade and create an eerie ambiance in your game space. You can also adjust the lighting to reflect a setting’s light level, if it’s easy for you to do so. However, always ensure the players have enough light to read by.

Music. You can use music to reinforce an adventure’s mood. Create playlists in advance, choosing options without lyrics and that aren’t recognizably tied to popular media. If you can subtly control volume, slowly raise it when you want attention and lower it when players are talking. Experiment with how volume and tempo influence the mood of a scene.

Props. Props and handouts are useful but shouldn’t become chores or distractions. They might be as simple as a whiteboard for notes or a handwritten letter from a Darklord. Ravenloft has a history of featuring props in its adventures, such as the tarokka deck (see appendix A).

Horror Monsters.name: Horror Monsters
Horror Monsters.text: Cultivating mystery and menace around the monstrous foes in your adventures can be a simple matter. Here are techniques you can use to transform any monster into a horror to haunt characters’ nightmares.

Denizens of the Mists.name: Denizens of the Mists
Denizens of the Mists.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.HUBa7BoNr9BNd5K8 cite=false]

Terrors haunt the Mists. This chapter begins with advice to help you, the DM, use monsters to create frightful encounters. Following this is a collection of horrifying monsters and stalwart monster hunters for your adventures in the Domains of Dread.

Monstrous Origins.name: Monstrous Origins
Monstrous Origins.text:
Unique monsters are singularly terrifying. Consider presenting the monster central to your adventures as a one-of-a-kind terror. Such a creature might be a fluke of nature, the product of insidious magic, a purposefully terrifying creation of the Dark Powers, or a being with an even more unsettling background. Use the tips for creating Darklords and drawing inspiration from different genres of horror in chapter 4 to create unique origins for the most important threats in your adventures.

Different origin stories suggest diverse paths for adventurers to follow when investigating these creatures, learning their weaknesses, and ensuring nothing like them ever menaces again.

Terrifying Language.name: Terrifying Language
Terrifying Language.text:
Describe monsters in vivid ways. A terror chasing characters could just “follow” them, but instead it might “wriggle,” “surge,” or “galumph” after them. Descriptive language underscores how a creature behaves and conjures a visceral description of the monster. Unexpected descriptions can emphasize a creature’s bizarre nature and unsettle the players. For example, it’s one thing to say a snake “slithers,” but another to say a sinister shadow or cruel witch does.

Use unsettling descriptions and similes. For example, a vine blight’s constricting vines might resemble “noose-like cords” or “oily digits.” Try less literal descriptions—even nonsensical ones. For example, a flesh golem might howl like “an earthquake in a mortuary” or “a charnel pit that’s learned to sob.”

Notorious Monsters.name: Notorious Monsters
Notorious Monsters.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.c76lh6yWI1XVS6TY classes="float-right size-three" cite=false]

Treat creatures central to your horror adventures as infamous threats. The more you foreshadow their menace or reveal that they have threatened others before, the larger such creatures loom. Build dread by giving monsters reputations that suggest the monsters’ form, deeds, or peculiarities while letting players’ imaginations embellish details.

For example, tales might describe a skeletal horror that whistles cheerfully while committing brutal acts. Characters might hear rumors of the Whistling Fiend’s melodies becoming haunting earworms for survivors of the fiend’s attacks. A party seeking the monster might hear it whistling in the Mists long before they confront it. All that time, characters can speculate about their enemy’s nature, but ultimately the Whistling Fiend might be any threat you choose.

Mysterious Monsters.name: Mysterious Monsters
Mysterious Monsters.text:
Use conflicting descriptions and strange behaviors to cultivate mystery around your monster. The less characters know about a creature, the more they’ll speculate about terrifying possibilities.

You can develop uncertainty about a creature even after it appears. Keep players guessing about an adventure’s threat by referring to a monster by a flavorful description rather than the name of its stat block. A “zombie” doesn’t sound like much of a threat, but a “stumbling mass of fetid skin and shattered bones” might be something else entirely.

Weird Traits.name: Weird Traits
Weird Traits.text: By adding unexpected traits to monster stat blocks, you can undermine players’ expectations of what a monster is and what it can do. Consider how you can use the Creature Traits list in the “Creating a Creature” section of the Dungeon Master’s Guide to give familiar monsters horrifying twists.

Putting It All Together.name: Putting It All Together
Putting It All Together.text:
Combine the techniques in this section to create a unique terror. If you have ideas about what you want your monster to do, write them down. Then develop stories that connect those pieces or reinforce the type of horror you want to create.

For example, perhaps you’ve got an idea for an arrogant mage who sought to prove he didn’t need his adventuring companions’ protection by constructing a suit of living armor. Considering the bizarre, magical whims of such a character, you choose a Zombie Beholder as the foundation for the mage’s animate armor. To reinforce its nature as a magical creation, you change the monster’s creature type from Undead to Construct and describe it as being made of stitched-together animal parts. Then you give it the Berserk and Magic Resistance traits of a Flesh Golem. The characters’ surprise at unexpectedly facing a beholder might be startling enough, but you add to your story that the mage has become trapped within his living armor as it hunts his former companions. Only by defeating the horror might the characters free what remains of the mage within, represented by the Emissary of Juiblex creature trait from the “Creating a Creature” section of the Dungeon Master’s Guide.

Describing Horror.name: Describing Horror
Describing Horror.text:
Rather than just naming a threat, inspire your players’ imaginations by describing the horror indirectly using the following techniques:

Before It Arrives. Foreshadow the horror’s approach. How do the environment, nearby animals, the weather, or shadows react to it?

Approaches. What sounds does the horror make as it moves? What parts do characters see before the whole comes into view?

Up Close. What does the horror smell like? How does it breathe? Does the air around it grow cold?

Attacks. How does the horror attack? What does damage from it feel like? When it grabs a character, what does it feel like? Does it have physical weapons, or is it a supernatural force that warps other life?

Wounded. When the horror is wounded, how does it react? What does its blood look like? What is inside it?

Horror Pacing.name: Horror Pacing
Horror Pacing.text: Not everything can be scary, and continuous terror is unsustainable. Before players grow numb to endless tension, create opportunities for them to catch their breath and regain a feeling of calm. The best horror adventures are like roller coasters, ratcheting up suspense and releasing it before building toward the next harrowing moment.

Idle Uneasiness.name: Idle Uneasiness
Idle Uneasiness.text: Occasionally slow your adventure’s pace to draw out tension. Linger on describing details. Ask players to describe what their characters are thinking or feeling, or ask what the players think is the worst possible thing that could happen right now. Describe feelings of being watched. Let characters respond to these stray thoughts. Sometimes these impressions should just be their nervousness getting to them. But sometimes it’s the calm before a threat strikes.

Let Players Fill Gaps.name: Let Players Fill Gaps
Let Players Fill Gaps.text: Invite players to turn their imaginations against themselves. Ask players to fill in details with questions like these: What does the way it moves remind you of? It emits the worst smell—what is it? Something on the shelf reminds you of a nightmare—what is it? Players’ responses don’t need to control the narrative; it’s up to you whether these responses are true or just the characters’ perceptions.

Mood Modeling.name: Mood Modeling
Mood Modeling.text: Model the behaviors you want players to emulate by focusing on the game, letting others speak in turn, heading off encroachment on boundaries, and complimenting exciting choices. Stand or pace during intense moments. Speak faster or anxiously when something scary is happening. Raise your voice during pitched battles, and whisper during tense or conspiratorial moments. Allow yourself to sound uncertain, correct yourself, or pause as if you heard something outside. Ask players, “Are you sure?” By engaging with the suspenseful atmosphere you want to create, you bring it into being at the table.

Out of Time.name: Out of Time
Out of Time.text: When the time for action comes, make your narration urgent. Speak quickly and in short bursts. Focus less on senses and more on descriptions—a splash of crimson, who knows where it came from? Politely demand players react quickly: “There’s no time to debate—what do you do?”

Reliability and Trust.name: Reliability and Trust
Reliability and Trust.text: Reliability and trust are just as important to your horror adventures as lurid shocks. Frequent betrayals can make players hesitant, so emphasize that certain people and locations are trustworthy through repeated positive encounters.

End on Cliffhangers.name: End on Cliffhangers
End on Cliffhangers.text: Leave players excited to play again. When ending a session, implement one of the following to conclude on a cliffhanger: Hint at a new threat, impose a complication on existing plans, increase the stakes, introduce a new mystery, have a foe appear, have the world change, or otherwise shock the characters. Then call for a break before players can react.

Horror Roleplaying.name: Horror Roleplaying
Horror Roleplaying.text: Consider the following methods to develop a sense of dread during your horror adventurers.

Innsmouth.name: Innsmouth
Innsmouth.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.P4lFQA4E4KYsLX4i]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.66e4v2wuCaZV0oiq]

Domain of the Imprisoned Apocalyptic

Darklord: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwCthulhu000000]{Cthulhu}

Genre: Cosmic horror

Hallmarks: Cosmic horror creatures, invasive alien dreams, surreal locations, warring cults

Mist Talismans: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwGreatCthulhuE]{Effigy of Great Cthulhu}, starmetal @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwStarmetalRod0]{rod} or @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwStarmetalAmul]{amulet} with eldritch runes, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwsahuagintooth]{sahuagin-tooth fishing lure}

Aligned Tarokka Card: Bishop

Opposed Tarokka Card: Donjon

Hidden amid the Domains of Dread lies a hideous realm defiant of reason and smothered by the Mists. From a violent, murky sea rises a crescent of shattered peaks, mangled spires broken like the jaws of some slain thalassic titan. From noxious fumaroles, miasmic pillars lurch from this accursed isle into a sky inconsistently lit by a distant, drowned light.

Strange ruins dot this island’s rocky coasts, some suggestive of forgotten peoples, others alien in form and purpose. Massive statues of crimson-flecked bloodstone loom over sheer cliffs, each a towering effigy of some profane monstrosity or alien god. One shore bears the wreckage of a few dozen collapsing wooden structures. Little evidence remains of this settlement’s residents, but at its edge lies a toppled sign bearing a word in Common that gives the island its name: Innsmouth.

Despite its forsaken appearance, the isle of Innsmouth isn’t abandoned. Cults vie for control of the island, among them the servants of Great Cthulhu, a harbinger of world-rending aberrance. At the island’s hidden heart, Cthulhu lies dreaming, its alien mind reaching across the Domains of Dread and beyond, drawing to it those who might rouse its full might and unleash it from its prison in the Mists.

Domain Features

Innsmouth is known for the following features:

Cosmic Horror Visions. Cthulhu’s dreams draw aberrant beings and shape Innsmouth as an amalgamation of cosmic horrors, from disparate ruins on the surface to stranger realms in the depths.

Cult Haven. Evil cults devoted to the entities Cthulhu, Dagon, Olhydra, and Zhudun struggle to control the domain. These cults view strangers as useful allies (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwInnsmouth0000.JournalEntryPage.CultsofInnsmouwm]{Cults of Innsmouth}” for details).

Dreams of Cthulhu. Cthulhu seeks freedom. It manipulates the dreams of those on the island, sharing visions of their escape from the domain resulting from the same rituals that would free it.

Locations in Innsmouth

Innsmouth is an inhospitable expanse of alien mountains and ruin-littered coasts. The village of Innsmouth and the mazelike Vaults are described below, and other notable locations are summarized on the Innsmouth Locations table.

Innsmouth

The ruined coastal village of Innsmouth is the most obvious shelter from the storms, alien creatures, and strange events on the island. The Cult of the Chthonic Night knows this and realizes its imprisoned master, Great Cthulhu, calls to strangers to aid the cultists in releasing it. This makes the ruined village a shadowy war zone, where cultists and their servants hunt for fresh acolytes and sacrifices.

The Vaults

Beneath the mountains of Innsmouth winds a labyrinth of improbable passages. Dramatic caverns narrow into the corridors of buried palaces that transform into the wooden halls of forgotten farmhouses. Regularly, these underground paths open into vast cavities, many with their own strange environments, weather, and celestial bodies. What lies among the Vaults? Cultists speak of the realms of alien beings, ghoul empires, countrysides ravaged by sapient energies, the dwellings of reclusive witches, and a thin place in the walls of the prison where Great Cthulhu lies trapped.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.4TFh1QaFVlnLxVbg cite=false]

Innsmouth Locations

Location

Description

Blind Observatory

Within a ring of bismuth monoliths called the Blind Observatory, the clouds vanish, revealing whirling galaxies. The Cult of the Heavenly Doom searches this yawning infinity for meaning.

Cleft of Dreams

Vividly colored gases escape the mouth of the sea cave called the Cleft of Dreams, which extends into surreal realms within the Vaults.

Devil Reef

Sahuagin dwell amid caves of sharp, crimson coral in the Devil Reef. They are violently divided between followers of the shark-god Sekolah and members of the Cult of the Scaly Abyss.

Drowned Colossus

The head of a giant statue depicting a half-melted, eight-eyed humanoid rises just above the waterline. Its mouth—large enough to sail a small ship through—leads to the Vaults.

Eyes of Olhydra

The swampy islands known as the Eyes of Olhydra almost disappear at high tide. At low tide, servants of Olhydra flock to a monument called the Fathomless Gate.

Graven Peak

The domain’s highest mount, Graven Peak, is carved into the shape of a terrifying octopoid fiend. Its brow is etched with a mysterious, branch-like rune. Deep below lies Cthulhu’s Vault.

Map of the Eclipse

Hollows in a series of standing stones depict the progression of a crumbling constellation. Cultists believe that a force capable of moving the stars left this message.

March of Elders

A procession of thirty-foot-tall bloodstone statues of gaunt, distorted figures circles the ruins of a domed structure. Some of the statues are partially buried in the nearby mountain.

Night Light

A tower blazing with a vaporous orange flame resembles a lighthouse, but the island’s cultists say the Night Light is meant to draw something close rather than warn viewers away.

Phase of Uldun-Dar

These physics-defying ruins change every time they’re observed, reflecting wildly different ages and cultures. Sapient, hyperdimensional cats occasionally live here.

The Wharf

A ghoul-haunted hotel called the Wharf leans precariously toward the sea.

Wrecks of the

Shrike

The wreck of the Shrike lies beneath the waves, inexplicably encased in black ice. Nearby, a duplicate of the ship shows evidence of a great fire, and another duplicate lies crushed.

Cthulhu.name: Cthulhu
Cthulhu.text:
Darklord What Eternal Lies

Cthulhu was—and inevitably will be once more—a threat to the entire multiverse. For untold ages, the Dark Powers have bound this harbinger of doom. Unlike the other prisoners of the Domains of Dread, Cthulhu isn’t plagued by ironic torments or thwarted ambitions. Rather, this ageless terror remains physically bound and can exert only a fraction of its terrible might. Nevertheless, Cthulhu’s dreams slip beyond its prison, manipulating minds and orchestrating events to ensure its release and a new epoch of multiversal doom.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwCthulhu000000 statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Cthulhu’s Tale

Spawned beyond the borders of the multiverse in realities where time and life have little worth,

Cthulhu is a servant of elder evils and a harbinger of their inevitable ruin. For untold eons, Cthulhu’s will influenced receptive minds across the multiverse, reaching out in dreams and using them to open gateways to the Far Realm. Through such portals, Cthulhu visited the Material Plane, corrupted and laid waste to civilizations, and erected city-size monuments to its mighty predecessors.

Such distortion and defilement didn’t go unnoticed by the Dark Powers. Multiple times the Mists closed around dread Cthulhu, and multiple times the terror evaded them. It was drawn into the Shadowfell when an extinct cabal of mages summoned it onto a sepulchral world of the Material Plane during a once-in-an-era alignment of eclipses. With the magic of the Shadowfell enhanced, the Mists rose and drew in Cthulhu. Raging and grasping, Cthulhu dragged with it remnants of past conquests and violent minds linked to its own. These disparate nightmares became the foundation of its prison: a surreal island hidden amid the Domains of Dread.

Cthulhu’s Domain

Innsmouth is altered by Cthulhu’s presence, creating the following effects:

Choking Depths. A creature can hold its breath for only 30 seconds while in the domain.

Closing the Borders. While in its domain, Cthulhu can close or open the borders of Innsmouth at will. While the borders are closed, those who enter the Mists surrounding Innsmouth become lost at sea amid inky fog for 3d4 days before arriving once more at Innsmouth.

Something in the Air. Creatures other than Cthulhu and its allies have Disadvantage on saving throws to avoid or end the Frightened condition.

If Cthulhu dies, these effects end immediately.

Cthulhu’s Lair

Amid surreal expanses within Innmouth’s depths lies the entrance to Cthulhu’s Vault, a prison within the Mists where Cthulhu lies trapped. The entrance to Cthulhu’s Vault is a ritual site etched with symbols of power from across time and hundreds of worlds. Those who perform the proper rituals, guided by visions from Cthulhu itself, might free themselves from the domain of Innsmouth, and, in so doing, unleash Cthulhu on the multiverse.

Cthulhu’s Vault Features

Cthulhu’s Vault is a hidden realm, but the entrance to it lies amid the Vaults beneath the island. The vault is depicted on map 2.18 and has the following features:

Noisome Brine. A dark, frothy waterway winds through the Vaults, ultimately emerging from either the Cleft of Dreams or the mouth of the Drowned Colossus.

Ritual Site. A partially toppled monument bearing a tentacled visage marks a weak point in Cthulhu’s prison. Before it stands an altar etched with bizarre runes. These symbols are of otherworldly design, but each cult of Innsmouth knows the meaning of a few. Only cultists imbued with the might of all four cults can perform the ritual these runes describe and force open Cthulhu’s prison.

The Perceptive. A small ship called the Perceptive lies beached here, a remnant of a group of cultists’ past attempt to access Cthulhu’s Vault. The bowsprit is broken, ending in a jagged point.

The Providence. The shattered remains of a ship called the Providence serves as the shelter of a mysterious seer. It’s said this former cultist knows more of Cthulhu’s Vault than any other creature.

Cults of Innsmouth.name: Cults of Innsmouth
Cults of Innsmouth.text:
Four cults operate in Innsmouth, each with its own vile ends. While these groups are ruinously evil, characters might temporarily ally with one or more to oppose another foe or to escape the domain.

Cults and Cultists

The following groups vie for power on and under Innsmouth. Following each cult’s name are the ritual words cult members use in their blasphemous practices, along with other details.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.BV2NkQUeMZIeGtAg inline classes="float-right size-two"]

Cult of the Chthonic Night

Hail, Hail, Cthulhu Awaits

Worships: Cthulhu

Leader: King-Priest Eeyog-Yah

Goal: Free Cthulhu from imprisonment

Lair: The village of Innsmouth and the Vaults

Initiates of the Cult of the Chthonic Night open their minds to the whispers of Great Cthulhu and seek to free the imprisoned terror from the Domains of Dread. Predominantly composed of @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmAberrantCultis]{Aberrant Cultists} and @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmKuotoa00000000]{Kuo-toa} that dwell amid waterlogged ruins deep below the Graven Peak, the cult interprets visions from Cthulhu as omens, calls for sacrifice, and signs of their god’s coming.

King-Priest Eeyog-Yah, an ageless and desiccated @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmKuotoaArchprie]{Kuo-toa Archpriest}, leads the cult. Eeyog-Yah occupies a state of perpetual somnambulism and narrates its visions. The priest’s attendants decipher their leader’s rambling as directives from their god.

Those who join the cult and participate in its induction rituals hear the voice of Cthulhu more clearly than others do (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwInnsmouth0000.JournalEntryPage.CultsofInnsmouwm#cult-dark-gifts]{Cult Dark Gifts}”).

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.nFWo2TKynCPgfyeg inline classes="float-right size-two"]

Cult of the Drowning Wave

Make Clean for the Aeon Wave

Worships: Olhydra

Leader: Draa Shoom

Goal: Sink Innsmouth below the waves

Lair: Eyes of Olhydra

Those who await the Drowning Wave seek to make Innsmouth and all its inhabitants part of Olhydra’s realm on the Elemental Plane of Water. Olhydra, the Prince of Evil Water, then could use the might of Cthulhu and the mass sacrifice of the island to dominate the Elemental Planes and flood the Material Plane.

Draa Shoom, the cult’s @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmAboleth0000000]{Aboleth} leader, believes Olhydra will return the cult to a place of dominance over countless worlds in return for its sacrifice. To effect this, Draa Shoom coaxes @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmElementalCulti]{Elemental Cultists} and shipwrecked individuals to the Eyes of Olhydra. There, amid boggy ruins and the lairs of murky elemental creatures, rises the Fathomless Gate. This ancient stone arch, thought to connect to the Elemental Plane of Water, is covered by bars of starmetal, which prevent passage by solid creatures. The cult seeks the proper rituals to open the gate, allowing Olhydra’s scouring waters to flood the domain.

Those who participate in the cult’s induction rituals are infused with a draught of Olhydra’s sapient form (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwInnsmouth0000.JournalEntryPage.CultsofInnsmouwm#cult-dark-gifts]{Cult Dark Gifts}”).

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.33Gud2irM66yi8jS inline classes="float-right size-two"]

Cult of the Heavenly Doom

Empty Yourself to Gorge on the Stars

Worships: Zhudun

Leader: Celesta Grayl

Goal: Open gateways to join Zhudun

Lair: Blind Observatory and the Map of the Eclipse

Among the broken heights of the domain, the obsessed astronomers of the Cult of the Heavenly Doom seek signs in the heavens and omens from Zhudun, the Corpse Star, an Elder Evil that wanders space and knows the secrets of extinction. These cultists hope to climb into the heavens and join Zhudun.

The cult’s leader, Celesta Grayl, has sought Zhudun for centuries. While documenting her observations, Grayl discovered a path to immortality and became a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmVampireUmbralL]{Vampire Umbral Lord}. @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmDeathCultist00]{Death Cultists}, along with desperate and deluded souls, have flocked to the ancient stargazer, forming the cult around Grayl and her discoveries despite their leader’s protestations and predations. The vampire suffers the cultists only so long as they help her decipher secrets from ruins around the island and retrieve knowledge from the grip of other cults. She believes their discoveries will help her reveal when the stars are right to join Zhudun in the dark places between the stars.

Those who join the cult and participate in its induction rituals release a portion of their life to the stars (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwInnsmouth0000.JournalEntryPage.CultsofInnsmouwm#cult-dark-gifts]{Cult Dark Gifts}”).

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.W5YYvXka1knbDQEW inline classes="float-right size-two"]

Cult of the Scaly Abyss

The Prince Awaits—Become the Way

Worships: Dagon

Leader: Xr’gh

Goal: Summon Dagon

Lair: Dagon’s Claws

The followers of Dagon, Demon Prince of the Darkened Depths, lurk among and beneath the waves of Innsmouth. Ancient even among inhabitants of the Abyss, Dagon seeks to drag demon lords and their Abyssal realms into its demesne of Shadowsea. Once sated on the flesh of its foes, Dagon aspires to unleash its might across the multiverse in a sanity-shattering wave.

Xr’gh, a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmSahuaginBaron0]{Sahuagin Baron} fanatically devoted to Dagon, seeks to turn all who dwell beneath the waves to the demon lord’s worship. Xr’gh seeks enough sacrifices to turn the sea red with blood, an offering great enough to attract Dagon’s notice and draw the demon lord to Innsmouth. The cult leader expects Dagon then to feed on Cthulhu and begin its conquest of the Abyss. Ravenous @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmSahuaginWarrio]{Sahuagin} follow Xr’gh, as do @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmFiendCultist00]{Fiend Cultists} and murderers desirous of the eerie gold treasures the sahuagin offer from their submerged vaults. The Cult of the Scaly Abyss hunts the domain’s ruined villages, abducting anyone who seeks shelter amid the wreckage. Those they find, they drag through secret paths beneath the waves to their ritual site under the island called Dagon’s Claw.

Those who join the cult and participate in its induction rituals develop shark-like organs (see “Cult Dark Gifts”).

Cult Rites

The cults of Innsmouth seek initiates to aid in their unfathomable works. Those who prove themselves reliable devotees gain access to the transformative power of strange gods.

Cult Renown

The cults of Innsmouth reward those who honor their obscene patrons. Use the guidance in the Dungeon Master’s Guide to track characters’ individual standing within Innsmouth’s cults. Characters can gain renown with a cult by performing deeds such as those in the Innsmouth Cults Renown table.

Innsmouth Cults Renown

Deed

Renown Gained

Slaying an enemy cultist*

+1

Despoiling an enemy ritual site*

+1

Participating in a cult ritual*

+1

Ritual sacrifice of an enemy or magic item

+2

Retrieving alien devices

+2

Disrupting an enemy ritual

+3

Slaying the leader of an enemy cult

+5

*Renown for this deed can be gained only once per week.

Cult Dark Gifts

Characters who have a Renown Score of 10+ with an Innsmouth cult are honored as the chosen of the cult’s patron. They are urged to take part in a ritual that inducts them into the cult and bestows on them a supernatural boon. A character who participates in such a ritual gains the Dark Gift (see chapter 1) noted on the Innsmouth Cults Dark Gifts table.

Innsmouth Cults Dark Gifts

Cult

Dark Gift

Cult of Chthonic Night

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.options.Item.rhwGatheredWhisp]{Gathered Whispers}

Cult of the Drowning Wave

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.options.Item.rhwSymbioticBein]{Symbiotic Being}

Cult of the Heavenly Doom

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.options.Item.rhwTouchofDeathA]{Touch of Death}

Cult of the Scaly Abyss

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.options.Item.rhwAberrantAnato]{Aberrant Anatomy}

These Dark Gifts include magical transformations associated with the cult’s patron and its members. This magic can make it easier to navigate parts of the domain and bypass seals made by cult magic. This includes the binding sigils that mark Cthulhu’s Vault. A character with one of these Dark Gifts can’t gain another. A character with one of these gifts who leaves Innsmouth can choose to lose the Dark Gift or keep it.

Adventures in Innsmouth.name: Adventures in Innsmouth
Adventures in Innsmouth.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.zAmAoIGgJUyM72z5 inline]

Adventures in Innsmouth involve cults, degenerate rituals, nightmarish visions, and aberrant transformations. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

Corruptive Horror

The cults of Innsmouth are monstrous and dangerous, but not unwelcoming. Each seeks to bring a different terrifying cataclysm to Innsmouth. As characters interact with these cults and learn more of the island’s secrets, bizarre dreams encourage the characters to accept the Dark Gifts the cults offer and balance rivalries to gain the might of each. Groups that engage with each cult might have strange adventures across Innsmouth and undergo bizarre transformations. Those that do will find themselves with the power to free Cthulhu from the island—and maybe themselves as well.

Dreams of Innsmouth

Cthulhu sends dreams to those in Innsmouth—and in realms beyond—that might help it escape its prison. These dreams include strange images spurring the dreamers to seek the cults of Innsmouth, gain their powers, and then dispel the sigils in Cthulhu’s Vault. Dreams sent by Cthulhu might focus on the following topics and imagery:

Cult of the Chthonic Night: The village of Innsmouth, the Graven Peak, desiccated fish, tentacles

Cult of the Drowning Wave: The Eyes of Olhydra, shipwrecks, tidal waves, three eyes underwater

Cult of the Heavenly Doom: The peaks of Innsmouth, corpses in space, monoliths, whirling stars

Cult of the Scaly Abyss: Dagon’s Claws, deep-sea predators, sharks eating sharks, undersea ruins

Cthulhu’s Vault: The Cleft of Dreams, the Drowned Colossus, dark passages, strange sigils

Any of these dreams might end with a vision of the Mists parting and the characters’ home or a similarly tantalizing location appearing beyond.

Old One’s Bargain

Cthulhu’s dream-messages tempt characters to release the entity to win their own escape from its domain. Whether Cthulhu can or will fulfill this offer is up to you, but the characters might not wish to jeopardize reality to escape. In the course of exploring Innsmouth, they can find the writings of obsessed wizards and cult heresies that suggest the following secrets:

Cthulhu’s Disruption. Cthulhu’s power is such that, should someone release it, the Mists around Innsmouth would open and those in the domain could escape.

Cthulhu’s Might. Cthulhu wouldn’t emerge from its prison at full strength. For approximately one hour after its release, heroes might force Cthulhu back into its prison, should they subject the terror to potent magic such as Imprisonment or Wish, or a blow powerful enough to disrupt its physical form, like a powerful lightning strike, a massive explosion, or a strike from a large sailing ship.

Elaborate on these possibilities as characters explore Innsmouth, and encourage them to believe they might be able to outwit a Great Old One.

Otherworldly Relics

The monsters noted in the “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwGenresofHorro.JournalEntryPage.CosmicHorrorNhHj]{Cosmic Horror}” section of chapter 4 make appropriate inhabitants of the ruins and vaults of Innsmouth. These beings often possess evidence of alien technologies and cultures. Innsmouth’s cults covet such relics. Roll on or choose a result from the Alien Relics table to inspire what otherworldly beings might possess.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwAlienRelicsSQ rollable resultLabel="The Alien Being Possesses …"]

Innsmouth Campaign Arc

Campaigns set in Innsmouth involve warring cults, unfathomable beings, and bizarre transformations. A campaign here might follow this outline:

Levels 1–2. The characters wash up on Innsmouth island and shelter in the village. They experience strange dreams. Soon after, they encounter cultists who seek to initiate them and others who seek to sacrifice them. The characters gain allies among one of the island’s cults.

Levels 3–5. The allied cult sends the characters to disrupt other cults and retrieve relics from the ruins. In return, the cult offers insights into the characters’ dreams and suggests ways to escape the island. After proving their worth, a character receives the cult’s Dark Gift (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwInnsmouth0000.JournalEntryPage.CultsofInnsmouwm#cult-rites]{Cult Rites}”).

Levels 6–16. Led by their dreams and duplicitous cult members, the characters move from cult to cult, gaining influence by delving into ruins and strange vaults. In doing so, they collect Dark Gifts, as well as hints about Cthulhu’s Vault and ways to undermine the Great Old One (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwInnsmouth0000.JournalEntryPage.AdventuresinIn3t#old-ones-bargain]{Old One’s Bargain}”).

Levels 17–20. After collecting a Dark Gift from each cult, the characters follow a treacherous path through the Vaults to Cthulhu’s Vault. There, they can risk releasing Cthulhu to escape the island.

Vault of the Elder.name: Vault of the Elder
Vault of the Elder.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.fG6SFpKb2Vy4lOFR classes="float-right" cite=false]

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.adventures.Adventure.UpRmiXLxrZ0xOzAV]{Adventure for Level 11 Characters}

Situation. Cult lore tells of a vault that is home to a wise elder who knows the secrets of escaping the island.

Hook. A cult leader shares directions to the vault but wants one of the strange relics the elder is said to hoard.

Encounters

The adventure uses map 2.18 and consists of these encounters:

Vault Ambush. On the way to the vault, two @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmCultistFanatic]{Cultist Fanatics} and a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmFiendCultist00]{Fiend Cultist} (or another enemy cultist selected from those mentioned in “Cults of Innsmouth”) ambush the characters.

Frigid Spires. The Vault of the Elder lies in a realm of icy mountains gripped by extreme cold (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-dungeon-masters-guide.content.JournalEntry.dmgDmsToolbox000.JournalEntryPage.xgLA30rErlkjIPAK]{Environmental Effects}” in the Dungeon Master’s Guide). The towers of a gravity-defying stone city protrude from one mountain.

Hollow City. The characters find the city filled with narrow ramps and abandoned, tubelike structures etched with eerie hieroglyphs. Of particular note is an out-of-place collection of tents and a templelike building.

Encampment. @UUID[Compendium.dnd-dungeon-masters-guide.content.JournalEntry.dmgDmsToolbox000.JournalEntryPage.lVkdQivhFHqVmxg7#brown-mold]{Brown mold} covers the tents (see chapter 3 of the Dungeon Master’s Guide). If the characters remove the mold, they find five cultists frozen inside the tents, wrapped in blankets. One wears a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-dungeon-masters-guide.equipment.Item.dmgRingOfMindShi]{Ring of Mind Shielding}.

Elder Temple. The templelike building bears carvings depicting five-winged beings fighting giant, fungus-like insects. Through the temple lies a path leading deeper into the mountain—and defended by a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwShoggoth00000]{Shoggoth} (see chapter 5).

Frozen Warning. Ice encrusts the walls of the deeper temple, covering more strange carvings. Etched into the ice over a depiction of another five-winged creature is a warning in Common: “Beware the Elder.”

Elder’s Reliquary. The passage ends in a chamber with four pillars of ice. Perched on one is a Hostile @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwElderThing000]{Elder Thing} (see chapter 5).

Ice Tombs. The pillars of ice each hold a shadowy shape. These pillars have AC 14, HP 20, Immunity to Poison and Psychic damage, and Vulnerability to Fire damage. The pillars contain the following:

Alien Relic. The first pillar contains a strange relic. Roll on the “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwAlienRelicsSQ]{Alien Relics}” table earlier in this chapter to determine what it is.

Mi-Go. Two pillars contain @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwMiGo000000000]{Mi-Go} (see chapter 5), which awake 1 round after being freed.

Vault Map. A hieroglyph-etched slab in the final pillar charts a route from Innsmouth’s surface to Cthulhu’s Vault. It also provides information from the “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwInnsmouth0000.JournalEntryPage.AdventuresinIn3t#old-ones-bargain]{Old One’s Bargain}” section. However, only someone with the Dark Gift of the Cult of Heavenly Doom can decipher its alien language.

Ramya Vasavadan.name: Ramya Vasavadan
Ramya Vasavadan.text:
Darklord Perpetually Denied Control

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.9W54prB0RgXjJN9q cite=false]

Ramya Vasavadan desperately fights to claim and hold power over Kalakeri in a brutal cycle of victory and loss with her equally uncompromising siblings. Unlike most Darklords, Ramya struggles to control her domain, leading her to seek allies in opposing her siblings and their treacherous allies.

In her true form, Ramya is an undead horror, but illusions mask her monstrous appearance. Her true nature is revealed by her chilled skin and her reflection, which always appears withered and terrifying.

Although Ramya’s siblings, Arijani and Reeva, aren’t Darklords of Kalakeri, they motivate the rebellions in Kalakeri and Ramya’s torments. This monstrous pair is detailed in “Arijani and Reeva.”

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwRamyaVasavada statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Ramya’s Tale

The Vasavadan dynasty has ruled Kalakeri since ancient times. Ramya Vasavadan was handpicked by her father to succeed him, but on the bleak day of his death, Ramya’s brother, Arijani, declared himself the new maharaja.

Ramya refused to yield to her brother, and with her allies, she captured and prepared to execute him. But Ramya’s sister, Reeva, counseled mercy. Instead, Ramya imprisoned Arijani and ascended as Kalakeri’s maharani, Queen of Kings.

In the years that followed, Ramya ruled justly. But in the shadows, Reeva plotted with rebels, who eventually freed Arijani and spurred nobles across the nation to revolt.

Regretting her mercy, Ramya sent her army to crush the rebels and mercilessly execute the treacherous. Reeva and Arijani combated Ramya’s superior forces with ambushes and deceptions. Once again exploiting Ramya’s sympathies, the pair tricked Ramya, invaded the Cerulean Citadel, and executed their sister by strangling. With her final gasps, Ramya cursed her brother and sister, calling them bloodthirsty beasts. Soon after, a terrible monsoon struck Kalakeri, bringing with it the Mists.

The storm lashed Kalakeri for weeks. At its height, Ramya returned from the dead and called on those who had fallen in her service. Loyal in death, a corpse legion joined the revitalized maharani, besieged the Cerulean Citadel, and slaughtered Arijani and Reeva. Ramya then stole their skulls and built them into the foundations of what would become the Tower of Traitors.

Arijani and Reeva’s plots didn’t end with their lives. They too were reborn, transformed into monstrous reflections of their ravenous desires.

Now in the Land of the Mists, the conflict gripping Kalakeri continues, trapping the nation in an endless war and forcing its people to swear loyalty to monsters.

Ramya’s Domain

While Ramya is in control of the Sapphire Throne, Kalakeri is altered by Ramya’s presence, creating the following effects:

Closing the Borders. While in her domain, Ramya can close or open the borders to Kalakeri at will. While the borders are closed, the Mists surrounding Kalakeri sap the life of those within them. A creature that enters the Mists for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there and isn’t a Construct or an Undead must succeed on a [[/save con 20 format=long]] or gain 1 &Reference[Exhaustion] level. A creature makes this save only once per turn.

Everlasting Army. A Medium or Small Humanoid slain in combat rises 24 hours later as a @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.actors24.Actor.mmWight000000000]{Wight} under Ramya’s control, unless the Humanoid is restored to life or its body is destroyed.

If Ramya dies or if her siblings control of the Sapphire Throne, these effects end immediately.

Ramya’s Lair

The Cerulean Citadel is Kalakeri’s royal palace. It derives its name from the blue sandstone used in its construction. Control of the citadel and the Sapphire Throne within regularly passes between Ramya and her siblings.

Cerulean Citadel

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.IsdCcr7FjUXZvDzN classes="float-right"]

Bas-reliefs adorn the palace, depicting the history, heroes, and legends of the kingdom. The site is depicted on @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.scenes.Scene.KNnl6ltJvHinCojq]{map 2.20a} and has the following features:

Domed Court. The central domed court houses the Sapphire Throne. It hosts guardians and treasures gathered by the paranoid Vasavadans. Towers guard the surrounding walls, which are accessible via bridges from the court’s upper floors.

War-Torn Courtyards. Within the palace walls lie marble pavilions arranged among baths, ponds, and gardens. Some are intact, but sieges and repeated conflicts have ravaged others.

Kalakeri.name: Kalakeri
Kalakeri.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.fPDebJeEmwHYSuYg]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.MHGqpInYutMkPROX]

Domain of Betrayal and Revenge

Darklord: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwRamyaVasavada]{Ramya Vasavadan}

Adventure Genres: Gothic horror and dark fantasy

Hallmarks: Monstrous leaders, family intrigue, war-torn nation

Mist Talismans: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwRustedTulwar0]{Rusted tulwar}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwWyvernandLotu]{shield} with a wyvern-and-lotus emblem, a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwPolishedSkull]{polished skull}

Aligned Tarokka Card: Avenger

Opposed Tarokka Card: Artifact

Deceptively beautiful, Kalakeri is a land of verdant rainforests and a web of rivers and lakes known as the Backwaters. At the height of its prosperity, Kalakeri was a robust center of art, commerce, and religion, with merchants spreading wild tales of the land, using fanciful names such as Sri Raji and the Steaming Lands. Now Kalakeri is a conflict-torn shadow of its past glory. The remnants of the Vasavadan dynasty—three heirs transformed into monsters by their depravity and hatred—trap the land in an endless cycle of war.

The people of Kalakeri live in fear of Maharani Ramya Vasavadan, the domain’s Darklord, as well as her treacherous siblings, Arijani and Reeva. The Darklord’s curse manifests both in her deathless desire to rule Kalakeri and in her siblings’ endless rebellions. Both sides escalate their atrocities against one another, drawing the powerful and the innocent alike into their squabbles. As the maharani’s siblings court allies, the Darklord’s forces hunt and execute renegades, adding skulls to the grim Tower of Traitors.

Domain Features

Kalakeri is known for the following features:

Factions in Conflict. Two factions claim Kalakeri’s Sapphire Throne: the soldiers and loyalists of Maharani Ramya Vasavadan, and the nobles and merchants who support the younger royal siblings, Arijani and Reeva Vasavadan.

Jadurai, a City at War. The vast city of Jadurai is Kalakeri’s capital, the site of the royal palace, and a battlefield where factions struggle to claim or hold it as a symbol of rule.

The Backwaters. Rare creatures and extraordinary magical plants flourish in the Backwaters, the collective name for the wilderness away from Kalakeri’s cities. Scattered refugee groups hide in the wilds and organize dangerous, desperate attacks on the Vasavadan factions.

Locations in Kalakeri

Tropical rainforests isolate Kalakeri’s communities. While the domain’s residents are adept at navigating these wildernesses, strangers often fall prey to the domain’s myriad hazards and predators.

The capital of Jadurai is detailed below, and other notable locations are summarized on the Kalakeri Locations table.

Jadurai

Ancient structures adorned with the artistry of past dynasties stand alongside modern buildings in Kalakeri’s capital, Jadurai. The city retains its splendor, but the conflict between the Vasavadan siblings takes an enormous toll as they struggle over the Cerulean Citadel, the nation’s capitol, as well as the Sapphire Throne within. Sections of the city lie in ruins.

On the outskirts of Jadurai rises the Tower of Traitors. Rows of skulls taken from the Darklord’s enemies fill depressions in this stone tower. Two skulls hold places of special prominence: the original human skulls of Arijani and Reeva Vasavadan. The Darklord’s skeletal soldiers build the tower as part of her plan to rid Kalakeri of evil and receive the gods’ blessings.

Ramya Vasavadan desperately fights to claim and hold power over Kalakeri in a brutal cycle of victory and loss with her equally uncompromising siblings. Unlike most Darklords, Ramya struggles to control her domain, leading her to seek allies in opposing her siblings and their treacherous allies.

In her true form, Ramya is an undead horror, but illusions mask her monstrous appearance. Her true

nature is revealed by her chilled skin and her reflection, which always appears withered and terrifying.

Although Ramya’s siblings, Arijani and Reeva, aren’t Darklords of Kalakeri, they motivate the rebellions in Kalakeri and Ramya’s torments. This monstrous pair is detailed in “Arijani and Reeva.”

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.qPH6ilwZAApdghEp cite=false]

Kalakeri Locations

Location

Description

Ashwagangha Mountains

The forbidden ruins of the accursed city of Bahru lie hidden somewhere in the Ashwagangha Mountains. A powerful evil force lies imprisoned there, attended by monstrous cultists.

Bay of Lost Lights

The villages of Kattachira and Grantha flank the Bay of Lost Lights, which is named for the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWillowisp00000]{will-o’-wisps} that bob in and above it.

Drowned Altar

The sea boils around an ancient, submerged ruin called the Drowned Altar. With Kalakeri’s upheaval, no one has performed the rites to placate what dwells in the deep.

Forest of the Still

Statues of helpless animals and terrified refugees alike decorate the quiet, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmBasilisk000000]{basilisk}-infested Forest of the Still. Some of the statues are said to be the remains of heroes from past ages.

Gharial Rivers

The villages of Banyan and Veena are renowned for their remarkable herbalists, but their secrets rely on plants guarded by the titanic crocodiles of the Gharial Rivers.

Island of the Naga

The eternal @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGuardianNaga00]{guardian nagas} of the Isle of the Naga have a perfect memory of Kalakeri’s lost secrets. Suspicious of strangers, the nagas test the worthiness of any who seek their knowledge.

Jalendu’s Sanctuary

The stone giant guru Jalendu lives in his sanctuary and claims to know a path to religious enlightenment. Those who fail to prove their devotion to his teachings turn up petrified.

Meenakara

The shadows of people of Meenakara are disappearing, and the shadow-bereft soon sicken and die. Meenakara’s people believe the cure lies in the Ashram of Niranjan.

Neelakurinji

Bandits overtook the village of Neelakurinji, claiming Arijani Vasavadan as their leader. In truth, they serve no one, opportunistically robbing those who fear disobeying the Vasavadan factions.

Silent Sitars

The giants who occupy the islands known as the Silent Sitars were cursed for oﬀending the gods. Sound is magically suppressed on these isles, stifling the truths within the giants’ music.

The Vochalams

Temples crown the Vochalam mountains. In one, a deity placed a treasure that grants wishes. In the other is a treasure that turns people into wyverns loyal to Kalakeri’s ruler.

Adventures in Kalakeri.name: Adventures in Kalakeri
Adventures in Kalakeri.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.kgKKcSRb2ZWAmLyn inline]

Adventures set in Kalakeri involve suspicion and power struggles. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

Horror in Kalakeri

Adventures in Kalakeri often include the following elements.

Arijani and Reeva

Ramya’s rule is jeopardized by her siblings’ pursuit of power. Arijani and Reeva Vasavadan promise their followers wealth and freedom, but in truth, their agendas serve only them. Ramya still loves her siblings, making her vulnerable to their promises and lies. So long as Ramya exists, the Dark Powers resurrect Arijani and Reeva as if they had Ramya’s Darklord Restoration trait.

Arijani. Arijani is the charming face of resistance for all who oppose Ramya. Now a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmRakshasa000000]{Rakshasa}, gregarious Arijani has decadent tastes. When in control of the Cerulean Citadel, he hosts parties for Kalakeri’s elite that conceal private feasts featuring obscene delicacies and mortal flesh.

Reeva. Reeva’s adoration of Ramya gave way to jealousy when Ramya eclipsed her in their family’s esteem. Now an @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmArcanaloth0000]{Arcanaloth}, Reeve uses insidious magic from beneath the Cerulean Citadel to summon fiends and manipulate foes from afar. Behind her back, many mockingly call Reeva “Inajira,” her charismatic Arijani’s opposite.

Renown in Kalakeri

You can track the characters’ influence with Ramya’s loyalist faction and her siblings’ rebel faction, using the renown rules from the Dungeon Master’s Guide to track the characters’ standing with these groups. Characters might gain renown with either faction by performing missions such as these:

  • Capturing or killing a rival agent

  • Planting evidence that a rival committed a crime

  • Conscripting a powerful ally for the faction

  • Revealing the location of a rival’s hold

  • Revealing traitors within the faction

Anytime the characters complete a mission for either group, their Renown Score with that group increases by 1, but their Renown Score with the other group decreases by 1 (to a minimum of 0). After the first time characters gain renown with either faction, any faction the characters have a Renown Score of 0 with treat the characters as follows:

Loyalist Faction. Members of this faction are Hostile toward the characters. The faction sends troops of @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWight000000000]{Wights} or other Undead to capture the characters weekly.

Rebel Faction. Members of this faction are Hostile toward the characters. The faction sends @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmAssassin000000]{Assassins} or @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWraith00000000]{Wraiths} under Reeva’s command to kill the characters weekly.

Characters can gain renown with a faction they have a Renown Score of 0 with by presenting proof of an action that undermines the other group. Even if betrayed, the factions seek the party’s aid, as they view the characters as useful pawns.

If the characters have a Renown Score of 5+ with either group, their deeds might benefit the faction enough for it to take control of Jadurai and claim the Sapphire Throne. The longer either faction controls the capital, the more extreme its reign becomes, its atrocities escalating week after week. The characters must balance aiding and opposing both factions to maintain equilibrium.

Kalakeri Campaign Arc

Campaigns set in Kalakeri typically involve balancing the horrors committed by the Vasavadan factions. A campaign here might follow this outline:

Levels 1–4. Agents from the rebel faction contact the characters, presenting Arijani and Reeva Vasavadan as misunderstood heroes seeking to overthrow a tyrant. They entreat the characters to save the heir of an allied noble family from a compound controlled by Ramya’s @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWight000000000]{Wight} soldiers and return him to a hidden safe house.

Levels 5–8. The characters meet Arijani and Reeva in their disguises as charming human royals. They thank the characters for their aid and ask them to reclaim the shield of an ancient hero petrified in the Forest of the Still, hoping the shield will be a symbol of their right to rule. As the characters travel to the forest, a messenger delivers a request from Ramya to do the same. The characters must face @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmBasilisk000000]{Basilisks} and the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWraith00000000]{Wraiths} of the petrified dead to recover the shield and then decide who to return it to.

Levels 9–12. Arijani and Reeva plot to attack Jadurai to claim the Sapphire Throne and request the characters’ help. Ramya’s spies have informed her of this plot, and she also seeks the characters’ aid. In the ensuing conflict, the characters might strike at any of the Vasavadan siblings.

Tower of Traitors.name: Tower of Traitors
Tower of Traitors.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.a7a2zm8VL8gbs8iW classes="float-right"]

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.adventures.Adventure.AQUphiT0liOdWl01]{Adventure for Level 11 Characters}

Situation. @UUID[Actor.MmGjKfGTcABsKAFB]{Arijani} and @UUID[Actor.VQXlVHyUHD8hmPhl]{Reeva} Vasavadan seek to recover the skulls of their mortal bodies from the Tower of Traitors. They believe the skulls will grant them greater power.

Hook. Arijani or Reeva order the characters to recover two skulls from the Tower of Traitors but don’t say who the skulls belong to. They offer to pay the party with [[/award 10000gp]] in gemstones and a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-dungeon-masters-guide.equipment.Item.dmgCandleOfInvoc]{Candle of Invocation}.

Encounters

This adventure assumes you are tracking the characters’ renown with Kalakeri’s loyalist and rebel factions (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwKalakeri00000.JournalEntryPage.AdventuresinKaLl#renown-in-kalakeri]{Renown in Kalakeri}” earlier in this section). It includes these encounters.

Stolen Skulls. The skulls Arijani and Reeva seek are from their own corpses, which were defiled by Ramya. The skulls are among hundreds in the @UUID[Scene.cbmCFnbiBRLrqw7n]{Tower of Traitors.} The pair don’t reveal who the skulls belong to but insist they’re vital to the rebel faction’s allies. To aid in the search, the siblings give the characters two @UUID[Item.sLxe4Difq2s0GGOk]{Spell Scrolls of Locate Object.} However, they don’t provide the characters with enough details for the spell to pinpoint the skulls.

Learning About the Skulls. The characters must learn three details about the skulls for them to be familiar enough to the characters that a spell like @UUID[Compendium.dnd-players-handbook.spells.Item.phbsplLocateObje]{Locate Object} can locate them. Characters can learn details in three ways:

Research. Characters who spend a day searching for rumors or investigating records in one of Kalakeri’s settlements learn a detail by succeeding on a [[/check 16 persuasion Investigation format=long]] respectively.

Renown. A character can spend a day questioning non-Hostile members of a faction. After doing so, the characters learn a detail, but their Renown Score with that faction is reduced by 1. The group can do this only once.

Magic. The @UUID[Compendium.dnd-players-handbook.spells.Item.phbsplLegendLore]{Legend Lore} spell reveals one detail; each character can use this spell once.

Each time the party learns a detail about the skulls, reveal a portion of the story from the “Ramya’s Tale” section, obscuring the names of the maharani and the traitors until the end.

Tower of Traitors. The Tower of Traitors is depicted on @UUID[Scene.cbmCFnbiBRLrqw7n]{map 2.20b} and is guarded by @UUID[Actor.yYnbiWjDEUIX7hCz]{patrols} of two @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmRevenant000000]{Revenants} and four @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWight000000000]{Wights}. If the characters have a Renown Score of 3+ with the loyalist faction, the undead are Indifferent and tell the party to move along—they fight only if characters linger for more than 5 minutes or remove a skull from the tower. If the characters have less renown with the loyalists, the undead are Hostile. A new @UUID[Actor.yYnbiWjDEUIX7hCz]{patrol} appears [[/r 2d6#Rounds]] rounds after a patrol is defeated.

Searching the Tower. The tower is composed of thousands of skulls, and the ones the rebels want can be found via magic such as the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-players-handbook.spells.Item.phbsplLocateObje]{Locate Object} spell. Once discovered, a skull can be removed in 1 minute using a sharp implement. When both skulls are freed, the @UUID[Actor.VmAhOOBQeGAte2lQ]{hatred lurking} in the skulls manifests as two Hostile @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwGallowsSpeake]{Gallows Speakers} (see chapter 5).

Loyalty or Treachery. The next time the characters rest, @UUID[Actor.MmGjKfGTcABsKAFB]{Arijani} (@UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmRakshasa000000]{Rakshasa}) appears and demands the skulls. If the characters give him the skulls, he compensates them and the characters’ Renown Score with the rebel faction increases by 4. If the characters refuse to give him the skulls, Arijani fights them. The party’s Renown Score with the rebels decreases by 4 if Arijani is slain. Soon after, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwRamyaVasavada]{Ramya} invites the characters to discuss future plans.

Regardless of how the adventure concludes, characters earn a bonus [[/award 4100xp each]].

Kartakass.name: Kartakass
Kartakass.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.0DtNmbE7Bes9OGC9]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.jblcNJuUcc8bg0vs]

Domain of Tarnished Dreams

Darklord: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwHarkonLukas00]{Harkon Lukas}

Adventure Genres: Dark fantasy and gothic horror

Hallmarks: Hidden identities, dangerous performances, exploitative ambitions, werewolves

Mist Talismans: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwHandbill00000]{Handbill} advertising remarkable performances, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwWoodcutofHunt]{rustic woodcut} of hunting wolves, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwWolfsToothNec]{wolf’s tooth necklace}

Aligned Tarokka Card: Swashbuckler

Opposed Tarokka Card: Beast

Life in Kartakass feels staged and surreal, as every plant, beast, and person strives to prove their greatness. Trees and flowers erupt into bloom; songbirds sing themselves hoarse; and every local knows that dreams, fame, and immortal adulation are theirs should they prove worthy. And where talent and expertise fail, obsession and duplicity reign, leading to cycles of triumph, betrayal, and despair. Predators flourish in this land of unrealistic hopes, consuming passions, and vicious secrets.

With each full moon, the truth of Kartakass is exposed, as the domain’s many werewolves and other lycanthropes indulge their hunger for dominance and blood. After the full moon passes, the idyllic facade returns. Locals don’t speak of why most bar their doors and shutter their windows on the nights of the full moon, but they acknowledge that the path to greatness is messy.

Domain Features

Kartakass is known for the following features:

False Idylls. Kartakass is supernaturally picturesque and outwardly serene. Every season is strikingly beautiful, communities appear idyllic, and many animals are affected as if by the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-players-handbook.spells.Item.phbsplAwaken0000]{Awaken} spell. This makes deceptions and violence all the more stark.

Meistersingers. Kartakass has no unified government. Each settlement is governed by a meistersinger, the acknowledged mayor and embodiment of local art. Meistersingers maintain creative rivalries with one another largely for show.

Theatricality. Settlements pride themselves on performative traditions, such as distinctive dress, traditional dances, or song stylings.

Wolves and Werewolves. Wolves roam the land freely, and @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWerewolf000000]{Werewolves} lurk in the domain’s settlements. Residents seldom venture outside during the full moon, fearing the lycanthropes’ hunger.

Locations in Kartakass

Kartakass is a land of rolling hills, light forests, clear lakes, and pastoral farmlands. Communities are welcoming and host frequent festivals. The settlements of Harmonia and Skald are detailed below, and other notable locations are summarized on the Kartakass Locations table.

Harmonia

Artists vie to perform at the town of Harmonia’s popular Crystal Club, a members-only venue inside a cave that features a stage constructed from a massive geode. The club features dramatic decor and discerning clientele. Rumors say that performers who fail to impress are exiled. The bloodier truth is that the club restricts membership to lycanthropes. Talented artists gain the curse and join their ranks, while the mediocre get devoured.

Skald

The largest community in Kartakass, Skald is a bustling hub of commerce and creativity. The lure of overnight celebrity and fawning crowds attracts the ambitious. Yet behind the facade of creativity and freethinking, business owners and celebrities prey on creativity, while performers struggle viciously over paltry opportunities. Vice, criminality, dark bargains, and supernatural predators flourish amid Skald’s many stages and frequent festivals. Among Skald’s most popular venues is the Old Kartakan Inn and Taverna, owned by the performer and Darklord Harkon Lukas.

The charming werewolf Harkon Lukas manipulates those around him to satisfy his narcissistic needs for adulation and control. Endlessly seeking fame, Harkon travels widely, earning applause and accolades. Yet, whenever he returns to a town he’s performed in, he finds he’s been forgotten. Lukas believes he needs just the right song or inspiration to make himself unforgettable. To that end, he searches for talented protégés, cultivating their skills, then stealing their work. He ensures their loyalty by gifting them copies of his cursed wolf-tooth necklace, Harkon’s Bite, or by transforming them into werewolves. He then manipulates these victims into believing only he will accept them.

Harkon wears a signature wide-brimmed hat and wolf-tooth necklace, and he carries a violin he calls Bleeding Heart. He uses flattery to charm those he takes an interest in. A skilled artist who declines the offer to become his protégé often earns his obsession. The Darklord might go to any extreme to claim such performers’ talents as his own.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.4Jzh4U3Y5FCrlb5B cite=false]

Kartakass Locations

Location

Description

Argent Falls Conservatory

Performers flock to a bardic college atop the Argent Falls. Caverns behind the falls bear rich silver deposits but are haunted by Banshees—the souls of failed performers.

The Catacombs

Violent goblins lurk amid mazelike canyons called the Catacombs. Here, the goblin gladiator Nym Pymplee hosts fighting tournaments, with victors winning treasures from distant domains.

Cavea Plains

The Cavea Plains’ acoustics carry voices over great distances. Any speech above a whisper might attract the attention of wolves.

Cesca’s Vantage

On the night of a full moon, a tarokka-reading wolf appears atop Cesca’s Vantage. She produces crimson cards from any corpse she is offered, and her readings are never wrong.

Cortton

A plague has taken root in Cortton. The town’s residents cake heavy makeup and garish blush on their faces, desperate to convince themselves and others that they’re uninfected.

Emherst

Every “resident” of Emherst is an in-character improvisational actor. They live in the Understage, an underground network of backstages, and they never interrupt performances.

Goblyn Graves

Undead goblinoids often emerge from the Goblyn Graves, the former lair of Radaga, an undead witch said to possess powerful magic treasures.

House of Illusions

This fun house of visual illusions is run by Jack Bequick: a vampire cursed with eternal invisibility and a twisted sense of humor.

Medria

Several dramatic-effects artists call the fishing village of Medria home. Colorful explosions and wandering puppets are commonplace.

Point Hallucination

The Mists often surge over Lake Variatio to deposit newcomers at the rickety quay known as Point Hallucination, where traveling merchants and performers eagerly greet them.

Wildersung Wood

At twilight, a sourceless chorus drifts through these woods. Those who don’t sing along risk losing their lives, while those who discover the last verse discover a hidden sanctuary.

Adventures in Kartakass.name: Adventures in Kartakass
Adventures in Kartakass.text:
Adventures set in Kartakass involve art, werewolves, and sinister ambitions. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

Horror in Kartakass

Kartakan adventures often include the following elements.

Harkon’s Curse

Harkon Lukas can create a copy of his signature necklace once per month on the night of the full moon. This cursed copy is called Harkon’s Bite.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwHarkonsBite2Q]{Harkon’s Bite}

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwHarkonsBite2Q cite=false caption=false]

Harkon’s Manipulations

Harkon Lukas seeks to win the attention and trust of artistic and charismatic individuals. This might include characters—especially bards—or their allies. Harkon might do this to further a grand plot, but he also does so to assuage his perpetually bruised ego and satisfy his need for control. Harkon also seeks supernatural forces he can exploit, including magic items or beings with performative powers—such as an Instrument of the Bards, harpies, or satyrs.

Harkon presents himself as a charming admirer or mentor seeking to aid others. He tries to manipulate those he pursues and earn their trust by encouraging them to do the following:

  • Collaborate in creating a new song or show

  • Compete in performances against the domain’s meistersingers

  • Join him for tutoring or training exercises

  • Protect him from a jealous former student

  • Find a performer who stole one of his songs

  • Travel with him on a tour of other towns

Harkon’s requests eventually turn criminal or violent. He might then blackmail those who aided him or threaten their reputations, or he might isolate them by gifting them Harkon’s Bite. Harkon continues such manipulations until it bores him, at which point he consumes his prey.

The Lukas Pack

An ever-changing entourage of followers and victims surrounds Harkon Lukas, but three individuals are constants in the Darklord’s life:

Akriel Lukas. Harkon’s eldest daughter, Akriel (Medium, Chaotic Evil @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmPerformerLegen]{Performer Legend}), idolizes Harkon and pursues her own performing career. The Dark Powers don’t torment Akriel as they do her father, and her reputation steadily grows—much to her father’s resentment. Akriel is a human and isn’t a lycanthrope.

Casimir Lukas. Harkon’s eldest son, Casimir (Medium, Chaotic Neutral @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmSpyMaster00000]{Spy Master}), is seeking his place in the world. He wants nothing to do with Harkon, preferring to travel, even to other domains. Whether at the whims of his father or the Mists, Casimir always finds his way back to Skald. Casimir is a human and isn’t a lycanthrope.

Haldrake Moonbaun. Harkon’s oldest ally, Haldrake (Medium @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWerewolf000000]{Werewolf}) appears as a heavily tattooed human. He serves as the Old Kartakan Inn and Taverna mixologist and head bouncer. When Harkon isn’t in Skald, he ensures things at the taverna run smoothly. Haldrake aids Harkon in his plots and knows many of the Darklord’s secrets, so Harkon compensates him well.

Traces of Terror

Kartakass’s charm masks its horrors. Roll on or choose a result from the Kartakass Sights table to inspire unsettling scenes in the domain.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwKartakassSiCW rollable resultLabel="Characters Witness …"]

Kartakass Campaign Arc

A campaign in Kartakass might follow this outline:

Levels 1–2. The characters meet Maria, a human woman whose idealistic son, Frantosh, ran away to Harmonia to become a performer. She gives the characters an Uncommon Instrument of the Bards and will allow them to keep it if they return Frantosh home safely. Along the way to Harmonia, the characters are plagued by @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWolf0000000000]{Wolves} and people-eating @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmGoblinWarrior0]{Goblins}.

Levels 3–6. In Harmonia, the characters find Frantosh has begun performing with a skilled bard named Harkon Lukas. Harkon encourages the characters to help Frantosh follow his dreams. To help Frantosh, Harkon asks the characters to fetch voice-enchanting waters from a mystical silver spring near Argent Falls Conservatory. The spring is guarded by dead performers risen as @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmBanshee0000000]{Banshees} and a covetous @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmYoungCopperDra]{Young Copper Dragon}.

Levels 7–8. On returning to Harmonia, the characters find Frantosh went to Skald with Harkon. There, Frantosh is performing nightly with Harkon at the Old Kartakan Inn and Taverna. Frantosh—now wearing a wolf-tooth necklace Harkon gave him—refuses to leave with the characters until after his first solo performance. The “Hungry for More” adventure below details this performance. After this Harkon might resent the characters or start obsessing over one of them.

Hungry for More.name: Hungry for More
Hungry for More.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.Ba8el3ET73q91ARs inline]

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.adventures.Adventure.sj3uoSvPXQHbAvFK]{Adventure for Level 7 Characters}

Situation. The singer Frantosh has fallen under the sway of Harkon Lukas. He refuses to return to his family until after he performs at the Old Kartakan Inn and Taverna on the night of the full moon.

Hook. Frantosh’s mother, Maria, offers the party an Uncommon @UUID[Compendium.dnd-dungeon-masters-guide.equipment.Item.dmgInstrumentOfT]{Instrument of the Bards} if they return her son.

Scene Configuration Overview

  • If you encounter broken links below, they will appear once you install the adventure in your World.

  • The interior map is completely set up for your adventures involving a tavern or inn. Global Illumination is turned off by default.

  • Interior lighting is operational in most public areas of the scene. Lighting in sleeping rooms is placed but not activated.

  • The scene regions designated for transporting tokens to different floors are preconfigured. Specifically, the scene region located on the second floor in the south octagonal room, which leads to Harkon Lukas's room, is disabled by default. This is to prevent the accidental revelation of the trap door in the ceiling.

  • Two rooms have Scene Regions that override the darkness level: Harkon Lukas’s Room on the third floor and the Larder below the kitchen.

  • NPCs mentioned in the adventure are placed in the scene, with Taven patrons (commoners) also present to conceal potential combatants from the players. There is an information box on the scene around the bandits to help locate the group among the patrons (the tables just west of the stage).

  • Frantosh has the Shape-Change feature listed on his character sheet, allowing him to transform into a werewolf either in front of the players or in the background. Ensure that he has at least half of his hit points when using him in combat. The remaining werewolves are placed in the room just east of the stage (all hidden by default).

Encounters

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.yqfICxb5BkadKlzS classes="float-right" cite=false]

This adventure uses map 2.22 and includes these encounters.

Frantosh’s Debut. @UUID[Actor.4vLo1h63RiKlnyIv]{Frantosh} (Medium Performer) is performing on his own for the first time tonight. If the characters seek him out and mention his mother, he agrees to leave with them after his performance—it ends around midnight. If the characters try to abduct Frantosh, three @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmToughBoss00000]{Tough Bosses} (or more) in Harkon Lukas’s service intervene.

Full-Moon. Mention that the performance is on first night of the full moon only if a character asks.

The Old Kartakan. The taverna is a popular spot in Skald. Crowds of @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmCommoner000000]{Commoners} fill the first floor of the Old Kartakan. @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwHarkonLukas00]{Harkon Lukas} (detailed earlier in this section) is here, but he avoids combat unless otherwise mentioned.

The Show. During his 1-hour performance, Frantosh makes a [[/check performance 15 format=long]] every 15 minutes. Characters can aid him with the &Reference[Help] action or in other ways. Track how many of these checks Frantosh succeeds on. During the performance, these events occur:

Heckler. A drunk @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmCommoner000000]{Commoner} heckles Frantosh. If he’s not silenced within 3 rounds, Frantosh has Disadvantage on his next skill check.

Brawl. A fight breaks out between three @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmBandit00000000]{Bandits}. After the first round of combat, a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmBandit00000000]{Bandit} with bad aim targets Frantosh with its Light Crossbow action each round. If Frantosh is struck, he automatically fails his next skill check.

Howl. A wolf howls outside, distracting the audience. The characters have 1 round to direct attention back to Frantosh, perhaps with flashy magic or by making a [[/check performance 18 format=long]]. If they don’t, Frantosh has Disadvantage on his next skill check.

After the Show. After the show, the characters see Frantosh vanish through a secret door. The next events take place in the inn’s hidden areas.

If Frantosh succeeded on three or more checks during the show, Harkon Lukas seeks to keep Frantosh.
