 @Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.gg93xEtMkVEl4Fp8 inline]

Tarokka Deck.name: Tarokka Deck
Tarokka Deck.text:
Tarokka fortune-telling cards originated among Vistani, who created these cards to guide their travels. Since Vistani entered the Mists, their cards have spread and gained renown as tools for divining the future and charting paths between the Domains of Dread. Now, diviners across many lands seek glimpses of what’s to come and answers to unknowable questions from the tarokka. Such diviners have a saying: “No secret is safe from the tarokka.”

The tarokka deck is a prop for creating mood-rich fortune-telling scenes in your adventures. It features prominently in the adventure Curse of Strahd, but you can use it to influence adventures of your own design as well.

Composition.name: Composition
Composition.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.bAeJTD9XvkCfQJRp]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.iP2H5CzNBOtAN1aH]

A tarokka deck has fifty-four cards, each of which has its own name. Forty of them compose the common deck, which is divided into four suits: coins, glyphs, stars, and swords.

Each suit contains cards numbered one through nine, plus a tenth card called the master of that suit. One can refer to a card by its suit designation or by its name. For instance, the six of stars is also known as the evoker, and the master of coins is the rogue.

The remaining fourteen cards make up the high deck and symbolize powerful and enigmatic forces.

All cards in the tarokka deck and what they represent are detailed at the end of this appendix.

Tarokka and the Mists.name: Tarokka and the Mists
Tarokka and the Mists.text: Tarokka fortune-telling cards (detailed in the appendix) have special relationships with domains and their Darklords.

Domains of Ravenloft.name: Domains of Ravenloft
Domains of Ravenloft.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.aFEycp4wJbS62juC cite=false]

At the unfathomable edge of the Shadowfell lie the Domains of Dread, realms bound by Mists where magic and reality twist into nightmare. This chapter for DMs explores the most terrifying of the Domains of Dread. Among other tools, each featured domain includes the following details:

Domain. This initial section introduces the domain, its unique horrors, and notable locations.

Darklord. The domain’s Darklord, their stat block, effects they create within their domain, how they close their domain’s borders (see the introduction), and details of their lair appear here.

Adventures. This section provides guidance on creating adventures in the domain, a campaign outline, and short adventures.

This chapter concludes with an overview of additional domains.

Tarokka Mist Talismans.name: Tarokka Mist Talismans
Tarokka Mist Talismans.text: Each Domain of Dread is aligned with a tarokka card. A character with a tarokka deck can use it to travel between domains. While in the Mists, the deck’s bearer can draw a random card from the deck; that card serves as a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwTheLandoftheM.JournalEntryPage.MistTalismansC63]{Mist talisman} (see the introduction), allowing travel to the domain it is aligned with. A tarokka deck can be used in this way once every 24 hours. A list of domains and their aligned tarokka cards appears at the end of this chapter.

Darklords and the Tarokka.name: Darklords and the Tarokka
Darklords and the Tarokka.text: Every Domain of Dread has a tarokka card that opposes it—one hinting at the truth of the domain’s Darklord. If a domain’s opposed tarokka card is drawn during a tarokka reading in that domain, that domain’s Darklord becomes aware of the reading and its participants. When this occurs, the Darklord can choose to see through the eyes of the tarokka user and speak through them for 1 minute. The Darklord might make their presence known, level a threat, or misinterpret the cards. A tarokka reader has no memory of what a Darklord uses them to say.

Tepest.name: Tepest
Tepest.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.2UqFofACvGbVqH5V]

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.gz063xJaDsQrhvGK]

Domain of Nature’s Cruel Secrets

Darklord: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwMotherLorinda]{Mother Lorinda}

Adventure Genre: Folk horror

Hallmarks: Fey bargains, rural cults, secret sacrifices, sinister festivals

Mist Talismans: @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwBloodstainedF]{Bloodstained farm implement}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwDriedCrownofW]{dried crown of white camellias}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwstrawdoll0000]{straw doll}

Aligned Tarokka Card: Druid

Opposed Tarokka Card: Executioner

All is well in Tepest. Fields shimmer with bountiful harvests, and horned village children recite sing-song rhymes. But those who linger among this land’s pastures and colorful cottages can’t escape the feeling of being watched or the impression that strange forms move amid the fields. Locals dismiss such worries as the work of fey tricksters, but their smiles fail to mask the desperation in their eyes.

Brutality wears a welcoming face in Tepest, a truth embodied by the ancient hag Lorinda, who betrayed her coven in pursuit of adoration. Taking the guise of a deity called Mother, Lorinda adopted the village of Viktal, protecting its people from nature’s whims so they can worship her even as they sacrifice themselves to feed her monstrous offspring. In the wilderness beyond, resentful fey watch and plot, offering cruel bargains to those who stray beyond Mother’s sight. The people of Viktal do what they must to survive, using tradition to cloak their fear and complicity in a cycle of murder. Strangers become symbols of hope for Viktal’s citizens—welcome alternatives to slaughtering their families in Mother’s name.

Domain Features

Tepest is known for the following features:

Children of the Fey. Few children are born in Viktal. Would-be parents must bargain with Mother or the fey to bring them a child.

Fey Threats. Caves dot the land, connecting to depths where dangerous fey dwell. The people of Viktal fear them and blame them for every ill.

The Last Village. Viktal is the only community in Tepest. The others displeased Mother and suffered terrible fates.

Mother. Viktal’s residents devoutly follow the optimistic faith of the nature deity called Mother.

Mother’s Minders. Mother’s most zealous faithful are called Mother’s Minders. These devotees sacrifice their left eyes to Mother to show their faith.

The Tithe. Viktal’s festivals include a fertility celebration called the Tithe that occurs once a season.

Locations in Tepest

The valley of Tepest is rocky, ill-suited to farming, and plagued by dangerous fey. Nevertheless, those who worship Mother are protected from the worst of the land’s dangers. Mother’s Sight and Viktal are detailed below, and other notable locations are summarized on the Sithicus Locations table.

Mother’s Sight

A mile outside Viktal, in a field apart from the surrounding farms, stands Mother’s Sight: an array of rune-etched standing stones. These stones rise as tall as thirty feet, and several have pieces that defy gravity. Within the central and largest stone floats a disk reminiscent of a great eye.

Here the people come to worship, celebrate, and sacrifice. Colorful crafts adorn long tables, where the villagers play games and feast in celebration of Mother’s bounty. Meals here are tasty, and guests are encouraged to eat and drink beyond their fill.

During rituals here, Viktal’s community leaders give thanks to Mother before a dramatic fire surrounded by an eight-foot scaffold. (See “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwTepest0000000.JournalEntryPage.TheTithe4AD1XMex]{The Tithe}” adventure for additional details.)

Viktal

Mother ensures peace in Viktal. The village consists of a few dozen cottages, a market square, and an inn on the banks of Lake Kronov. Wicker scarecrows guard the rich fields blessed by Mother, and friendly people go about their daily lives. Cheery villagers welcome visitors, show them hospitality, and demonstrate interest in their stories.

Mother Lorinda desires a family over all else, not realizing she’s destroyed every family she’s ever had. A creator of monstrosities, this ancient green hag obsesses over unnatural maternalistic pursuits.

Over generations, Tepest’s villagers have come to view Lorinda not as a powerful witch, but as a god of the land and fertility called Mother. Lorinda doesn’t dissuade her fragile mortal followers from their baseless cult practices. The villagers provide willing sacrifices for the hag’s flawed magical attempts to create life, and their adoration gradually soothes the violent, magical outbursts that follow her failures.

Lorinda’s walking tower of bones, the Gurgyl, ranges across Tepest. Those who seek her here find a distracted but deceptively cunning hag clinging to

grotesque dolls of her family. She laments the loss of her sisters, Laveeda and Leticia, never acknowledging that they remain alive as her prisoners. She often speaks of her daughter, Laoirse (pronounced “LUR- shuh”)—the name she uses over and over again for each of her monstrous creations.

When Lorinda visits Viktal, she appears in her illusory guise as Mother, an ancient, powerful woman wearing a mothlike cloak and bearing a branch with burning limbs. She calls on the people to prove their love for her through sacrifice, whether of food and crafts, their left eyes, or their lives. Sometimes her arrival is sudden and terrifying, such as when she magically appears to crawl from one of her followers’ empty eye sockets.

Lorinda seeks new ways to create a permanent family. This might involve great sacrifices, stolen fey magic, strange adoptions, or all manner of supernatural bargains.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.obbLDeJEAypiuI8r cite=false]

Tepest Locations

Location

Description

Arkenheights

Mysterious valleys hide amid the forested Arkenheights mountains. Each is home to territorial fey who know the strange histories and mystical properties that linger in each.

Blackroot

A gigantic evil Treant called Blackroot roots amid the stone circles of an ancient order of druids. Blackroot hoards the druids’ magic and protects its glade with its deadly saplings.

Briggdarrow

This ruined village of Briggdarrow is overgrown by a toxic, vision-inducing fungus. Eerie fungal beings emerge from the ruins by night to spread the infestation beyond the village.

Cas Island

A ruined fortress covers most of Cas Island. Within it lies a mysterious magical relic, but the waters around the island are guarded by a notorious, crocodilian lake monster, the Avanc.

The Cauldron

A fey witch drowned in this pool long ago. Now, any corpse bathed in its magical waters returns to life—but the spirit inhabiting the body never matches the original owner’s.

Goblinwood

People-eating goblins lurk in the Goblinwood. They attack trespassers, but a curse affecting

the goblins prevents them from seeing anyone wearing a cap of a certain color.

Kellee

The walled village of Kellee once worshipped Mother, but something went wrong. Now, Lorinda’s forsaken followers haunt the place, and neither she nor her current worshippers dare approach.

The Lost Court

By day, this idyllic valley is serene and filled with friendly animals. At night, the ground

turns to mud and brambles, from which rise the transformed victims of fey curses.

Mount Arak

Beneath Mount Arak lies a hidden realm of the fey. The goodly Queen Maeve and wicked

Prince Loht jointly rule their people. The fey resent the surface folk for ancient slights.

Nobody’s Inn

Fey creatures avoid this sturdy, welcoming Nobody’s Inn. Few travel so far from Viktal, but those who do are welcomed by Bryonna, the inn’s gregarious but unnerving skeleton proprietor.

Seer’s Glass

The mountain lake called Seer’s Glass has prophetic powers. Those who gaze on their reflection in it see their past by day and their future by night.

Adventures in Tepest.name: Adventures in Tepest
Adventures in Tepest.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.NoLDCbPgZl1J6XcW inline]

Adventures set in Tepest feature rural cults, deceptive fey, and dread of nature’s mysteries. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

Horror in Tepest

Adventures in Tepest often include the following elements.

Strangeness in Viktal

The locals seem welcoming and vigorous in their worship of the nature deity, Mother. The village leader, Wyan, guides them in their faith, assisted by elders called Mother’s Minders—all of whom are missing their left eye.

Residents invite visitors to stay at the village’s only inn, the Fisher’s Rest, free of charge for as long as they please. They encourage new arrivals to linger and participate in their upcoming festival, called the Tithe (detailed later in this section).

Mother’s Children

Multiple times a year, Lorinda goes through a cycle of trying to magically create a new child. She calls this monstrous being Laoirse and dotes on it for a time. She believes that sacrifices to Laoirse during the Tithe can extend her child’s life, but inevitably, the monster’s magic fades, and it becomes mud and bones once more. For weeks afterward, Lorinda remains inconsolable, and violent storms and strange disasters plague Tepest.

Lorinda’s children take varied forms. To create such a monster, choose a stat block appropriate to challenge your characters, then consider how that creature might appear if it were made from different parts. For example, a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmMinotaurOfBaph]{Minotaur of Baphomet} or @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmWerewolf000000]{Werewolf} might be a bipedal mixture of ram, stag, and wolf parts, while a @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmShamblingMound]{Shambling Mound} might be a person made of wicker. These descriptions should seem outlandish but reflective of the creature’s traits and actions. And no matter how bizarre her children are, Lorinda loves them all the same.

Tepest Campaign Arc

Campaigns set in Tepest often involve Viktal’s cult and the dangerous spirits of the wilds. A campaign here might follow this outline:

Levels 1–2. Characters find themselves welcomed in Viktal and encouraged to take part in the Tithe (see the adventure below). At the rite’s conclusion, the villagers try to sacrifice one or more characters to one of Mother’s children. The characters defeat the monster, earning the enmity of both the villagers and Mother Lorinda.

Level 3–5. Fey creatures or druids who have been victims of Lorinda aid the characters; they reveal that she is no god, but a powerful hag who wanders the land in a walking tower called the Gurgyl. They send the group on a quest to learn from a mystical site or elder forest spirit how to infiltrate the hag’s lair.

Levels 6–8. After learning how to infiltrate the Gurgyl, the characters locate and break into Lorinda’s lair, finding some of the hag’s bizarre creations and her diminished, imprisoned sisters. When they emerge from the mobile fortress, they find themselves near Viktal, where Lorinda confronts them, backed by her fanatical followers.

Levels 9+. If the group defeats Lorinda, the land spirals into chaos as fey from Mount Arak run wild. The characters might become involved in a power struggle between the good and evil fey of Arak. Lorinda’s return might drive the fey back—but the Darklord will help only if someone becomes her new child.

The Tithe.name: The Tithe
The Tithe.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.8yTH2xpN3yjeYVNz cite=false classes="float-right"]

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.adventures.Adventure.Sn53K9GRkB6kLwhZ]{Adventure for Level 2 Characters}

Situation. The characters participate in a religious celebration, at the end of which they are poised to become sacrifices.

Hook. The human leader of Viktal, Wyan (Medium, Neutral Evil @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmPriest00000000]{Priest}) invites the characters to participate in the Tithe, a rite honoring their god, Mother.

Encounters

This adventure begins the day of the Tithe, after @UUID[Actor.SukoqQ8TUf4zQSSE]{Friendly villagers} from Viktal (@UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmCommoner000000]{Commoners}) lead the characters to Mother’s Sight. It uses @UUID[Scene.YswRV37h9qrpTBf2]{map 2.33} and consists of these encounters.

Tithe Competitions. @UUID[Actor.9qBDs6Vs1w5nm51N]{Wyan} directs the assemblage in a prayer to Mother, and the day of games begins. Characters each can play three games, participating in each selected game once. During each game, a character makes the ability check or skill check listed on the table below three times. Those who succeed on the check two or more times win the game; if they fail, a villager wins.

Tithe Game

Check

Crooked Joust. Participants use stilts strapped to their arms and legs to topple one another.

[[/check Athletics]]

Powrie Chase. Pursuers play tag in while wearing tattered red cloaks.

[[/check Stealth]]

Lost Siblings. Blindfolded participants identify other players by touching their hair.

[[/check Investigation]]

Gossamer Glutton. Whoever eats the most live moths wins.

[[/check Constitution]]

Never Naughty. Participants flatter elderly villagers, who decide who wins and who gets paddled.

[[/check Persuasion]]

Characters who don’t play a game might socialize and see some benign oddity extrapolated from the Viktal Sights table (earlier in this section).

Competition Winners. The day’s games draw to a close at dusk. Wyan names a character who won two or more games as Mother’s Favorite and crowns them with a @UUID[Item.FKlXSlpcqxFvOFeN]{garland of white camellias.} If no character won this many games, a human villager named @UUID[Actor.N4E65bR60kGbAACI]{Lorelei} (Medium @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmCommoner000000]{Commoner}) wins the crown and invites them to sit with her at the head table during the feast.

Feast. Wyan begins the feast with another prayer as the Mother’s Minders light a grand bonfire within a tall scaffold. During the feast, villagers encourage the characters eat and drink to excess. A character who does so must succeed on a [[/save con 10 format=long]] or gain the &Reference[Poisoned] condition for the next 6 hours.

The Sacrifice. At midnight, standing before the scaffold (see @UUID[Scene.YswRV37h9qrpTBf2]{map 2.33}), Wyan announces, “Now we prove our undying love for Mother and beg her for another season of bounty. Bring forth our sacrifice!” Mother’s Favorite receives a bowl of food and tiny crafts and is instructed to take it to the bonfire.

If Lorelei is Mother’s Favorite, she obeys and approaches the bonfire. If a character is Mother’s Favorite, they choose whether to approach. Regardless, 2 rounds later, a @UUID[Actor.HwCjsdWNkqzKQWbn]{biped with wolflike features} and stag’s horns (use the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-monster-manual.actors.Actor.mmMinotaurOfBaph]{Minotaur of Baphomet} stat block) emerges from the field nearest Mother’s Favorite. This is one of Lorinda’s monstrous children. It fights Mother’s Favorite (and anyone else who damages it) to the death. During combat, the villagers raise their voices in a hymn but stay out of the conflict. Lorelei smiles and doesn’t defend herself if attacked.

Conclusion. If the monster dies, Wyan and the villagers are horrified that the Tithe went wrong. They turn Hostile, blaming the characters and demanding they leave. Mother Lorinda learns of this and seeks revenge.

If the monster wasn’t killed, the villagers remain Friendly, but their true nature becomes clear. Mother Lorinda also takes an interest in the characters, potentially seeking to adopt them as her new children.

Regardless of how the Tithe concludes, the characters gain [[/award 200xp]].

Mother Lorinda.name: Mother Lorinda
Mother Lorinda.text:
Darklord Who Demands Adoration

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.pxs8HjjeoSV5gmlY cite=false]

Mother Lorinda desires a family over all else, not realizing she’s destroyed every family she’s ever had. A creator of monstrosities, this ancient green hag obsesses over unnatural maternalistic pursuits.

Over generations, Tepest’s villagers have come to view Lorinda not as a powerful witch, but as a god of the land and fertility called Mother. Lorinda doesn’t dissuade her fragile mortal followers from their baseless cult practices. The villagers provide willing sacrifices for the hag’s flawed magical attempts to create life, and their adoration gradually soothes the violent, magical outbursts that follow her failures.

Lorinda’s walking tower of bones, the Gurgyl, ranges across Tepest. Those who seek her here find a distracted but deceptively cunning hag clinging to grotesque dolls of her family. She laments the loss of her sisters, Laveeda and Leticia, never acknowledging that they remain alive as her prisoners. She often speaks of her daughter, Laoirse (pronounced “LUR- shuh”)—the name she uses over and over again for each of her monstrous creations.

When Lorinda visits Viktal, she appears in her illusory guise as Mother, an ancient, powerful woman wearing a mothlike cloak and bearing a branch with burning limbs. She calls on the people to prove their love for her through sacrifice, whether of food and crafts, their left eyes, or their lives. Sometimes her arrival is sudden and terrifying, such as when she magically appears to crawl from one of her followers’ empty eye sockets.

Lorinda seeks new ways to create a permanent family. This might involve great sacrifices, stolen fey magic, strange adoptions, or all manner of supernatural bargains.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.actors.Actor.rhwMotherLorinda statblock caption=false classes="double-column hide-in-embed"]

Mother Lorinda’s Tale

The sisters Lorinda, Laveeda, and Leticia Mindefisk lived desperate lives at the wooded edge of a terrible land. Their brutish family claimed the girls were unnatural fey in disguise and forced them to toil constantly. After years of harsh treatment from their cruel father and brothers, the hopeless sisters snapped and killed an abusive traveler during a chance encounter. They butchered the body and served the remains to their unwitting family. Thus began a cycle of murder and cannibalism that persisted for years.

The Mindefisk sisters’ crimes were finally revealed when they all fell in love with a charming stranger. They squabbled, and unwilling to relent or share, they murdered the stranger in full view of their horrified family. With nothing left to lose, the sisters killed the rest of their family as the Mists rose around them.

The horror of the Mindefisk sisters only grew in the Domains of Dread. Finding themselves in Tepest, the sisters discovered their new nature as hags. Over generations, mortals and fey alike came to know them as fickle miracle workers. Despairing families came to them often, willing to trade much for @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.options.Item.rhwHexbloodcXtyA]{hexblood} children born from the hags’ magic (see chapter 1).

Eventually, Lorinda grew fascinated by others’ pursuit of family. She entreated her sisters to help her create a child, but Laveeda and Leticia refused, loathing the idea of any one of them having greater happiness than the others.

Defying her sisters, Lorinda assembled a monstrosity of animal parts and foul magic in secret. With her new daughter’s help, the hag trapped her sisters inside their magic cauldron and stole their powers. Yet Lorinda’s joy with her creation was short—the monster’s magic quickly waned, and it rotted away. Ever since, Lorinda has sought to create a family of her own, producing countless monstrosities in experiments and approaching the villagers of Viktal in her guise as Mother. Over generations, this has given rise to the cult of the Mother in Viktal, but it has brought Lorinda no closer to attaining the lasting family connection she covets.

Mother Lorinda’s Domain

Tepest is altered by Lorinda’s presence, creating the following effects:

Closing the Borders. While in her domain, Mother Lorinda can close or open the borders to Tepest at will. While the borders are closed, a violent storm fills the Mists surrounding Tepest. A creature that enters the Mists for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there must succeed on a DC 15 Strength saving throw or take 2d8 Lightning damage and be pushed up to 30 feet straight toward Tepest. A creature makes this save only once per turn.

Mother’s Watchers. Creatures that have sacrificed an eye to Mother can take a Magic action and pray to her. When they do so, Lorinda can perceive through the creature’s senses as well as her own until the end of the creature’s next turn. When perceiving through a creature’s senses, Lorinda benefits from any special senses it has.

Sun and Storms. While in Tepest, Lorinda can cast Control Weather, requiring no Material components and using the same spellcasting ability as Spellcasting. When casting the spell this way, Lorinda can control the weather within 5 miles of herself, regardless of whether she is inside or outside.

If Lorinda dies, these effects end immediately.

Mother Lorinda’s Lair

Ranging across the domain is Mother Lorinda’s home, the Gurgyl. This towerlike structure of thorns, wicker, and giants’ bones walks on three skeletal dragon legs.

The Gurgyl

The villagers of Viktal know the Gurgyl serves their god in some way, but sighting it portends bad luck. The site has the following features:

Kitchen-Laboratory. The Darklord concocts foul creation magic in this central chamber.

Nursery-Oubliette. This room is home to whichever monster Lorinda calls her daughter, Laoirse.

Old Cauldron. Lorinda’s hag sisters languish in this plugged, oversize pot.

Nightmare Logic.name: Nightmare Logic
Nightmare Logic.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.GdzVIuyUhXT2h3ha inline classes="float-right size-two"]

Ravenloft cultivates dread in defiance of logic. Domains don’t abut one another, historians don’t record coherent pasts, and communities cloak ignorance in strange traditions. The Domains of Dread operate on the same logic as nightmares: Whatever is the most terrifying is true. The Dark Powers, the Mists, and the nature of domains are purposefully mysterious. Mundane details and the lives of domain inhabitants are constructed on illusions and impossibilities. Should characters realize the surreality of the setting, those revelations only create a stronger sense of existential dread and suggest greater questions. Ultimately, none can ever fully understand the Domains of Dread.

The Dark Powers.name: The Dark Powers
The Dark Powers.text:
The Dark Powers are faceless, godlike forces that manipulate reality within the Domains of Dread. Few even suspect their existence. These enigmatic forces help you create horror adventures and serve two complementary roles:

Narrative. The nature and goals of the Dark Powers are mysteries behind the scenes of the setting. Most adventures won’t engage with these forces, but that doesn’t prevent characters and Darklords from seeking to learn about them, particularly as a means to escape the Domains of Dread.

Practical. The Dark Powers provide a narrative veneer for DM intervention. They are why characters appear in Ravenloft, why the Mists can’t be escaped, and why reality-defying strangeness occurs. The desires of the Dark Powers correspond with your needs in creating horror adventures.

The Mists.name: The Mists
The Mists.text:
The Mists of Ravenloft appear without warning and move with eerie intention. Areas within the Mists are Heavily Obscured. No amount of wind can disperse the Mists.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.0ZMRTSIPWXbAnXdT]

The Mists serve the following roles:

Dark Clutches. The Mists serve the Dark Powers. They might steal residents of other worlds or entire locations from elsewhere in the multiverse and trap them in the Domains of Dread. Whether the Mists ever return what they claim depends on the whims of the Dark Powers.

Domain Borders. The Mists surround and isolate every Domain of Dread, preventing easy or predictable travel between them.

Transportation. The Mists can appear anywhere, claim individuals, and spirit them elsewhere in the Domains of Dread.

Mysteries of the Mists

The inhabitants of the Domains of Dread ascribe sinister stories to the Mists but accept the Mists as a natural part of their homeland. Those who travel between domains refer to their world as the Land of the Mists. Any supernatural happening or malicious force might be blamed on the Mists. Those who interact with the Mists—or who come from beyond—are typically met with suspicion.

Mist Talismans.name: Mist Talismans
Mist Talismans.text:
Mist talismans are nonmagical objects that resonate with certain domains. They might be any object, and some might be aligned with multiple domains. By holding or wearing a Mist talisman and focusing on the domain it’s associated with, a creature in the Mists can reach the intended domain after [[/r 1d6#hours]] hours of travel. Creatures following within 30 feet of a Mist talisman’s bearer reach the same destination. Mist talismans associated with domains are detailed in @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwTarokkaandthe]{chapter 2.}

Mist talismans don’t allow their bearers to escape domains with closed borders (see @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwTheLandoftheM.JournalEntryPage.DarklordsoLBMaAM]{“Darklords”}). Similarly, if the borders of the destination domain are closed, roll on the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwTheLandoftheM.JournalEntryPage.WanderingtheMist]{Wandering the Mists} table to determine what happens.

Wandering the Mists.name: Wandering the Mists
Wandering the Mists.text:
So long as a Darklord hasn’t closed their domain’s borders, creatures that enter the Mists might travel to other domains. When characters without a Mist talisman enter the Mists surrounding a domain, they emerge elsewhere after [[/r 1d6#hours]] hours of travel. Roll on or take inspiration from the Wandering the Mists table to determine where the Mists relocate characters.

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.tables.RollTable.rhwWanderingth46 rollable classes="caption-top" resultLabel="Characters Emerge from the Mists …"]{Wandering the Mists}

Domains.name: Domains
Domains.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.KRF8F3q2NentsAg4 inline classes="float-right size-three"]

The Domains of Dread are untold numbers of individual demiplanes hidden in the Shadowfell. Some are created entirely by the Dark Powers, while others the Mists claimed from elsewhere in the multiverse and bear evidence of other worlds. Domains might change as the Dark Powers find new ways to torment their captives, with domains changing shape, merging, fading away, reappearing, and even repeating events.

The Domains of Dread share these features:

Domains Are Finite. Domains vary in size from countries or regions to a single city or structure.

Domains Are Isolated. Each domain is bordered by the Mists. Generally, domains don’t share geography, and the residents and cultures of different domains don’t influence one another.

Domains Hold Darklords. Each domain exists to contains a Darklord. Darklords exert some control over their domains, but they are tormented by their imprisonment. A domain without a Darklord has no purpose; if a domain’s Darklord escapes or is permanently defeated, it might cease to exist.

Chapter 2 describes some of the most notorious Domains of Dread, while chapter 3 explores how to use domains in horror adventures. Use the methods in chapter 4 to create domains of your own.

Darklords.name: Darklords
Darklords.text:
Every domain torments a Darklord, a villain whose depravity attracted the attention of the Dark Powers and led to their imprisonment in the Domains of Dread. A Darklord might be anyone in a domain, from a national leader to an unassuming resident. Darklords share these qualities:

Darklords Are Evil. Each Darklord is the ultimate villain of a domain and the root of its terrors.

Darklords Are Prisoners. Darklords can’t leave their domains. If a Darklord enters the Mists or uses another method to attempt escape, the Dark Powers return them to their domain. Even death rarely allows a Darklord to escape for long.

Darklords Are Tormented. Every domain torments its Darklord in an ironic way tied to their crimes, failures, or ambitions.

Darklords Influence Domains. Darklords have supernatural influence over their domains. A Darklord can close their domain’s borders (see “Closing Domain Borders” below) and create other effects.

Darklords Are Active. Darklords might brood over their failures, but their attempts to achieve their depraved goals fuel their conflicts with characters. Liberally use Darklords in your adventures rather than saving them for climactic plots.

Darklords Don’t Realize They’re Darklords. Darklords generally don’t realize they occupy a special place in the world. Their obsessions make them oblivious to the strangeness of their circumstances. Most don’t suspect the nature of the Domains of Dread or the Dark Powers.

Closing Domain Borders. Darklords can close the borders of their domains at will. When this occurs, the Mists become dangerous (see the Darklord descriptions in chapter 2), and no matter how far a creature travels in the Mists, it eventually finds itself back in the domain it left. This makes leaving a domain impossible until the Darklord opens the borders or is defeated.

Chapter 2 presents stat blocks for numerous Darklords. Chapter 3 explores how to use Darklords in your horror adventures, while chapter 4 aids in creating your own Darklords.

People of the Mists.name: People of the Mists
People of the Mists.text:
The inhabitants of the Domains of Dread fall into these categories:

Soulless Shells. Some domain inhabitants are creations of the Dark Powers. These beings are indistinguishable from normal creatures, living unremarkable lives and existing to reinforce whatever status quo the Dark Powers impose. At the will of the Dark Powers, these inhabitants might reappear in a domain even after being slain.

Bright Souls. Some beings are like those who populate other worlds in the multiverse. Some live unremarkable lives, while others awake to their homes’ wrongness and fight their domains’ evils.

Strangers. Those whom the Mists abduct from other worlds likely suspect the surreal nature of the Domains of Dread. These individuals often seek ways to escape the Mists and return home.

Death and Rebirth

The spirits of those who die in the Domains of Dread or the Mists don’t travel to the afterlife. Those magically returned to life after being dead for at least 24 hours return with the grim realization that they’re trapped within the Mists.

The souls of those who die and aren’t returned to life are trapped within the Domains of Dread. The Dark Powers might eventually reincarnate these souls. Individuals who inherit a soul often look like their predecessor and might recall memories of their past lives.

Magic in the Mists.name: Magic in the Mists
Magic in the Mists.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.eNtaMJucYsf37Tm5 inline classes="float-right size-three"]

Some magic functions differently in the Domains of Dread. Characters who receive spells from otherworldly forces or patrons do so as normal, but the magic noted here functions in distinct ways:

Banishment. Aberrations, Celestials, Elementals, Fey, or Fiends aren’t transported to different planes after the @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.pxpb2eOB6bv4phAf]{Banishment} spell ends. Rather, they appear in a random different domain.

Escape. No spell allows escape from Ravenloft.

Etherealness. Magic that accesses the Border Ethereal, such as the @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells24.Item.phbsplEtherealne]{Etherealness} spell, functions as normal, but a creature that enters the Border Ethereal from a domain is pulled back into the domain it left on leaving the Ethereal Plane.

Extradimensional Spaces. Extradimensional spaces function as normal, but spells cast within them are subject to the same restrictions as magic cast within a domain.

Otherworldly Contact. Magic that allows contact with beings from other planes, such as the @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells24.Item.phbsplCommune000]{Commune} spell, functions normally, but the domain’s Darklord senses when someone in their domain casts such a spell. The Darklord can make themself the spell’s target so that they become the one who is contacted and answer as they please.

Planar Boundaries. For the purposes of spell effects that change across or are blocked by planar boundaries (such as the @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.GtGjNjPBgUHxGYAD]{Sending} spell), each domain is considered its own plane.

Planar Travel. Magic that allows travel to other planes, such as the @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.TIoadMIsUKD4edXi]{Astral Projection} and @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.J6Jpw5XzB5aTeqnz]{Plane Shift} spells, fails in the Domains of Dread. @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.XbwGq5kDJNvAxNXV]{Gate} can conjure beings into the Domains of Dread, but it doesn’t allow them or others to escape. Magic that creates or sends creatures to demiplanes—such as the @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.xNM9CzQQr2CieM4B]{Demiplane}, @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.clwv2PWOcT822hlr]{Maze}, and @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.ZVnL9L8v1KC9TBF4]{Imprisonment} spells—functions as normal.

Summoning. Magic that summons creatures or objects functions as normal, but whatever is summoned is a creation of the Dark Powers and might appear or behave in eerie ways.

Cosmetic Magic Effects

Magic effects produced in Ravenloft appear more sinister than they do elsewhere in the multiverse. For example, a creature conjured by @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.JGT5bNqu9REL7Fuz]{Find Familiar} or @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.5eh2HFbS13078Y3H]{Find Steed} might appear unnatural or deathly, or the @UUID[Compendium.dnd5e.spells.Item.cYI4RNNjI5GAmLhy]{Find the Path} spell might cause a silent, childlike spirit to guide the caster to their destination. Describe such effects in whatever way reinforces a horrific atmosphere, drawing inspiration from the domain where your adventure takes place.

Life in the Mists.name: Life in the Mists
Life in the Mists.text:
@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.Uqz5dHCvbXkmyEHx inline classes="float-right size-two"]

The Domains of Dread might seem surreal to individuals from other worlds, but for the residents of the Mists, Ravenloft’s bizarre qualities are normal parts of reality.

Shared Delusion

Domain residents don’t see incongruities in their existence. Societal pressure and soulless individuals controlled by the Dark Powers encourage inhabitants to embrace ignorance, dismiss coincidences, or doubt their senses. Residents of the Domains of Dread are no less perceptive than people elsewhere in the multiverse, but their home domains manipulate them into believing everything is as it should be, must be, and always was.

Those who call domain residents’ reality into question are likely to be dismissed, distrusted, or worse. Individuals who speak of other worlds are broadly considered to be liars or delusional. Few individuals are open-minded to such concepts.

Adventurers

Ravenloft rarely gives rise to heroes. For the inhabitants of most domains, survival is the only act of courage they pursue. Those willing to confront the terrors of the Mists are typically strangers from other worlds or domain residents jarred from their apathy by traumatic events.

Adventurers find few peers in the Land of the Mists. Guilds of adventurers and shops catering to their needs generally don’t exist. As a result, the challenge of confronting evil rests squarely with a few rare souls. @UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwCampaignsinth]{Chapter 3 }provides details on wanderers who might aid adventurers across domains.

Culture and Technology

Each domain has a distinct culture. Such cultures might be inspired by societies elsewhere in the multiverse or fantasies manufactured to torment a Darklord. Traditions are purportedly based on history, but few domains have coherent records of the past. Details of ages before a Darklord’s arrival are usually vague or absent.

Similarly, every domain has its own level of technological advancement, the details of which are up to you. In some domains, firearms and clockwork devices are common, while others might feature cutting-edge weird science.

No cultural exchange occurs between the Domains of Dread. While evidence of one domain might appear in another, such things are viewed with suspicion.

Currency

Some domains mint their own gold, silver, and copper coins. Though these currencies bear different markings, merchants aren’t particular about the designs stamped on their currency. Platinum and electrum coins rarely circulate through the domains, but they appear often enough—originating from hidden troves or ancient vaults—that no trader questions their value.

Language

Common is the shared language of the Domains of Dread. Some domains might have unique words, and speakers from different domains might have distinctive accents, but individuals from different domains are understandable to one another. Other languages from the Player’s Handbook are also known among the domains, but their rarity varies between domains as you see fit.

Religion

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.biewssuni5S67nVZ classes="float-right size-two" inline]

In many domains, residents worship stern but vague gods, and clergy have scant supernatural powers. Any deity, patron, or philosophy from anywhere in the multiverse might have followers in the Domains of Dread, though these followers’ beliefs might be unconventional. The Dark Powers also breathe power into the faith of delusional zealots. Whether otherworldly forces or the Dark Powers empower faithful spellcasters is up to you.

Ezra. Mysteriously, worship of one god has its origins in the Mists. The god Ezra is depicted as a vaporous figure with dark, billowing hair and is believed to be able to manipulate the Mists. Ezra’s holy symbol is a sprig of belladonna atop a silver kite shield. Ezra’s followers across domains view the deity in contradictory ways. For some, Ezra is a stalwart guardian, while others perceive the god as a soul-stealing embodiment of the Mists. The truths of Ezra’s existence and nature are mysteries.

Time and Dates

The Domains of Dread don’t share a calendar. However, in most domains, locals measure time by “moons” rather than months. Each moon begins on the first night of a full moon and lasts a full lunar cycle. A year consists of twelve moons.

Although domains don’t subscribe to a shared history, the populace of all domains inexplicably accept the current year as 735. Some local histories cleave to anomalous dates and methods of tracking years, but these are considered obsolete. No such irregularities exist in Barovia, though, where year 1 corresponds with the founding of Barovia by the von Zarovich family.

Travel

Most domain residents have little interest in domains beyond their own, and the nature of the Mists makes mercantile ventures and military conquests between domains essentially impossible. Nevertheless, rare individuals travel between the domains either at random, using Mist talismans (see “@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwTheLandoftheM.JournalEntryPage.TheMistsFcILNQ1p]{The Mists}”), or by engaging mist-wandering guides. Those who enter the Mists by road, river, or sea usually emerge in a new domain by the same method. This means that domains with no coastline must be reached by methods other than ship, but also that travelers following roads rarely emerge from the Mists on the sea—though they might arrive inland within a domain surrounded by water.

The Land of the Mists.name: The Land of the Mists
The Land of the Mists.text: This section provides you, the DM, an overview of Ravenloft. For details on specific Domains of Dread, see chapter 2.

Using Darklords.name: Using Darklords
Using Darklords.text: Second only to the characters, Darklords are the most important inhabitants of the Domains of Dread. This section provides details and options for using Darklords in horror campaigns.

Darklords in Your Adventures.name: Darklords in Your Adventures
Darklords in Your Adventures.text:
Most Darklords are important, recurring villains. Take time to understand these characters’ origins and develop details that you can consistently express as characters repeatedly encounter them, their agents, and evidence of their deeds. The Dungeon Master’s Guide provides details on developing villains as well as an NPC Tracker sheet. Use the guidance there and in the following sections to develop Darklords into infamous villains your players will be excited to learn about and oppose.

Roleplaying a Darklord

Most Darklords are irredeemably evil villains, obsessed with their own drives and desires. Many delude themselves into believing they’re heroes or beings beyond morality. When preparing to roleplay a Darklord, review the Darklord’s origin story and consider what traits are core to the Darklord. Aim to roleplay the Darklord in a way that makes sense with the villain’s origins and that makes you excited to play them as a recurring character.

Then consider the following questions:

  • What do you like most about the Darklord and want to highlight?

  • What are the Darklord’s three most positive traits?

  • What are the Darklord’s three most negative traits?

  • Why do others fear the Darklord?

  • What makes the Darklord effective in their role?

  • Does the Darklord think they’re a hero or better than others? Why?

  • Does the Darklord always lie or never lie?

  • How is the Darklord a reflection of one or more of the adventurers?

  • What does the Darklord want more than anything?

  • When would the Darklord be gracious or heroic?

Record your answers to these questions, then create a short list of characteristics you want to convey to the players over encounters with the Darklord.

Darklord Goals

In the adventure Curse of Strahd, the Darklord’s goals are stated as follows:

  • Transform and possess a victim

  • Torment and slay a rival

  • Test or toy with the characters

These goals guide Strahd’s interactions with the characters through an entire campaign, allowing for the villain’s consistent representation.

Recurring Darklords (or other prominent villains) should have similar, simple-to-understand goals. While you might know the finer details of a Darklord’s motivations, players engage with villains only a limited number of times—likely with significant amounts of time between interactions. Choose one to three objectives for a Darklord that you can reinforce during character interactions or that you can hint at with other evidence. This helps the players understand what the Darklord wants and how to act against them.

Use the goals presented above and on the list below to inspire your Darklord’s objectives:

  • Change society

  • Claim a new role

  • Continue a tradition

  • Correct a tragic event

  • Defend the status quo

  • Enforce order

  • Escape their prison

  • Find a missing person

  • Maintain a lie

  • Prevent the inevitable

  • Recover a lost treasure

  • Relieve their boredom

  • Seek a revelation

  • Suppress a secret

Darklord’s Tale

A Darklord’s villainy informs their goals and the torments they suffer. As characters adventure in a Darklord’s domain, they learn about the Darklord, hear reports of the Darklord’s infamy, and eventually discover secrets to frustrate or undermine the villain. Consider these ways of revealing details about a Darklord:

Rumors and Legends. Domain inhabitants typically know of a Darklord or the results of the villain’s evil. They can provide consistent reports of the Darklord’s depredations, what little they know of the villain’s origins, and examples of how their lives are affected by the Darklord’s evil.

Evidence. Show the characters examples of the Darklord’s evil, the suffering of the villain’s victims, the crimes of the Darklord’s servants, and how the domain is scarred by the Darklord’s wickedness.

Lies. The Darklord or their servants provide flattering but false details of the villain’s origins, personality, or objectives. Within such lies are truths commoners don’t know.

Primary Sources. The truth of a Darklord’s past might come from the villain’s family, longtime servants or rivals, or historic records—such as journals, correspondence, or neglected artwork. Magical sources, such as mirrors that reflect the past or methods to enter the Darklord’s memories, might also reveal secrets. Such truths might be hidden, but the Dark Powers ensure they survive to torment the Darklord.

Darklord’s Menace

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.e194iogHGRIoQHxj inline classes="float-right size-three"]

Not every Darklord is a threat in combat to every party, but a Darklord’s menace isn’t rooted in their stat block. Rather, it’s built over adventures where evidence of the Darklord’s wickedness is established and reinforced. Consider the following questions to develop a Darklord’s reputation, adding them as features of adventures:

Demonstrations. When the characters offend the Darklord, how does the villain express displeasure? Do they take it out on the domain’s inhabitants? Do they dub the characters criminals or heretics and execute innocent people who helped the characters?

Law and Superstition. What do the domain’s inhabitants do or refuse to do out of fear of the Darklord or the situation the Darklord has created? Have these habits become local traditions?

Servants. What serves the Darklord? Are they soldiers and servants? Are they monsters or strangely behaving animals? Is it the weather or environment itself? How do such servants make it clear they’re extensions of the Darklord’s will?

Signature Evidence. How does the Darklord prey or experiment on the domain’s inhabitants and what evidence does that leave? If the Darklord executes or sacrifices foes, what is their signature form of slaughter?

Strangers. Do domain inhabitants believe strangers are agents of the Darklord or likely to attract the Darklord’s attention? Do they avoid strangers or spy on them for the Darklord?

Titles. What is the Darklord’s title? Is it a royal or religious title, or is it an epithet the domain’s inhabitants call them?

Meeting a Darklord

Your players’ first encounter with a Darklord should be memorable. Prior to this encounter, the characters might have heard rumors of the Darklord and witnessed their depravity. Reinforce this by planning how the characters will meet the Darklord and what the villain will be doing. Consider whether the Darklord is …

  • Attempting to hire the characters.

  • Commanding the Darklord’s servants.

  • Committing a signature crime.

  • Engaged in an experiment.

  • Having a decadent or horrible meal.

  • Impatient with the characters’ interruption.

  • Pretending to be heroic.

  • Trying to lure a victim.

Pay attention to the Darklord’s first words to the characters. Chapter 2 presents greetings along with many Darklords’ portraits. Take inspiration from these greetings, and have the Darklord’s first words reflect their self-obsessed personalities and how they perceive strangers.

In future meetings, the Darklord should behave consistently. You might develop a signature way of speaking, turns of phrase, or an in-character voice for a Darklord, but keep these simple so the villain doesn’t feel like a caricature or comedic figure.

Darklord Allies

Determine who serves the Darklord. A Darklord’s servants should feel like facets of the Darklord’s nature or goals. Characters should overcome these foes during adventures wherein they learn more about the Darklord. Defeating lesser villains gives characters a sense of progress while maintaining a Darklord’s menace. Make a Darklord’s allies exciting, but don’t lose sight of the Darklord as the most important villain.

When developing allies for a Darklord, consider the following types of allies:

Advisers. Advisers grant the villain insights or control they wouldn’t otherwise possess.

Copies. Some servants are lesser versions of the Darklord. They do the same things as the Darklord to a lesser extent, or they’re a weaker version of the same creature as the Darklord.

Creations. A Darklord might be served by their creations, such as Construct or Undead servants. A relic or ritual might maintain the Darklord’s control of such creatures.

Devotees. Devotees of a Darklord follow them without question. They might be whole organizations of soldiers or worshippers.

Grudging Allies. Some servants obey because they must. They might serve the Darklord to keep loved ones safe or out of a sense of duty.

Incidental Allies. Some allies don’t directly serve the Darklord, but they and the Darklord both gain benefits from their partnership. A monster the Darklord keeps fed with sacrifices would be such an ally.

Majordomo. A majordomo is likely the Darklord’s most powerful servant—the one who knows the most about them and, potentially, the one most likely to betray the Darklord.

Mini Darklords. Some servants control their own portions of a domain and embody the Darklord’s will in that region.

Individual allies can occupy multiple roles.

Defeating a Darklord

Slaying a villain isn’t in itself a heroic goal, but the death of a villain generally means an end to its evil and its ability to affect others. Such isn’t true of Darklords.

Typically, when a Darklord is defeated in combat, their domain doesn’t change, and fear of the Darklord doesn’t fade. Moreover, the Darklord returns after a short time. While a Darklord is sidelined, their domain-affecting powers end, their servants go without orders, and the domains’ borders open. Characters might enter the Mists and find their threats reduced, but this doesn’t mean the Mists allow travel to other lands.

Thwarting a Darklord and escaping their clutches ultimately means delivering the villain a dramatic defeat—one that sends them into such a state of rage, desolation, or uncertainty that their grip on their domain is undermined. Such a defeat might involve a climactic battle following a series of heroic accomplishments. If you plan a climactic confrontation with a Darklord—after which the characters escape the Darklord’s domain—determine how the characters must subvert the villain. These requirements should develop from the Darklord’s origins and as counters to the goals you’ve developed for the villain (see “Darklord Goals” earlier in this chapter). Take inspiration for additional goals for the characters from the suggestions here:

Destruction. Destroy what the Darklord draws their power from, what they’ve been working to create, or their sanctuary.

Escape. Help a victim or captive of the Darklord escape the domain, or ensure they can’t be part of the Darklord’s plans.

Isolation. Disrupt those who empower the Darklord—like their soldiers, cultists, creations, or other allies—forcing the Darklord to spend years to make more.

Relic. Recover a symbol, icon, or weapon that is anathema to the Darklord.

Rivals. Empower foes of the Darklord, resurrect storied heroes, restore bastions of opposition, or reawaken sites of good magic within the domain.

Truth. Reveal the Darklord as a fraud or pretender to their position.

Characters should achieve these goals over the course of multiple adventures, culminating in a climactic encounter where the Darklord discovers the full extent of the characters’ successes.

Unexpected Ends

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.book.JournalEntry.rhwArtHandouts00.JournalEntryPage.My13gWlNGgRJLwzw inline classes="float-right size-three"]

It’s fine if characters defeat a Darklord in combat before you intend. A Darklord loses control only after extraordinary events during an adventure’s climax. Until then, any defeat is an inconvenience or was part of a Darklord’s plans—in any case, it’s an opportunity for adventures. Don’t rob characters of the accomplishment of a victory, but don’t let them think they’ve won for long. Use the suggestions below to reinforce a Darklord’s threat even after an unexpected defeat.

Familiar Death. This isn’t Darklord’s first death. As they die, a Darklord might be amused, blasé, cocky, or fascinated, but not angry or fearful. As it departs, a Darklord might congratulate the characters or narrate their own end. Before dying, the Darklord should make it clear that the characters now have the Darklord’s full attention.

No Remains. Darklords don’t usually leave corpses. When a Darklord is defeated, the Mists claim their body, or the corpse dissolves in an unsettling or thematically appropriate effect.

Deadly in Defeat. With the Darklord’s defeat, what contingencies take effect? Perhaps the Darklord left a letter or a gift to be delivered on the event of their death. Maybe their servants have orders about what to do on the event of the Darklord’s disappearance—like commencing an attack or making a sacrifice. Perhaps threats the Darklord was restraining—such as hidden monsters or volatile servants—run wild, making the domain’s inhabitants desire the Darklord’s return. In any case, use a Darklord’s defeat to demonstrate that the villain is just the face of a much larger, evil system.

The Cat’s Away. Use a Darklord’s defeat to allow characters to have an adventure they couldn’t have had otherwise. Perhaps new allies feel emboldened to share information or the guardians of the Darklord’s lair are distracted. Use the Darklord’s absence as a chance to reveal more about the villain and provide the characters with the tools they need to defeat the Darklord once and for all.

The Darklord’s Shadows. The Darklord might be temporarily gone, but their evil isn’t. During the Darklord’s absence, consider ways the domain might focus more on the characters. Perhaps everyone in the domain whispers about them, wolves are curious about them, zombies flee from them, or the Mists seem to follow them. Cultivate the idea that the Darklord is still watching them or that the characters have violated a taboo.

The Darklord’s Return. Determine how the Darklord returns and what they do once they’re back. Does the Darklord seek revenge against the party? Is the Darklord more curious about—or obsessed with—the characters (see “Darklord Patrons” below)? Does the Darklord invite the characters to their home, devise a trap for them, or seek to involve them in some broader plot? Or was the Darklord’s defeat truly inconsequential, with the characters learning of the villain’s return only well afterward?

Darklord Patrons.name: Darklord Patrons
Darklord Patrons.text:
Most Darklords have lost track of how long they’ve been prisoners of their domains. Countless times they’ve failed to achieve their goals, and countless times the Dark Powers have tempted them with hope and opportunity again. Darklords seek new factors that might finally allow them to achieve their desires. And few factors hold more potential to further Darklords’ plots—or simply distract them for a time—than the characters.

Darklords might become fascinated by characters, viewing them as potential allies, protégés, tools, or challenges to overcome. In such cases, the Darklord might patronize or test the characters, sending them on quests throughout the Darklord’s domain or into the domains of rival Darklords. In return for the characters’ aid, the Darklord might offer the characters escape, secrets, or treasures, or the villain might promise not to harm innocents or the characters’ allies.

Darklords might serve as typical quest givers, but they tend to be more possessive of their allies. At your discretion, a Darklord’s magic might grant all the characters a Dark Gift from those presented in chapter 1. Alternatively, the Darklord might grant characters a sinister supernatural gift.

Darklord Gifts

Darklords might bestow a supernatural gift on characters they’ve allied with (see @UUID[Compendium.dnd-dungeon-masters-guide.content.JournalEntry.dmgDmsToolbox000.JournalEntryPage.U9qU1oUFTPAK7g50]{“Supernatural Gifts”} in the Dungeon Master’s Guide for details). These sinister Blessings might be a symbol of a promise made between the Darklord and the recipient, or they might be a reward for the characters’ service. A character must accept a Darklord’s Blessing to receive it. The Blessing can be removed by the @UUID[Compendium.dnd-players-handbook.spells.Item.phbsplRemoveCurs]{Remove Curse} spell, or the Darklord might revoke it anytime.

The following Blessings are examples of those a Darklord might bestow upon a character. Customize them to create gifts appropriate to specific Darklords.

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwMarkofBeast5Z]{Mark of Beasts}

Supernatural Gift (Blessing)

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwMarkofBeast5Z inline]

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwMarkoftheDrL0]{Mark of the Dread Lord}

Supernatural Gift (Blessing)

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwMarkoftheDrL0 inline]

@UUID[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwMarkofObsesam]{Mark of Obsession}

Supernatural Gift (Blessing)

@Embed[Compendium.dnd-ravenloft-horrors-within.items.Item.rhwMarkofObsesam inline]
